# Thread: CT ( non-dieoff) side effects
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-03-16T17:09:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1485>

Hi all,

I'm tubbing daily for just over a week - up to 40 minutes - and the last few days I'm noticing persistently cold hands and/or feet (sometimes tingly toes, too), even before I've CT'd for that day. Hopefully this is just a transitional thing, but it's a bit of a bother! Input?

### Croak — 2012-03-16T17:13:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1488>

Body's response to lowered core temp is to reduce blood flow to the extremities. Might be overdoing the time or temps and getting your core too cold. Try to keep hands/feet out of the water and see if that helps any too.

### MartiD — 2012-03-16T17:18:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1492>

I have to keep my feet and hands out or I really tremble while in tub. I find it much easier to really relax if they are out. Good luck and keep us posted. FYI...I am doing two a day, the one in the morning is only about 15-20 min and evening is more like 30. I'm not patient enough to do 40!

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-16T19:04:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1592>

> **Croak;1490 wrote:** Body's response to lowered core temp is to reduce blood flow to the extremities. Might be overdoing the time or temps and getting your core too cold. Try to keep hands/feet out of the water and see if that helps any too.

I actually \*do\* keep hands/feet out of the tub. I usually CT in the later morning....and for the past couple of days I've been noticing cold feet (mostly left one, go figure) anytime, even now, pre-tub. Considering putting on a thick pair of socks once I'm in, so they stay dry'ish.

### JC2K — 2012-03-16T19:38:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1615>

I use neoprene booties and gloves from my scuba gear. Makes life substantially easier, especially with the feet. I had real problems with cold without them.

### Lyndra — 2012-03-17T01:12:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1760>

> **JC2K;1617 wrote:** I use neoprene booties and gloves from my scuba gear. Makes life substantially easier, especially with the feet. I had real problems with cold without them.

Same for me. One time I tried wearing neoprene shoes instead of booties. My feet were miserable in the tub and I shook violently for a couple hours after I got out. Now it's neoprene booties plus gloves and a cap.

### JC2K — 2012-03-17T01:28:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1767>

I haven't needed the cap yet (also have a neoprene beanie for scuba  ), but my water's not in the 50 degree range yet. Got to get a bunch of ice for that I think.

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-17T01:59:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-1783>

> **August;1775 wrote:** Stupid question: What's dieing off? I thought "die off effect" usually referred to Candida . . . . I think with CT the hope is that some subcutaneous fat cells will come to a bad end . . .

oops! my mistake. I should have phrased it as "detox". I've had candida issues for YEARS, so that's what my fingers threw in.

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-17T19:55:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2076>

> **meyoolia;1487 wrote:** Hi all,
>
> I'm tubbing daily for just over a week - up to 40 minutes - and the last few days I'm noticing persistently cold hands and/or feet (sometimes tingly toes, too), even before I've CT'd for that day. Hopefully this is just a transitional thing, but it's a bit of a bother! Input?

and to add to this, I've finally figured out that the nasty scabby-itchies on parts of my scalp are likely contact dermatitis from the (neoprene?) of my cpap sleep mask headgear. I've been wearing it for 3 years and \*now\* it decides to give me an allergic reaction?? yeeshk. Changes, they are afoot! guess I'll have to figure out how to stitch up a flannel sleeve thingy for it til this sensitivity passes  .... and I've also upped my b complex and C's... \*crossed fingers\*

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-17T19:57:35+0200

### Birdy — 2012-03-17T22:52:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2125>

Gosh, we need to start a photo album (except Quelson!) this all sounds too funny.

### Melinda — 2012-03-17T23:03:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2131>

> **meyoolia;2082 wrote:** and to add to this, I've finally figured out that the nasty scabby-itchies on parts of my scalp are likely contact dermatitis from the (neoprene?) of my cpap sleep mask headgear. I've been wearing it for 3 years and \*now\* it decides to give me an allergic reaction?? yeeshk. Changes, they are afoot! guess I'll have to figure out how to stitch up a flannel sleeve thingy for it til this sensitivity passes  .... and I've also upped my b complex and C's... \*crossed fingers\*

scabby-itchies might be a yeast infection rather than contact dermatitis. I use tea tree oil shampoo and de-tinctured iodine to fight scabby itchy problems, usually clears up. Hopefully you won't need the headgear much longer!

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-18T15:17:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2360>

> **Melinda;2137 wrote:** scabby-itchies might be a yeast infection rather than contact dermatitis. I use tea tree oil shampoo and de-tinctured iodine to fight scabby itchy problems, usually clears up. Hopefully you won't need the headgear much longer!

that's what I originally thought, I've been a long-time occasional user of Nizoral shampoo, but it's been no help whatsoever. Neither has the oregano-oil spray I've been spritzing on it the past several nights. As it's worsened, I finally clued in that the irritation is following the lines on my scalp where the neoprene straps lie. got some fabric first-aid tape and wrapped it up last night - crossing my fingers again!

Re the cpap, I dearly hope so!! My doctor didn't tell me til recently that YEARS ago I'd had a major hashi's flareup, still had lots of antibodies on the go til last fall. I consulted with an endocrinologist who told me the throat thickening \*might\* go down but if I'd had that many years to build up scar tissue, I shouldn't get my hopes up toooo high. Still giving throat icepacks a good go anyway

### kathylu — 2012-03-18T16:29:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2378>

Question: I am feeling not so good....tons of muscle soreness, mild headache and mild nausea. Should I keep trucking ahead with cold baths? 60 minutes daily at 55 degrees so far. Or should I take a day off so the detox reaction can subside?

Btw, the fat layer on my tummy feels like jelly now, almost like a filled water balloon. A few days ago it was firm, firm, firm! Something is happening. And I'm down 6 lbs in 8 days with NO changes in diet...wahoo!

### SuzieQ — 2012-03-18T19:20:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2421>

I have been CT'ing for over a month now... my sleep is so much better but my skin is like lizard skin on my legs, itchy off and on too. No other changes, no new body composition or weight loss. Just weight gain along with doing LR. Temporary? I wish Dr. K was responding and/or encouraging us through this process. It's making it hard to want to continue.. not giving up as I want optimal health but I feel like the deaf leading the blind here.... no offense to anyone. We are all facing so many different reactions as I am sure is normal because we all have different issues... sorry just having a low moment in life I guess.

I do pool CT up to my waist every night. I don't have scuba booties on, my feet are always cold when I go to bed. Do you think my having my feet in the pool (due to standing upright in pool) that it is harmful?

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-18T22:19:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2459>

> **kathylu;2385 wrote:** Question: I am feeling not so good....tons of muscle soreness, mild headache and mild nausea. Should I keep trucking ahead with cold baths? 60 minutes daily at 55 degrees so far. Or should I take a day off so the detox reaction can subside?
>
>
>
> Btw, the fat layer on my tummy feels like jelly now, almost like a filled water balloon. A few days ago it was firm, firm, firm! Something is happening. And I'm down 6 lbs in 8 days with NO changes in diet...wahoo!

Every time I'm poised to break through another small plateau, I seem to get nauseous. I'd wait and see.... if it lasts more than a day or two, perhaps take a day off then. Mine have happened 3 times now, the first two accompanied by a couple of headachy nights and early mornings, this time just the nausea (which, blessedly, has passed after a nap!).

### Glamorama — 2012-03-19T01:01:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2498>

I was finally able to do an unassisted pull-up! Thats a nice little CT-side effect! I haven;t even trained specifically for it, other than having a go once a week on my pull up bar. Today I just managed to do the full lift, amazing.

To those of you despearing with detox etc... I'm in the same boat, but think of it like this: years of crap, toxins and junk is being flushed out through our melting WAT... this is nothing but a good thing. Doc has told us to up B6 and B12 to assist the liver in clearing the toxins. I think the worst of the detox will last 6-8 weeks... so stick it out, I'm sure it will be worth it.

### Kiera2 — 2012-03-19T02:18:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2521>

I started getting arthritis in my fingers/hands years ago. Swollen, painful, and took a while to warm up in the morning. That went away when I went totally grain free over two years ago. But I had a nodule that had started to grow on the first knuckle of my thumb.

It was a Heberdens nodule, and 4 years ago my GP wanted xrays just to see what was going on. But I don't like routine xrays, so I didn't get them. Now I wish I had, because the nodule is gone. And so is the pain I used to feel if I ever bumped that thumb.

I don't know if it was the CT, or the Leptin Rx, all I know is that I noticed it last week. No pain, no bump. And sadly, no evidence.

### quelsen — 2012-03-19T17:10:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2750>

for as long as i have known me i have had a hump on my atlas. Friday CT saw me in a big bunch of pain and i felt like i had been hit by a truck. Saturday morning i notice the hump was GONE.

GONE... WTF

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-19T19:13:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-non-dieoff-side-effects.206/post-2817>

Strange stuff happening the past couple of days - 8 and 9 of tubbing - gotta shift my CT'ing to evenings somehow (and leave a couple of hours to warm up before bed) cuz now I'm SOOO sleepy an hour or two afterward.....and was nauseous yesterday after tubbing, thankfully passed today BUT my appetite seems to have taken that much-predicted dive. Always reassuring when our systems behave as predicted, lol.

# Thread: Free range eggs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cowboy — 2012-03-16T17:19:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1495>

I buy my eggs from two friends who raise chickens. Both use cracked corn and various other grain products along with letting the chickens run free to eat whatever insects etc they can find. Niether uses commercial "chicken scratch" which reportedly contains ground up animal slaughter waste products.

My question is will these eggs contain omega 3 or omega 6. Do the commercial omega 3 eggs feed something really unique to gain omega 3 increases?

### MonteD — 2012-03-16T17:39:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1512>

They feed them fish oil and flax oil. If you look hard enough you can find vegetarian fed, soy free, omega 3 eggs commercially.

[http://wholehealthsource.blogspot.com/2009/11/omega-3-eggs.html](http://http://wholehealthsource.blogspot.com/2009/11/omega-3-eggs.html) for more info including conversion rates in chickens for DHA. Don't miss the comments.

### Chrisgraff — 2012-03-16T17:51:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1527>

I buy my eggs from the Amish folk at my local farmer's market. The expensive organic eggs at the grocery store (literally) pale in comparison!

### Cowboy — 2012-03-16T18:35:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1567>

No problem finding commercial omega 3 eggs locally. Just wondering if eggs produced by free range chickens have omega 3 as a matter of course without feeding flax and fish oil to them.

I would guess the eggs I get are the equivelent to the Amish eggs. Lots of pale blue colored egg shells.

### donkjellberg — 2012-03-16T18:38:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1570>

I get my eggs from a lady in my area. They almost taste sweet (almost!) . . . so good. No other eggs compare. She spends a great deal on expensive organic feed for them and they are not caged but eat on her land.

### JC2K — 2012-03-16T19:11:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1599>

> **Cowboy;1497 wrote:** My question is will these eggs contain omega 3 or omega 6. Do the commercial omega 3 eggs feed something really unique to gain omega 3 increases?

As long as they're free to run and eat bugs/flowers/etc. you'll be fine. You'll notice variations depending on season. They should watch out for soy in the chicken feed too. If it's just corn, should be ok (although you'll hear some issues with that as well). The critical thing is for them to eat bugs though, IMHO. The colors come from flowers like marigolds.

As mentioned commercial "omega 3" eggs feed flax seed to the chickens to boost O3's in their eggs. There's a couple problems with those eggs. 1) They're usually "vegetarian fed" chickens. Chickens are omnivores, limiting them to just vegetarian feed = not very nutritious eggs. 2) The flax seed's usually not fit for human consumption (possibly rancid, etc). That goes "downstream" to the eggs.

Are Omega 3 eggs bad? Well, in my opinion, in the scheme of things, think of factory chickens way at the bottom, omega 3's around the 30 percent range, and your friend's eggs way up at the 90-95 percent range.

### ASHO — 2012-03-16T20:05:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1630>

donkjellberg, where do you get your superior eggs?

### nonchalant — 2012-03-16T23:14:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1705>

The omega 3's will come from the grass the chickens eat, as well as the grass-eating bugs they eat. My backyard chickens have orange yolks, but they don't eat marigold flowers. I also make my own scratch, to limit corn and eliminate soy.

You might check Craigslist to find someone near you that is selling their backyard eggs. Now that spring is near, production should increase, and it will be easier to find people with excess eggs to sell. I have purchased eggs that way. Sometimes the yolks are light and disappointing, sometimes they are orange and fabulous. Depends mostly on whether the chicken yard is bare dirt, or grassy.

### Cowboy — 2012-03-16T23:38:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1719>

> **Sally;1707 wrote:** I also make my own scratch, to limit corn and eliminate soy.

What do you include in your scratch?

### Birdy — 2012-03-16T23:52:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1727>

I don't bother getting the fancy eggs, because I haven't noticed much of a difference. The eggs here are made mostly with commercial feed, and even a few folks I know do use organic, but it is still very grain-based. I used to get eggs where the hens truly ran around and ate bugs and stuff all day, but they all got eaten by raccoons. So, it's not optimal (for the hens, at least.)

### nonchalant — 2012-03-16T23:57:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1729>

Oh, my, lots of things. I usually go to Sprouts and buy most of the ingredients. Various forms of wheat, oats, barley, rice, lentils, split peas, flax, safflower, sesame seeds, sunflower seeds, peanuts, and I forget what else. I also buy cracked corn, milo and rye from the feed store and mix it in. And sometimes I'll add some yeast. I try to keep the cost per pound to around $1, so I watch for the bulk buy sales at Sprouts, and usually get my rice elsewhere. Sometimes I mix in some cod-liver oil.

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-17T01:19:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1764>

You might try looking for a local co-op. There is one near us where you can buy 15 dozen (!) cage-free (whatever that means) eggs for just over $30. Not sure about O3/O6, etc, but they are brown and have the rich, yellow yolks. For $2/dozen, they will do...

### kathylu — 2012-03-17T03:32:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-1834>

It's fine for chickens to eat seeds and grains, it's part of their natural diet. They are not vegetarians though, they need lots of bugs and I have seen chickens devour dead mice (so I know they are not vegans!). No soy, it's not good for any animal. If they run around outdoors all day aeting grass and bugs, they'll produce eggs with a balanced O3/O6 ratio. That's what you want.

### nonchalant — 2012-03-18T20:35:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-range-eggs.207/post-2440>

Kathylu, you are very correct. During winter, when there were few bugs around, my chickens got extra protein from skimmed raw milk. And they took to eating leftovers from primal kitty's breakfast, usually canned tuna or salmon. Now the bugs are starting to become numerous, and it's fun to watch them scratch, peck, and run after them. I've taken to feeding kitty indoors, because the chickens are a bit aggressive, not waiting for her to finish!

# Thread: Success in Progress
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/success-in-progress.209/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-03-16T17:36:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/success-in-progress.209/post-1509>

I'm like most of you. It has taken me a lot of years to get to where I am now, which is MUCH better than 5 years ago. At the age of 55 I was 322 lbs, could hardly move myself around, had terrible trouble sleeping through the night (needed a nap every day just to make it through the evening), felt lousy, undiagnosed hypothyroid, definitely pre-diabetic and eating a very poor SAD diet. I had tried every diet around. At one point tried Atkins and lost 70# in 6 months, then it just quit working with probably 35# left to go. Of course I always gained everything back plus more. I have no idea what my hormones were doing. Didn't like doctors, didn't trust them at all. Didn't want to take drugs, so just avoided the whole thing and kept getting heavier.

My entire life has been a weight struggle. I've always been hot, even as a baby (according to my mother). As a little girl I couldn't run all day and keep up with the neighbor kids. I'd wear out quickly and couldn't recover fast. Even a couple of time I was successful at taking the weight off (actually SKINNY once!) when I tried jogging and lifting, just couldn't do it!

In spring 2007 I discovered hcg. Jumped in with injections and lost 125# in 11 months. Actually lost a lot more, but kept gaining back between rounds. At that point I began gaining more than I lost which of course put me way back up again.

Last year I discovered leptin through Byron Richards book (just a chance recommendation from a good friend) and discovered I could continue to lose weight during p3! I also discovered that I had a very hard time maintaining, once I increased my calories even a very little bit while doing "my" leptin routine. It wasn't until I found Dr. Jack that I realized it had to do with hormone issues.

So, here I am, finally dealing with the underlying cause of all my problems. Feeling better, sleeping fantastic! FBG in the double digits now, hoping everything else is improving as well. I am now coming off hcg, hopefully for the last time. Doing CT and will begin LRx tomorrow. I have about 106# to lose right now to get to my initial goal. From there it just depends on how I look, feel, and hormones I guess.

All in all, it has been a wild journey. I trust only 1 doctor (well, 2 if I include Dr. Jack  ) and she treats as naturally and as minimally as possible to let the body heal itself. She understood all the lab tests I requested and explained them all to me when the results came back. Don't know that I will ever reach optimal, but I will at the very least feel good for the years I have left on this earth.

My sister just passed away 2 months ago at the age of 64 from heart disease. She was over 400#. I refuse to follow that path!!!!!

ONWARD HO!!! The journey progresses. I consider myself immensely successful if for no other reason than I refuse to give up. Even more so because I can see and feel the improvements in my health happening. I am a happy camper!!!

### Kathy — 2012-03-16T19:29:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/success-in-progress.209/post-1609>

> **grammasmitty;1511 wrote:** ... but I will at the very least feel good for the years I have left on this earth.
>
>
>
> My sister just passed away 2 months ago at the age of 64 from heart disease. She was over 400#. I refuse to follow that path!!!!!
>
>
>
> ONWARD HO!!! The journey progresses. I consider myself immensely successful if for no other reason than I refuse to give up. Even more so because I can see and feel the improvements in my health happening. I am a happy camper!!!

Hi Grammasmitty, Your story touched me & I'm looking forward to watching your progress. Congratulations!

Kathy

Rock it out, Gramma! You rule!

# Thread: What brand of Bitter Melon Extract are you all taking?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ASHO — 2012-03-16T18:18:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-1550>

There are so many brands out there to choose from. I am a real stickler for quality.

### Rojo — 2012-03-16T22:12:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-1679>

Good question. I'm taking two versions of the extract:

\* Himalaya Herbal Healthcare, Bitter Melon. Label claims: (per 1 caplet)

\* organic bitter melon fruit standardized extract (5% Bitters containing Momordicosides & Charantin - 12.5 mg) 250 mg

\* organic bitter melon stem powder (1% Bitters - 4.10 mg) 410 mg

\* Swanson Superior Herbs Momordica Bitter Melon . Label claims: (per capsule)

\* Momordica fruit 300mg

\* Momordicin (2.5% bitter principles) 200mg

I will say they seem to help make the CT less excruciating.

### Nan — 2012-03-16T22:14:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-1681>

I use bitter melon from Swanson, and get most of my vitamins and minerals there; quality is good, and price is very good.

### nonchalant — 2012-03-16T22:52:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-1695>

I bought one of the cucumber-sized melons from my local international grocery store. Cut off about 2-3 inches, removed the seeds, chopped fine, and fried it in coconut oil and then added eggs, mushrooms, onions, etc. Delicious.

### ASHO — 2012-03-17T00:25:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-1740>

[http://www.swansonvitamins.com/SW1161/ItemDetail?cm\_vc=SWH053](http://http://www.swansonvitamins.com/SW1161/ItemDetail?cm_vc=SWH053)

### Rojo — 2012-03-18T02:24:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-2193>

I usually pick a standardized form if one is available. I'm not sure about Swanson, but I've heard many other brands will use inferior product in their non-standardized versions.

### MonteD — 2012-03-18T02:30:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-2197>

### Phoenix — 2012-03-18T04:29:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-2281>

> **Sally;1697 wrote:** I bought one of the cucumber-sized melons from my local international grocery store. Cut off about 2-3 inches, removed the seeds, chopped fine, and fried it in coconut oil and then added eggs, mushrooms, onions, etc. Delicious.

My husband said he saw bitter melon at a store near me, so I'm going on a bitter melon shopping excursion tomorrow. Glad to hear it was delicious!

### David — 2012-03-18T14:28:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-2352>

I have been getting two different varieties of bitter melon from our local Asian market (excellent prices and quality on nearly everything).

I cut the melons lengthwise, remove the pith and seeds with the tip of a tablespoon and then cut them into quarter-inch, half-moon shaped pieces. Fry in coconut oil until tender (about five minutes) and then store it in the cooler until breakfast. I usually add to eggs, but it is also good heated with any left-over meat. It has an unusual, citrus-like flavor that removes some boredom from the daily eggs.

### ASHO — 2012-03-18T22:07:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-2454>

OK, Bitter Melon makes CT less excruciating. What other effects does every one experience?

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-19T05:58:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-2627>

You can find bitter melon in the Asian markets.

### ASHO — 2012-03-19T18:28:32+0200

### donkjellberg — 2012-03-20T10:03:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-3173>

Nonetheless, I plan to find the best local source, and start incorporating it in my diet. Some Asian friends bring it to work and it tastes great in a spicy mix. I just learned that the Okinowans eat it

### Ane — 2012-03-20T21:39:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-3459>

I just picked up a bitter melon supplement today. Is there a particular time to take it that will help with the cold exposure?

### JC2K — 2012-03-20T22:02:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-3481>

> **Ane;3484 wrote:** I just picked up a bitter melon supplement today. Is there a particular time to take it that will help with the cold exposure?

I don't think there's any research studies in humans with a dosage scheme. In the rat tests, it looked like they fed it at every meal. So it's not so much a "take it X minutes before CT" but more a "take consistently over time" thing.

### Ane — 2012-03-20T22:11:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-3485>

Thanks! I will get started today.

### Garfield — 2012-03-20T22:49:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-3512>

> **Ane;3484 wrote:** I just picked up a bitter melon supplement today. Is there a particular time to take it that will help with the cold exposure?

Dr. K said to take it ~2 hours prior to CT.

### ruby — 2012-03-21T01:27:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-3619>

Hi ASHO,

I find it very potent - to the point of giving myself hypoglycemia once, after downing 4-5 cups in quick succession. I like this tea so I have to really watch it to avoid overdosing.

### ASHO — 2012-03-21T23:01:59+0200

### ruby — 2012-03-22T02:51:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-4181>

### Cody — 2012-03-22T18:59:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-4511>

> **Garfield;3537 wrote:** Dr. K said to take it ~2 hours prior to CT.

I remember reading this as well.

Do people generally just have reading comprehension issues? Or are a majority of people here not actually reading what Dr. K writes?

### TerrierMom — 2012-03-22T19:52:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-4558>

@Cody I think it's just overload. I have been buried in Dr K's blog for weeks now, and copy and paste little nuggets out into another document to save for reference and pull ideas together for myself.

My problem (and I bet I'm not the only one) is I read a bunch of stuff, then the light only goes on LATER, and I can't remember where I found it to go back and get the details I need. I may be reading about CT, then see something in the comments relating to something I didn't think applied to me (like leaky gut), only to figure out later that I may \*have\* leaky gut issues... and I never copied out THAT info and can't find the comment I remember seeing.... sigh!

Glad there are people here who DO remember and can point us in the right direction

### Cody — 2012-03-22T20:15:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-4577>

Use Google to search Dr. K's blog and comments.

**Search within a specific website (site**

Google allows you to specify that your search results must come from a given website. For example, the query \[ iraq site:nytimes.com \] will return pages about Iraq but only from nytimes.com. The simpler queries \[ iraq nytimes.com \] or \[ iraq New York Times \] will usually be just as good, though they might return results from other sites that mention the New York Times. You can also specify a whole class of sites, for example \[ iraq site:.gov \] will return results only from a .gov domain and \[ iraq site:.iq \] will return results only from Iraqi sites.

From [http://support.google.com/websearch/bin/answer.py?hl=en&answer=136861](http://http://support.google.com/websearch/bin/answer.py?hl=en&answer=136861)

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-24T00:09:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-5174>

Has anyone really noticed that the bitter melon makes it easier?

I haven't tried it yet. Seems a tad weird. I don't want to be tied to a bottle of bitter melon for life, ya know. Course, that could just be a neolithic thought messin' with my paleolithic genes, however.

### Cody — 2012-03-24T01:21:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-5221>

Dan, I'm pretty sure that Dr. K said it was only needed for the initial adaptation. Probably from two weeks to a month. It's in one of the earlier CT blogs or the Next Evolution of the Leptin Reset, in the comments...

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-02T22:26:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brand-of-bitter-melon-extract-are-you-all-taking.211/post-10465>

That's my concern. I can feel the effects of Bitter melon within an hour of taking it. I get warm and my skin turns cherry red (regardless of what i've eaten, or if I'm currently cold or hot). But how long do we need to use this crutch before my internal pilot light turns on by itself?

# Thread: Philips Wake-up Light
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Garfield — 2012-03-17T00:02:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-1731>

As part of our Leptin Rx we blacked out the windows in the bedroom, which helped a lot with sleeping, but it made waking up at the correct time more of a problem. I remember Jack mentioning that he put in a Lutron with a light sensor to help his wife, but I didn't want to do that. We found the [Philips Hf3485 Wake-up Light Plus](http://http://www.amazon.com/Philips-Hf3485-Wake-up-Light-White/dp/B003Y6B4XE/ref=sr_1_4?ie=UTF8&qid=1331934907&sr=8-4) at Amazon and have been using it for a few days, and I can say it makes waking up much easier. (It gradually increases the light level as it gets closer to your chosen time, before the alarm goes off.)

### Glamorama — 2012-03-17T10:29:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-1894>

I have a similar product, the Pure Twilight. Very happy with it!

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-22T18:03:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-4468>

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-22T20:16:25+0200

> **primat;4597 wrote:** I love my wake-up light
>
>
>

No cons? Some of the model reviews state small things are an issue like you can't turn off the light while turning off the alarm, etc., or the sound. Any annoying things about it or all a bunch of pros?

### Garfield — 2012-03-23T05:12:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-4841>

> **Clabbergirl;4606 wrote:** No cons? Some of the model reviews state small things are an issue like you can't turn off the light while turning off the alarm, etc., or the sound. Any annoying things about it or all a bunch of pros?

I don't consider having to turn the light and the alarm off separately as a con. I normally want the sound to stop, but the light to still be on while I finish waking up.

### Glamorama — 2012-03-23T05:15:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-4842>

With the Pure Twilight, we found it hard to actually turn off the mood-light at night (we use the red light for... ahem... you know, mood-lighting...), other than that, its a brilliant and easyw ay to wake up.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-23T05:17:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-4844>

> **Garfield;4871 wrote:** I don't consider having to turn the light and the alarm off separately as a con. I normally want the sound to stop, but the light to still be on while I finish waking up.

And does it really help you wake up? I've got my bedroom nice and dark now which I love, but it's like a dark cave on the weekends when I would like to wake up before noon for a change. I never see any sunlight and am thinking this kind of thing would help. You've noticed real improvement in wake ups? I like the model they sell that comes with an iphone dock, but can't find it on Amazon. They are pricier than I'd hoped so I'm still debating getting one.

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-23T05:40:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-4851>

I have one. Mucho better than alarms. Although now im wearing a mask - its just the chirps.

### Glamorama — 2012-03-23T05:40:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-4852>

> **Clabbergirl;4874 wrote:** And does it really help you wake up? I've got my bedroom nice and dark now which I love, but it's like a dark cave on the weekends when I would like to wake up before noon for a change. I never see any sunlight and am thinking this kind of thing would help. You've noticed real improvement in wake ups? I like the model they sell that comes with an iphone dock, but can't find it on Amazon. They are pricier than I'd hoped so I'm still debating getting one.

Without mine I can sleep from 9 pm till 7 am every day. With the light-alarm I'm able to sleep 9 pm- 5 am (I feel rested and have had 8 hours) and wake up feeling ready to go. I use the 5 am-alarm 3-4 days weekly depending on gym-schedule.

### Garfield — 2012-03-23T06:10:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-4861>

> **Clabbergirl;4874 wrote:** And does it really help you wake up? I've got my bedroom nice and dark now which I love, but it's like a dark cave on the weekends when I would like to wake up before noon for a change. I never see any sunlight and am thinking this kind of thing would help. You've noticed real improvement in wake ups? I like the model they sell that comes with an iphone dock, but can't find it on Amazon. They are pricier than I'd hoped so I'm still debating getting one.

Absolutely made a difference. We too had blacked out the bedroom which improved sleep but made waking up worse, and DST didn't help. Now with the wake-up light we don't have any problem getting up when we want to.

### [email redacted] — 2012-03-23T13:25:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-4902>

> **Clabbergirl;4874 wrote: And does it really help you wake up?**

**

If nothing else, it just makes waking/getting up that much better**. It subconsciously triggers the brain into thinking the world is up instead of feeling like you're getting up in the middle of the night. And that helps get you out of bed if you have lower self control.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-23T14:57:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-4923>

> **Optim;4934 wrote: If nothing else, it just makes waking/getting up that much better**. It subconsciously triggers the brain into thinking the world is up instead of feeling like you're getting up in the middle of the night. And that helps get you out of bed if you have lower self control.

I think this just convinced me to buy one. You've described how I feel pretty much every morning - alone in my awakeness while the rest of the world slumbers! Thanks!

### PaleoSue — 2012-03-26T01:47:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-6101>

I love, love, love my wake-up light! I, too, really struggled when I made my bedroom completely black with getting up in the morning. My body just didn't want to get out of bed since I couldn't see any sunlight, even if it were 9 or 10 a.m.

The other thing I love about the Phillips light is that you can set it to gradually darken when you're ready to go to sleep. This is a great feature for me because I have a habit of reading until very late at night without realizing the time. I just get lost in a good book. So I can set the light to gradually dim for 30 minutes, which makes me feel even more sleepy and I put the book down.

Then the light gradually brightens for 30 minutes in the morning, and I almost always wake up to the light getting brighter before the actual "alarm." I love the bird chirping as the alarm feature; it's not annoying like other nature sound programs I've heard.

My biggest suggestion to improve the Phillips wake-up light is that the clock display needs to be able to be turned off. It generates a very small amount of light. It has four brightness settings, but you can't turn it off. For me, NOT being able to see the clock at night was one of the best changes I made to help fight insomnia, so I never want to see it until the morning. They should make it so you can turn the time display off, and just have the time display in the morning.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-27T17:32:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-7025>

I bought one of these over the weekend and used it for the first time this morning. I woke up before the alarm sounded, but it wasn't a normal sleep night for me as I had a TMJ/Migraine attack over the last couple of days that has disturbed my sleep significantly. I'm hoping on a regular night that I won't lay awake in anticipation of the light coming on like I did this morning. I will admit it was nice to hear birds chirping instead of that horrible buzzing sound my previous alarm made (which oddly enough is the same sound as the cash registers at my local Aldi's grocery store, putting me on edge the entire time I'm shopping there!). One of my cats ran in to investigate the chirping sound too, which was kind of humorous. We'll see if I like this thing...

### PaleoSue — 2012-03-28T14:07:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-7659>

@Clabbergirl, I hope you'll soon like it in a few more days. I have been surprised at how "sleepy" the dimming nighttime mode makes me. Unless something's going on, I almost always fall asleep rather quickly, when it used to take me 45 minutes or more to fall asleep. (I think doing CT at night also really helps here, too.)

### e_h — 2012-03-28T21:22:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-7869>

I use a blue light and asked Dr. K in the last blog post- he said "I have no problem with this"

### LinD — 2012-03-28T22:30:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-7913>

Is this bad? I cover my clock so the light doesn't bother me at night, but I sleep with an eye mask now so it shouldn't matter....

What is not good, I'm sure, is by chance waking up at night to go to the bathroom only to realize DH is awake and reading his Nook book on his phone. I wouldn't know this except for taking off the mask to traverse in the dark to the bathroom. While he has the phone display light low, it's still kinda bright.... Should I just feel my way to the bathroom and keep the mask on?

I don't mind waking up to the clock radio sound set to an AM morning station....

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-13T20:00:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-30937>

My Philips wake-up light had all sound die on it for some reason. Called Philips and they replaced it quickly with no hassle.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-26T21:31:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/philips-wake-up-light.217/post-33740>

just got mine in the mail today... looking fwd to setting it up and using it!

# Thread: CT-Thyoid connection
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 54
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-03-17T00:58:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/post-1755>

I know from the CT-series blogs, a lot of talk was done about thyroid processes. Dr. K. claimed even if you had no thyroid, CT would provide for you via TRH bypassing normal TSH system.

I will put him to the test. I have been hypothyroid for many years. I was first diagnosed in 2005 with a TSH of 54. I was put on Synthroid 150mcg and have maintained a TSH of 1.0 for 6 years at that dose.

Last summer, after doing the Leptin Reset, I had lost 70lbs from my max weight, gotten off meds for high bp, high chol, high trigs, and gout. Against my doctor's advice, I also quit my Synthroid. I tested my TSH every couple months and it went like this:

Jan 2011 (on Synthroid): TSH - 1.2, T4 - mid-range, T3 - midrange

May 2011 (off Sythroid): TSH - .55, T4 - high-range, T3 not tested

Sep 2011 (off Sythroid): TSH - 2.5, T4 - mid-range, T3 - midrange

Dec 2011 (off Synthroid): TSH - 3.6, T4 - low-range, T3 - low range, antibodies normal

Feb 2012 (ON Synthroid 30 days) - TSH - 1.5, T4 - high range, T3 - high range

Mar 2012 (ON synthroid 60 days) - TSH - .55, T4 - just above high range, T3 - just above high range)

I stopped my Synthroid on 14 March 2012. I started full-fledged CT 15 Feb 2012. I will retest 25 April.

Here is something interesting I found today. It appears it has been studied that people living in polar regions (like me) experience seasonal fluctuations in thyroid functioning. What is explained here is exactly what I experienced. This study was focusing on decreased thinking ability (mind-fog) of polar researchers and they came across what they call "Polar T3 Sydrome".

"Season could affect cognition through endocrinological changes as well. Cold

exposure and a cold season are known to affect the hypothalamic pituitary-thyroid axis

(Pääkkönen 2002, Leppäluoto et al. 2005), increasing the secretion of thyroid-stimulating

hormone (TSH), which leads to increased thyroid hormone production and clearance

rates (Reed 1995) and lower levels of free thyroid hormones. It appears that the cold induced

increased clearance rates of thyroid hormones lead to decreased levels of thyroid

hormones that sensitise pituitary thyroid cells. This state is called the polar T3 syndrome.

These seasonal changes in thyroid hormones have been demonstrated in overwintering

personnel in Antarctica (Reed et al. 1990, Reed 1995, Palinkas et al. 2001), as well as in

residents living in northern Finland and employed in outdoor occupations (Leppäluoto et

al. 1998, Hassi et al. 2001). The lowered levels of free thyroid hormones can lead to a

state of sub-clinical hypothyroidism and to increased anxiousness and depression (Xu et

al. 2003), which could impair cognition. A recent study demonstrated that anxiousness in

hypothyroidism was related to attentional and executive disturbances (Constant et al.

2005). Further support for the assumption that cognitive performance is connected to

season and decreased levels of thyroid hormones was provided by Reed et al. (2001). In

their study, personnel overwintering in Antarctica showed a decline in cognitive

performance after 11 months, but the effect was reversed with a thyroid hormone

supplement. In addition, Shurtleff et al. (1994) demonstrated that administration of

tyrosine significantly improved matching accuracy in cold.

Interesting Akman. I have been hypothyroid for over 20 years and until last summer was on Eltroxin (T4 only). I switched to Acella (porcine desiccated thyroid) last summer and feel it does a far better job for me.

We travel to Mexico for the winters and the heat seems to keep me feeling better and more active than the cold weather did at home in our cabin in a snow-belt. Of course, my diet wasn't optimal then either so that may make a difference now.

I am CT'ng as best as I can in the spring heat down here and will be interested if progress speeds up in my cold creek water up north.

### Kiera2 — 2012-03-18T03:38:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/post-2234>

Keep in mind that this research is reporting observations in _warm adapted_ people eating a 'summer' diet. I have a feeling that if they changed to a keto paleo diet, and allowed themselves to become cold adapted, rather than 'cold exposed', they would activate the ancient pathway, and BAM! Their brain would take over from their thyroid. Thyroid hormone levels wouldn't matter.

### Claudia — 2012-03-18T03:47:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/post-2240>

You seem to be very knowledgeable about testing and thyroid. I suspect I have a thyroid issue and will finally go to the doc and get tested soon. It's a new doc and I'm going to insist on the following testing:

TSH, free T3, free T4, reverse T3. I also plan on asking for HS CRP, thyroid antibodies, TP antibodies, thyroglobulin antibodies, and a salivary hormonal panel.

Anyway, can you provide any advice on this? Are these the right tests. I see you concentrate on TSH. I read that doesn't give you the whole picture.

Also, my symptoms are gaining weight with TOM that doesn't go away, impossible to lose any weight despite a very clean Paleo diet, hair loss (but this is off and on), brain fog (could be something else).

Thanks for any advice.

### Phoenix — 2012-03-18T03:54:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/post-2246>

> **Coriander;2240 wrote:** Keep in mind that this research is reporting observations in _warm adapted_ people eating a 'summer' diet. I have a feeling that if they changed to a keto paleo diet, and allowed themselves to become cold adapted, rather than 'cold exposed', they would activate the ancient pathway, and BAM! ### AKMan — 2012-03-18T04:28:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/post-2280>

> **Claudia;2246 wrote:** You seem to be very knowledgeable about testing and thyroid. I suspect I have a thyroid issue and will finally go to the doc and get tested soon. It's a new doc and I'm going to insist on the following testing:
>
> TSH, free T3, free T4, reverse T3. I also plan on asking for HS CRP, thyroid antibodies, TP antibodies, thyroglobulin antibodies, and a salivary hormonal panel.
>
>
>
> Anyway, can you provide any advice on this? Are these the right tests. I see you concentrate on TSH. I read that doesn't give you the whole picture.
>
>
>
> Also, my symptoms are gaining weight with TOM that doesn't go away, impossible to lose any weight despite a very clean Paleo diet, hair loss (but this is off and on), brain fog (could be something else).
>
>
>
> Thanks for any advice.

If you are paying out of pocket, TSH and T4 are a good place to start. If TSH is high and T4 low, you'll want to check all the rest, especially rT3 and thyroid antibodies. But if TSH is at bottom of ref range and T4 is within ref range, you are most likely not hypothyroid. If insurance is paying and doc will do it, get all you can. good luck!

### AKMan — 2012-03-18T04:35:46+0200

### AKMan — 2012-04-04T03:48:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/post-11083>

I don't get it either. I don't understand Jack's claim that TRH (thyrotropin releasing hormone) can take the place of T3 and T4. TRH stimulates the production of TSH which stimulates the thyroid to produce T4, which is converted to T3. The T3 is what regulates metabolism--or so I have always believed.

Here is a good read on the thyroid: [http://www.thyroidmanager.org/chapter/physiology-of-the-hypothalmic-pituitary-thyroidal-system/#toc-normal-physiology:](http://http://www.thyroidmanager.org/chapter/physiology-of-the-hypothalmic-pituitary-thyroidal-system/#toc-normal-physiology:)

From it: "The activity of the thyroid gland is predominantly regulated by the concentration of the pituitary glycoprotein hormone, thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH). In the absence of pituitary or of thyrotroph function hypothyroidism ensues. Thus, regulation of thyroid function in normal individuals is to a large extent determined by the factors which regulate the synthesis and secretion of TSH. Those factors are reviewed in this chapter and consist principally of thyrotropin releasing hormone (TRH) and the feedback effects of circulating thyroid hormones at the hypothalamic and pituitary levels. The consequence of the dynamic interplay of these two dominant influences on TSH secretion, the positive effect of TRH on the one hand and the negative effects of thyroid hormones on the other, result in a remarkably stable concentration of TSH in the circulation and consequently little alteration in the level of circulating thyroid hormones from day to day and year to year. This regulation is so carefully maintained that an abnormal serum TSH in most patients is believed to indicate the presence of a disorder of thyroid gland function. The utility of TSH measurements has been recognized and its use has remarkably increased, due to the development of immunometric methodologies for its accurate quantitation in serum, although the criteria to define a “normal range” still remain matted of controversy."

So please, let's talk this out...how can CT bypass the thyroid, and yes, I've read CT6.

From CT-6:

"""THYROID TOO?

ALL REUNITE (MORE GEEKY THOUGH): THE WARM ADAPTED STORY of metabolism and biochemistry YOU HEAR REGURGITATED EVERYWHERE ON THE NET:

Once leptin enters and binds to its receptors, it effects the lateral hypothalamic tracts to immediately send a second messenger signal to the thyroid to signal it to up-regulate thyroid function and efficiency. See whne we are warm adapted it require help to partially access this pathway from T3 hormone. If you are LR you never can access this pathway. This specifically is how we can raise our basal metabolic rate when we are leptin sensitive. These coupled events, matched with leptin’s actions peripherally in muscles, occur at the UCP3 sites……to burn fat as we sleep at a higher basal metabolic rate. This means electron chain transport does not make ATP as usual. When leptin allows this uncoupling to occur we make heat and not energy from normal metabolism. This means we will burn off our excess calories as pure heat. This is one reason why calories in and calories out argument makes no biologic sense once you understand how leptin works. Humans are built to burn fat at night as we sleep to loose excess weight we don’t need. This is our modern day equivalent of hibernation. Out big fat amazing human brain got rid of winter sleep and took over a two hour window during sleep to replace it. CT-7 covers the gorgeous symphony of human circadian biology. Since it shrunk our risk of mismatches has risen exponentially. This is how your brain or feelings, can undercut you, when you are unaware of what a sped up epigenetics meant to human mammalian biochemistry. SKEPTICS: You wanted data, and now I am killing you with it.

THE COLD ADAPTED HUMAN THYROID FXN: does not bother with T3 at all. Why? When you are supremely LS by cold you go straight the the source, the hypothalamus and make TRH from the brain. The brain controls all thyroid function in cold…….forget the Moose thyroid.

SKEPTIC BOMB: You bypass all hormones and TSH too. TRH drives the whole show. The brain is completely in control and it up regualtes fat burning everywhere. This is how the Ancient Pathway lights your pilot light. The warm adapted human always complains about the cold and always feels cold……the cold adapted on is always pink to cherry in cold radiating heat like a furnace. You can thank TRH for this. This is does not even require a thyroid gland either. Is not life grand in the cold, folks?"""

I read through the 1300+ responses, and nobody seemed to question these claims about the thyroid. ### AKMan — 2012-04-05T01:13:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/post-11524>

From Wikipedia:

"The (thyroid hormones, T3/T4) act on nearly every cell in the body. They act to increase the basal metabolic rate, affect protein synthesis, help regulate long bone growth (synergy with growth hormone) and neuronal maturation, and increase the body's sensitivity to catecholamines (such as adrenaline) by permissiveness. The thyroid hormones are essential to proper development and differentiation of all cells of the human body. These hormones also regulate protein, fat, and carbohydrate metabolism, affecting how human cells use energetic compounds. They also stimulate vitamin metabolism. Numerous physiological and pathological stimuli influence thyroid hormone synthesis.

Thyroid hormone leads to heat generation in humans. However, the thyronamines function via some unknown mechanism to inhibit neuronal activity; this plays an important role in the hibernation cycles of mammals and the moulting behaviour of birds. One effect of administering the thyronamines is a severe drop in body temperature."

So, how can CT bypass all this?

My morning cortisol was 14.9 (range 5-25 mcg/dl)

I also had low D, bottom of the range at 28.8. I was surprised. I get lots of sunlight spring/summer and into fall. Then, that doesn't happen in the winter, as I"m in NY, but I take cod liver oil for vitamin D. Guess that isn't working.

### marthabob2u — 2012-04-25T01:19:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/post-18510>

Well I'm back in Canada and ready to arrange thyroid lab tests, but I just noticed a surprising change in myself.

I don't know whether it relates to changing to desiccated thyroid meds last June or to CT'ing, but my outer eyebrows are growing in again. The outer third has been very sparse for years before now.

I can also state that Mexican cold water with ice blocks added is much warmer than good old Canadian tap water- holy crap!

### Claudia — 2012-04-25T01:20:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-thyoid-connection.218/post-18512>

I have a weird tightness in my throat, it comes and goes. I only started noticing it around the same time last year. I think it went away for awhile completely. Now it's back. I'm thinking it is def. thyroid related. I'm so bummed because based on my labs that i posted above, I'm pretty sure my doc is going to follow through because the numbers aren't out of range. I think I'm going to have to go alternative medicine and I have some options for that but they will not be covered by insurance most likely and we have huge financial struggles at the moment.

### Destiny — 2012-04-25T02:02:05+0300

I just haven't read enough to put the puzzle together and I think I have brain fog, too, and it makes it harder. Yes, it is a puzzle. I was also surprised with my vit. D at 114 since I don't get a lot of sunlight and do not supplement heavily. It doesn't make much sense. I had my salivary cortisol, it is not bad during the day but is spikes at night. I am trying to work on that. Just recently I got a high TSH number and low T3. I am hoping if I take care of the cortisol that my thyroid will adjust as well.

I see you are in New York like me so that is very interesting that your D is so good and mine is so bad. I work so hard at getting lots of sun during the summer. # Thread: How are you getting the water cold enough? It's spring already.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### itsybitsy — 2012-03-17T01:37:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1772>

The warmer temps mean warmer tap water and it is more of a challenge to practically and economically get the temp down. Out of the tap, mine is in the 60's.

My fridge only makes so much ice and it melts before the water gets cold enough.

How are you doing it?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-17T02:10:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1786>

> **itsybitsy;1774 wrote:** The warmer temps mean warmer tap water and it is more of a challenge to practically and economically get the temp down. Out of the tap, mine is in the 60's.
>
>
>
> My fridge only makes so much ice and it melts before the water gets cold enough.
>
>
>
> How are you doing it?

I use reusable ice blocks that I keep in ziplock baggies and all the ice my fridge makes. My water is about 60-62 coming right out of the tap. I get the water to 55-58. I need more ice blocks but have run out of room in my freezer!

### vwaggs — 2012-03-17T02:16:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1791>

I am putting water from the sink into big tupperware like containers so that I don't use up my filtered ice and freezing it in the freezer.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-17T02:42:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1802>

Freeze tap water in emptied 1/2 gal milk jugs or other tall, slender things if you're struggling with freezer space. Then lay them in the tub and let them chill that water on down. Easy to rinse off and refreeze when done, and free.

### mshobbs — 2012-03-17T07:47:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1878>

> **Clabbergirl;1805 wrote:** Freeze tap water in emptied 1/2 gal milk jugs or other tall, slender things if you're struggling with freezer space. Then lay them in the tub and let them chill that water on down. Easy to rinse off and refreeze when done, and free.

+1 for the milk jugs and the tupperware etc. Here in OZ, the water temp is around 80 so it takes a sh!tload of ice to get to anywhere near 60F. I have 12 x 2L milk bottles cut in half and frozen along with a couple of tupperware containers filled with water. I have a 90L upright freezer that is used exclusively for the production of ice!

We are starting to come into autumn so hears hoping the water starts to cool down shortly.

### quelsen — 2012-03-17T09:19:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1888>

Old Fage containers and large ziploc containers so far two and two. my tap water is currently 51F

### Glamorama — 2012-03-17T10:37:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1895>

> **mshobbs;1881 wrote:** +1 for the milk jugs and the tupperware etc. Here in OZ, the water temp is around 80 so it takes a sh!tload of ice to get to anywhere near 60F. I have 12 x 2L milk bottles cut in half and frozen along with a couple of tupperware containers filled with water. I have a 90L upright freezer that is used exclusively for the production of ice!
>
>
>
> We are starting to come into autumn so hears hoping the water starts to cool down shortly.

In Melbourne the tap-water is about 60-65 degrees. I throw in ice bricks and throw a frozen gel-pack over my belly.

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-17T11:21:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1901>

> **Glamorama;1900 wrote:** In Melbourne the tap-water is about 60-65 degrees. I throw in ice bricks and throw a frozen gel-pack over my belly.

What brand/type of gel pad? I'd like to use something like that on my belly too.

### sd13 — 2012-03-17T16:03:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1973>

I filled and froze 11 one liter bottles and I use three homemade gel packs. Plus keeping the water level in the tub a wee bit lower aids in getting the tap temp down from 60 degrees to 50. I use the gel packs on areas that aren't direcetly covered with water, plus I rotate--lay on my back at start, flip to stomach etc. In addition the bottles can be placed under arms, under the chin...you get the idea. I got the gel pack idea from someone on line--ziplock bag with a ratio of 1 part rubbing alcohol to 2 parts water--but frankly I don't measure. The bags are very cold and you should use caution at the start by placing some sort of materital (towel etc) between bag and skin. The bottles don't have that issue being placed directly on the skin.

### JC2K — 2012-03-17T17:02:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-1997>

I use two liter soda bottles. The 1 liter bottles sd13 mentioned might be better though if you've got a small tub. Annoying thing (to me) is the bumping against the sides of the tubs. Seems to sound like a drum. I might try using the alcohol/water in the bottles (instead of zip lock bags). Might get it colder than just ice.

### tellmisty — 2012-03-17T18:16:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-2031>

Ya'll are so creative with your ideas! I just pick up a 20-lb bag of ice at Kroger. ;-) Siphon off some of the ice into ziploc bags for my torso; the rest goes into the water. Keeping up with the ice is kind of a pain ... this is going to a $100 / month expense if I keep buying the 20-lb bags! I'm gonna work on some of your ideas, see what Jack does, too.

### Bee — 2012-03-17T18:56:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-2051>

These ideas are so much better than buying bags of ice! Off to find the rubbing alcohol and some ziplocks, and grab those empty milk containers from the recycling box!

### tellmisty — 2012-03-19T06:14:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-2632>

Tonight for my cold bath, two huge slabs of ice - frozen into containers about a foot in diameter. I think they worked just as well as the 20-lb bags as far as water temps. Yeah, save the dough and it's so much more convenient, too!

### nonchalant — 2012-03-19T17:44:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-2768>

Water getting warmer, yes. I used up my frozen bottles of water and all my ice cubes, then remembered the quarts of lard I have in the freezer. They did the trick.  I'll try putting alcohol in those bottles, maybe that will help as the water warms even more.

Not looking forward to our warm tap water summers. I've learned that every summer I need refill my water glass after I brush my teeth and let it sit. Then when I brush my teeth again, at least the water will have cooled down to room temp. Guess I can do that with the tub as well.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-03-19T17:47:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-2769>

my icemaker got jammed up this weekend because of the overload from CT. So I was forced to create my own large blocks of ice in the freezer. But lucky for me, my well water is usually 50 or below if I run it long enough.

### Groketteliveson — 2012-03-19T19:37:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-2833>

I'm bought some 1 gallon buckets with lids. I'm going to try and add rubbing alcohol to see if they will get colder. How much alcohol do you normally add?

### kathylu — 2012-03-19T21:04:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-2886>

I'm using 1 qt soda water bottles (frozen of course). It takes 8 of them to keep the water between 53-55 degrees. Then I pop them back in the freezer.

### Adriana — 2012-03-21T08:30:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-3808>

I like the idea of 4 gallons of reusable frozen containers. Is it possible to refreeze gallons daily? In my case the freezer is downstairs and the tub is up, hauling them up and back downstairs adds weight training .

Reusable frozen containers also allows you to use less water as this raises the water level by displacement. It also allows recycling the tub water, although it might require additional ice since the standing water will warm up to ambient temperatures which are higher than the temperature of the water coming out of the spigot.

### Jillm — 2012-05-07T11:30:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-21896>

You are doing well, mshobbs

### fitness@home — 2012-05-07T19:59:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-21973>

I feel blessed to have found a new local business that is selling 20lb bags of ice for $1 each. It is a self-serve ice trailer set up in a shopping plaza. Insert your $ or card, choose bagged or loose, and wait for it to drop down one of the chutes. (Great idea for a niche business in Houston. Wish we had thought of it...)

Two bags thrown into my bathwater 30 minutes before my bath cools it perfectly. There is plenty of ice left in the bags to place directly on my abdomen/chest.

I do ice baths 2-3 x per week and ice packs on the other nights. So, even at 3x per week it is only $24 per month. We have a large freezer so storage isn't an issue.

### Drew — 2012-05-10T10:06:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-are-you-getting-the-water-cold-enough-its-spring-already.220/post-22790>

I guess I'm lucky in that I live close to the Cascade Mountains. The tap water is pretty cold year-round (45 degrees or so), due to melting snow.

# Thread: curious about leptin/cortisol effects
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/curious-about-leptin-cortisol-effects.222/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### gmel1084 — 2012-03-17T03:00:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/curious-about-leptin-cortisol-effects.222/post-1808>

Hello everyone,

Im relatively new to the subject of hormone optimization but am eager to learn.

I have have been athletic my whole life, but despite being very active (I'm on my feet all day work, I have been training intensely in martial arts for years, and also do CrossFit up to 3x/ week), I just can't get to the point with my body where I'm very lean. Coming across the topic of hormone optimization on Mike Mahler's website, I began to wonder if it is Leptin/ Cortisol related. Upon more research, it definitely seems to be the case. With my activity level, I wouldn't be surprised if I'm experiencing adrenal fatigue, HIGH CORTISOL, and LR, IR.

My diet for the most part is good, but I definitely cheat a lot. The last body fat test I got said I was around 13%, but I store most of it in my lower stomach and love handles. For a very active person for years this is frustrating.

I'm 27 years old, 5'7, and 155lbs. Obviously not a bad thing but I'm intrigued by hormone optimization and its role in the fitness industry.

After coming across Dr. Kruse's Leptin RX info I knew I was on the right track.

I try to space out my meals as much as I can, but with the amount of workout volume I do, its hard when you want to optimize your post workout recovery with shakes, and make sure that you're getting enough calories.

Any help is appreciated!

Greg

### Owl — 2012-03-17T08:07:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/curious-about-leptin-cortisol-effects.222/post-1883>

Adrenal fatigue is actually what happens when you've had high cortisol for a long time, and then the adrenals start producing less and less of it. Then your levels can fluctuate during the day from too low to too high. Over time, cortisol levels drift downward more and more, until they are all too low.

I have high cortisol, although it's much better now since I've been working on reducing it. My symptoms were being a night owl, not feeling tired at night, staying up till 1am. I didn't sleep well. Also waking up at 3 or 4 am or so. When I got up in the morning, I was not rested. I needed caffeine to get through the day, because my sleep was so bad.

I tested my cortisol with an adrenal saliva test. My night time and morning cortisol was through the roof, no wonder I wasn't sleeping well.

Dr. K would tell you to cut out the Crossfit. He thinks chronic cardio and/or very intense workouts like that will shorten your telomeres and therefore your life. He'd also tell you the shakes after workouts are not necessary.

I started on the leptin reset last summer, and am eating post leptin now. I used to snack all day, because I was hungry all day. With the leptin reset, you have to limit yourself to 3 meals a day, no snacking. Eventually, you get to 2 meals a day, because you're just not that hungry. And it really works. I am not hungry in between meals anymore. I feel much better about that, not having to always carry snacks with me to keep from getting ravenous.

According to Dr. K, you can spot reduce fat by using cold packs on those areas. There's a medical device called Zeltiq, which operates on that principle. Research showed that frostbite victims had fat cells permanently destroyed, and Zeltiq was designed around that. With the cold packs, you're not trying for frostbite, just red/pink skin. It takes longer than Zeltiq, but it works on the same principle. It sounds ideal for you. Check out the Cold Thermagenesis (CT) threads.

### gmel1084 — 2012-03-17T21:28:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/curious-about-leptin-cortisol-effects.222/post-2106>

Thank you for the response.

I'm definitely going to look into CT. Im looking forward to it.

what adrenal saliva test did you use?

I just came across this site yesterday and there's so much info to go through. Whats the best to start with in regards to Dr. K's Leptin, CT, Cortisol thoughts, etc?

### Owl — 2012-03-18T04:54:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/curious-about-leptin-cortisol-effects.222/post-2296>

Here's the leptin rx, the original instructions: [http://jackkruse.com/my-leptin-prescription/](http://http://jackkruse.com/my-leptin-prescription/)

And read the leptin FAQs: [http://jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/)

For cortisol: [http://jackkruse.com/primal-cortisol-response/](http://http://jackkruse.com/primal-cortisol-response/)

He's done 6 or 7 blogs on CT, but here's the first, where he tells how to do it: [http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/)

You really do need to read the rest too, though.

Make sure you read the comments after each one, sometimes there's even better info there.

I got my adrenal saliva test here, they have the best price: [http://www.canaryclub.org/index.php?Itemid=167](http://http://www.canaryclub.org/index.php?Itemid=167)

They send the kit to you, and you send it back, they email the results.

### gmel1084 — 2012-03-18T05:02:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/curious-about-leptin-cortisol-effects.222/post-2298>

Thanks a lot!

I'm looking forward to delving into all of this info.

There's so much information out there and this is a great site.

I'm going to start with cutting out the post workout shakes and just getting in a clean paleo meal within a couple hours post workout.

And I'm also going to spread my meals out to 3x per day with protein and fat in the morning and see how it goes.

My workouts are mainly in the morning so I have to time it properly.

### Owl — 2012-03-18T09:26:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/curious-about-leptin-cortisol-effects.222/post-2318>

You're welcome, tell me how you it goes for you. It was tough for me with no snacking for the first week or so. I was eating 3 huge meals a day, and was still hungry in between. Breakfast was 5 eggs and 1/2lb of ground beef! (and I'm female, 5'7", and at that time 147lb) But then it got much easier, and I wasn't hungry at all in between.

At your weight and height, you may not be leptin resistant. I know I was, because I had just lost 75lb with hcg, and was having trouble keeping it off, had gained back 15lb. (I then lost 13 of those pounds on the reset.) The leptin reset is designed to confuse your hypothalamus so it reroutes your leptin signalling to an older way of accounting for calories we all used in utero and up to age 1 -2. If you're leptin sensitive, this will be a waste of time, because your hypothalamus is working fine. So you may want to follow the eating plan of the post leptin rx, for people who are leptin sensitive: [http://jackkruse.com/so-you-completed-the-leptin-rx-what-is-next/](http://http://jackkruse.com/so-you-completed-the-leptin-rx-what-is-next/)

### gmel1084 — 2012-03-18T18:21:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/curious-about-leptin-cortisol-effects.222/post-2401>

thats the thing.... I'm not sure... I probably have to get a reverse t3 blood test to see if its elevated. I'm very active and have been for a long time. Most of the fat I have is in my stomach and love handles... just getting on track with paleo should do it for me I think... In addition to CT and controlling cortisol and Inflammation.
