# Thread: Low Thyroid, Gall Bladder Removed and Roux en Y due to botched surgery
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jocelyn7777 — 2012-03-30T22:11:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-8935>

Dr. Kruse,

I want to start the Leptin Rx because my doctor already tested my Leptin and said I'm leptin resistant. Now, the only problem is that ever since I had my gallbladder removed and had to have repair surgery I'm hungry 24/7.

At first the doctors all told me that it was just heartburn, but I've done rounds of Prilosec and Zegerid. During the two weeks of medication, the hunger subsides a tiny bit. As soon as the round is through, I'm back to starving all the time.

I'm concerned that even doing the Leptin Rx, even eating something like 75 grams of protein, I'm scared I'll feel hungry just a few hours later.

I feel like I could constantly be a binge eater, but without the purging. It has been very disturbing to someone that was never food dependant. It was just something that you had to do, not something that I loved to do.

Now, I don't love it, but I crave food all the time. Even if I eat a huge steak for dinner like 12 oz with a large salad, I can be hungry about 15 min later.

Will doing the leptin reset fix this? And, if it works, will I be starving the first few weeks?

Thanks,

Jocelyn

### janagram — 2012-03-31T04:19:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-9081>

is it hunger... or the need for betaine hcl and enzymes (due tp no gallbladder)?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-31T05:14:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-9094>

I'd re-read the Leptin Rx protocol and the FAQ post again. It's not just protein (though you need that 50g + for the BAB) but also fat you need. The fat is definitely part of what satiates the hunger. Protein alone won't do it. Most of us don't find that we're starving at all, but the cravings remain and can be heightened in that first few weeks, especially if you've been eating carbs. You'll need to concentrate on getting those good fats in (the tallow, ghee and coconut oil of course) in good amounts.

### jocelyn7777 — 2012-04-01T02:01:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-9545>

I forgot to mention the Roux En Y was not for bariatric surgery. It was connected to a hepaticojejunostomy.

I wish I had issues with needing bile salts, but if anything mine overproduces bile (and sends me rushing to the bathroom).

I'll re-read the Leptin Rx, but I've been adding a heaping tablespoon of coconut oil to the eggs that I cook in the morning. Do you think I need to add more fat than that?

Maybe I need to add some of the supplements, like magnesium, etc?

It isn't carbs that I'm craving... It is any kind of food. I could eat bacon, meat, salad, anything... I could eat carbs because they are there, but the hunger isn't for carbs specifically.

I've wondered if maybe the liver bile is backing up into my stomach or something and causing me to feel hungry.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T16:01:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-9751>

Yes, you need more fat than that, most likeyl. CO, butter, tallow, ,lard, etc. I'd strongly recommended trying betaine HCl (and staying FAR away from anything that aims to reduce your stomach acid, as you almost certainly have too little). If you can take a capsule and not feel stomach discomfort or burns or runs, etc., you need it. I need a LOT. I maxed out at one point and now need a lot again after some time off it.

Anyway, digestive enzymes are also a good path to go down.

But yes, the type of hunger you're talking about, many people, including me, have had success in killing. I started the LR with 50g ofprotein, and it helped a lot with cravings, etc., but I still felt hungry after eating it.. I went to 65g and still felt every morning like I could eat the whole thing over again, but didn't. After a month at that level, though (and that was the level for me, your mileage may vary), I started feeling satisfied after breakfast. After another month, I couldn't eat lunch anymore, and haven't needed lunch more than once in nearly fivev months.

It works. Somehow it tells the body it is being nourished, and \*will continue to be\* nourished, and shuts off the desperate hunger from a body that fears malnourishment.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-01T16:11:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-9755>

> **jocelyn7777;9607 wrote:** I forgot to mention the Roux En Y was not for bariatric surgery. It was connected to a hepaticojejunostomy.
>
>
>
> I wish I had issues with needing bile salts, but if anything mine overproduces bile (and sends me rushing to the bathroom).
>
>
>
> I'll re-read the Leptin Rx, but I've been adding a heaping tablespoon of coconut oil to the eggs that I cook in the morning. Do you think I need to add more fat than that?
>
>
>
> Maybe I need to add some of the supplements, like magnesium, etc?
>
>
>
> It isn't carbs that I'm craving... It is any kind of food. I could eat bacon, meat, salad, anything... I could eat carbs because they are there, but the hunger isn't for carbs specifically.
>
>
>
> I've wondered if maybe the liver bile is backing up into my stomach or something and causing me to feel hungry.

No, you def need digestive enzymes! When you have your gallbladder removed you can no longer digest fats. That is why you are "running to the bathroom". When you can't digest fats they are just shooting through your system without being broken down and assimilated. Also heartburn is a sign of low stomach acid.. if you actually have heartburn. I think you should get digestive enzymes.. making sure it has the stuff that digests fat.. should be easy. After a couple days I bet you stop feeling so hungry. I think you're feeling so hungry because you are unable to digest so much of the food you are eating you are literally starving yourself even though you are eating.

### jocelyn7777 — 2012-04-01T23:13:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-9947>

Thanks everyone... Any specific suggestions on the brands or amounts. I'm one of those that doesn't do so well when faced with too many options. i stand around and look dumb trying to figure out what to buy!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-02T02:40:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-10056>

Me, too. I use Digest Gold for enzymes & Nutrivene or Twin Labs for HCl. I'd try Nutrivene first, b/c their caps are smaller, so you can start low and work up. But when you're at the top, it's helpful not to have to take so many, so I like Twin Labs then.

With HCl, my understanding is that you want to take it at the beginning, or partly through, each meal. Start with one before a meal. If no discomfort, do one the next meal. Then after a few days, two at one meal, then two at the other after a few days. Then three. Etc. The protocol I saw said to stop at three, but I don't know if that's universally recommended. At whatever point you start feeling discomfort (it varies for everyone; for me it was burning runs & nausea that showed up 4-5hrs after meals), start backing down slowly, continuing to back down each time you get to your personal max again. (It's not nearly so bad as I'm describing it, lol.)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-02T07:54:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-10221>

I think you should probably just give it a go. You started by saying, "**I want to start the Leptin Rx** because my doctor already tested my Leptin and said I'm leptin resistant. Now, the only problem is that ever since I had my gallbladder removed and had to have repair surgery I'm hungry 24/7." So give it a try and see how you fare. My guess is you'll be great!

### Casey — 2012-04-02T08:14:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-thyroid-gall-bladder-removed-and-roux-en-y-due-to-botched-surgery.547/post-10224>

Adding coconut oil to Green tea is a way to up your fat intake.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-02T00:13:59+0300

I would say to make sure you use gelatanized (cooked) maca rather than raw. It's never traditionally consumed raw. Whole World Botanicals and Maca Magic are both good brands.

### nath5212 — 2013-04-24T09:00:53+0300

Whole World Botanicals and Maca Magic are both good brands.

Maca is made out of a combination of alkaloids that stimulate certain parts of the brain (such as the hypophysis and the hypothalamus) to produce different kinds of hormones (testosterone) that improve the sex drive and the libido.

Does anyone find they get extremely warm after eating? My cheeks get red and flushed and I just feel hot all over. It doesn't happen with every meal and I can't link it to a certain food, so I don't think it's an intolerance. Is this just another sign of the pilot light turning on?

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-31T03:29:57+0300

# Thread: Cook Book - post recipe experiences here
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cook-book-post-recipe-experiences-here.552/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-31T03:06:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cook-book-post-recipe-experiences-here.552/post-9050>

Just thought it might me good to have a thread where people post their results/questions/culinary disasters trying to cook from the Optimal Book. Share your knowledge!

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-31T03:14:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cook-book-post-recipe-experiences-here.552/post-9054>

The Chokin On Shrimp

Made it tonight as it was a Friday during Lent - yay yummy meatless meal.

I used frozen shrimp that had all the work already done for me - was feeling lazy.

The recipe has two cans of artichokes listed, but I figured that was a typo.

As luck would have it I had shrimp stock in the freezer and used it instead of chicken stock (no meat on Friday...)

The hubby absolutely refuses anything with coconut, so I made the sauce with olive oil/butter (yes, I'm of French & Italian stock)

I served it with braised Lacinato kale on the side. The non paleo fam members ate some Jasmine rice with theirs.

I added sea salt and pepper to my sauce.

It was very, very tasty and I will definitely make it again, taking the time with fresh shrimp - so much better flavor. And I would be inclined to saute them vice boil them. Again more work, but that will yield even more flavor.

The dh ate it and when I asked what adjustment he would make... he said he would top it all with crumbled bacon or a toasted nut to add some crunch.

Definitely a winner!

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-01T02:49:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cook-book-post-recipe-experiences-here.552/post-9597>

Hi Friends,

I have been doing a low carbohydrate version of the SCD/GAPS/modified paleo diet for over six years. I have found and developed many recipes that would be good for anyone on the modified paleo diet. Of course, not all food agrees with all people so use you good judgement with my recipes. Here is a link an index of all the recipes on the **eatkamloops.org** website. eatkamloops.org also has a listing of over 150 local farms and ranches in Kamloops, BC, Canada:

[http://www.eatkamloops.org/specific-carbohydrate-diet-scd-recipes/](http://http://www.eatkamloops.org/specific-carbohydrate-diet-scd-recipes/)

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-15T03:55:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cook-book-post-recipe-experiences-here.552/post-15198>

So I made the Picasso Shrimp and it was a disaster. The breading got too wet just like the recipe warned. Good news is that with the egg mixture it made wonderful "hush puppies". I also made the recipe again in a smaller quantity as breading for some baked haddock that was quite yummy.

# Thread: Avocado oil: do or don't?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Maria Anna — 2012-03-31T12:35:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/post-9145>

How about avocado oil during the Leptin Rx? Do or don't?

### hazyjane — 2012-04-01T23:25:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/post-9955>

I would focus more on sat. fat like butter and coconut oil and on omega 3 fats but I think a little avocado oil now and then is ok (it's monounsaturated, similar to olive oil). Don't cook with it, though- save it for salad dressing or mayo.

### Maria Anna — 2012-04-01T23:36:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/post-9966>

That was exactly I wanted to use it for! Thanks so much!!!!

### hazyjane — 2012-04-02T02:55:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado-oil-do-or-dont.555/post-10070>

I really love avocado oil as a skin moisturizer! I use it on my arms and legs and in the winter, on my face

# Thread: CTing your pets
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Adria — 2012-03-31T16:10:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9204>

I live out in the country in cold climate. It's still snowing here now, this wk its been around 20 degrees. We have 2 lge dogs that are free to roam our property and can go outside whenever they want to. They swim in the lake almost every day when the water is open, ice melted. Now spring is coming and the work for us starts! They'll be full of sand almost every day from diving into the creek. We hose them off with cold water shower outside, sometimes multiple times in a day.

They are both 7yo and the vet is amazed. They've never had to have their nails cut, teeth are white and healthy, they're slim and ripped with muscle.

I think they're cold adapted. I want them to stay healthy and live a very long life !! Learning alot here to apply to my pets. I plan to make their showers extra cold and be sure they get fresh meat in their diet everyday to preserve their heath.

### vwaggs — 2012-03-31T16:28:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9208>

I thought about this too! My Chihuahuas HATE any temp under 70 though. I have to put them outside myself to get them to go. My dogs would hate me forever if I stuck them in cold water! I would be reported for animal abuse for sure LOL! Amazing results for your dogs though! Makes complete sense that it would work for them too!

### Adria — 2012-03-31T16:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9212>

vwaggs, suggestion for your little dogs, maybe you would maybe start with putting their paws in cold sink water then giving yummy treat like some chicken afterwards, then each time in cold a little longer. They might start to like it because they want that treat!

### Kirstin — 2012-03-31T16:42:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9215>

I've been thinking the same thing - how to leverage this for my dogs! My oldest collie is almost 13 and has some hindquarters weakness and knuckling and I've been wondering if I should be using cold packs on her back. I have been letting her stay outside most days, but because of her age don't want her to get too cold (MN here, so we are warmer than normal, but it's been fairly cool). I've also been trying to switch them from kibble to real food - anybody know if it is easier on their guts to go to raw or cooked food? Several episodes of diarrhea as I've tried switching over, even slowly.

### Adria — 2012-03-31T18:26:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-your-pets.559/post-9265>

Kirstin with your dog I would suggest making cooked rice w/meat then slowly reduce the rice until mostly meat. After adapted to that you could add some chunks of raw meat when you serve it, increasing raw slowly. Then when adapted to that add more fatty meat very slowly. One of my dogs took a long time to transition, probably a year, now hes doing great.

# Thread: CT for weight gain?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-weight-gain.561/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jonnyh — 2012-03-31T18:06:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-weight-gain.561/post-9249>

Hi

Is there anyone who is using CT to gain weight? most posts I read are about CT being great for weightloss etc. but dont see many about gaining weight.

I'm 5'9", 140lbs, and could do with putting on a bit of weight. Since eating primal for the last 2 years I have lost weight and hover around the 140 mark.

The only way I seem to put weight on is to up the carbs, lots of sweet potatoes, fruit etc. and hit the weights. The trouble is Dr K advises against starches in the winter while CT'ing.

I cant just keep eating more fat surely?! my cholesterol is already through the roof, though my trigs and HDL are good, and the ratios are sound, the LDL is very high though!

I'd be keen to know of others in the same sutuation as me.

Thanks for reading.

### Destiny — 2012-03-31T19:47:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-weight-gain.561/post-9289>

I just have to comment - all I can say - I am jealous. Obviously, I am not in the same situation as you.

### Inger — 2012-04-01T17:43:18+0300

That is an awesome idea. Love it! So, you are only staying in for 4 minutes? Are you making progress with such a short time? Your water is much colder than what Dr. Kruse recommends. I think he does 50 the coldest.

### Birdy — 2012-04-01T22:13:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-9922>

very cool. I ws going to barrel-shop, but now it looks like I need a 2-person set-up.

### Inger — 2012-04-02T07:46:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10216>

> **Claudia;9959 wrote:** That is an awesome idea. Love it! So, you are only staying in for 4 minutes? Are you making progress with such a short time? Your water is much colder than what Dr. Kruse recommends. I think he does 50 the coldest.

Yes I am staying for 4 minutes when the water is that cold. When the water was 41 degrees F as it was warmer outside, I did 10 minutes. I shiver about 15-30 minutes after so I guess it is effective enough.. I have no batthub so the alternativ is to use the shower what I did until hubby got me the barrel. The shower takes much longer time though to get that cold. This is short and effective (I hope..). THe water will get warmer as it is soon spring here so then I will stay longer for sure! I figure it will be okay to follow the temps in the nature.. I know it is too cold but that is why I stay so short. I do get adapted cause I can stand cold a little better now and I am starting to feel so warm at night/mornings in bed so I guess it is a good sign. But I feel I am adapting rather slow I hate to shiver that much after... The bath is a piece of cake but the shivering after is not too pleasant.. I also had detox, like sinusitis and cough... but they are gone now. I started to take milkthistle, K2, vit B, curcumin a few days ago too.

I do the bath mostly to times / day. Some days I shower warm in the morning and finish off with cold and then take the bath in the evening or daytime. I do facedunks too in the mornings and sometimes in the evening.

### Inger — 2012-04-02T07:49:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10217>

> **MamaGrok;9980 wrote:** Do you have anything to sit on, or are you just curled up, like a Baby Bucket?
>
>
>
>
> [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/9980=74-tummy_tub.jpg)

Yes, I curl up just like that.

And try to make my back not touch the barrel so it gets cold exposure too, it works fine.

### Inger — 2012-04-02T07:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10220>

> **Marsha;9977 wrote:** Inger, I like your setup! Do you empty this every day, or just put the lid on at night and add water as necessary? Also, is this hard to get into -do you have help getting out?

I do not put the lid on at all.. and I figure will change the water once in a while. Now when it is so cold, I guess very occasional like once in a month.. and in summer I will change it more often. I can use the water for the plants in the yard then. I do not need any help to get up and yes it is very easy to get in. I always stay for a few seconds with only feets and legs in the water and then I slowly sumberge..

### ste colombe — 2012-04-02T08:13:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10223>

This is to the ladies out there. Inger WONDERFUL photo, thanks for posting. Ladies, I worry that being fully emerged will melt away our lovely bits, so I lie in the bath and cover mine with my hands raising them out of the water, so I am nearly fully emerged only my upper chest is out of the water. Yours thoughts ??- Jack you are so busy, so I would never presume you to answer this, but if you have the time, am I wrong in thinking as they are made mainly of fat, they would also shrink in the cold?

### Casey — 2012-04-02T08:20:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10225>

Jack has responded to similar comments over at MDA. Yes it would shrink the fat but it would also get rid of the toxins stored in fat. Depends on how healthy you want to be....

### Inger — 2012-04-02T08:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-10226>

My boobies have shrinked since CT. They was not real big to begin with... BUT. They are nice and firm!! I think they look better now. I do not care if my boobs are small, if they are firm and beautiful and healthy. I do not need BHs at all anymore.. I can run, jump.. without too, no pain or discomfort at all!

I also had some strange occasional pain in my right boobie at the beginning of the CT. Not anymore. I guess there was some healing going on?

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-04T04:50:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-11113>

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/11182=95-temp.jpg)
[](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/11182=96-in the pool.jpg>)

Here we are CT'ing today in our pool! Don't know if you can see the first one, thermometer says 50 degrees. The second picture is my husband, 7 year old son, and me.

My son saw me perusing Dr. Kruse's facebook page and so I told him this is the doctor who taught mommy about about CT (he's been listening to me talk to my husband about it and really understands a lot of it). Anyhow, he said "That's cool, can we invite him over to swim with us?!" I told him no, that he lived down in Nashville and he was a very busy Doctor. THen he says, "Well, when we go on vacation in May can we go meet our latin teacher and Dr. Kruse and swim in his pool?" lol (my son and I are learning latin together using a course called Visual Latin and he really likes the instructor )

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-07T02:17:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-12328>

My new cold tub!!!!!

[](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/12397=105-cold tub.jpg>)

It is a 100 gallon stock tub, I got it for 60 dollars at a local farm supply store. I found a commercial ice machine about 2 1/2 miles from my house that sells ices for 1.75 for 20 pounds, so for 3.50 I can get 40 pounds of ice. I am going tomorrow to get a thermometer and a small aquarium pump to circulate the water. Should be interesting......

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-07T02:19:44+0300

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-05T10:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-21554>

Inger; 'cool' cool tub! I feel so guilty as all the water goes down the plughole after my dip in the bath in our second floor bathroom. Your tub idea could be the answer!

You also 'invented' a great new CT word in on of your posts; an swop of m and b keys changed 'submerge' to '_sumberge_'

Sumberge: Latin meets scandinavian. "To become one with the ice"

### Inger — 2012-05-25T05:52:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pictures.562/post-27260>

I need to share these pics with you.

The Kruse-protocol works!!! After a few Months of following it, I weigh the same as in the before-pics (taken about 3 years ago, eating a keto-diet, a very healthy kind of keto with organs etc. but having it warm 7/24 and lots of artificial lights..

Before are the left pictures, after, right side.

.

.

I cannnot believe really, that I weigh the same in both pictures, and I am doing no sports and living quite sedentary!

I eat a lot.. I got such a huge appetit from CT! It is better now, but I just ate as much as I wanted when I had the appetit. I and have started to add in carbs a few weeks ago - I eat them as organic or wild fruit like dried Figs, Beries, Mangos.., wild raw honey / honeycomb, occasionally a little vegtables like carrots and cucumber. I eat wild greens/herbs every day now.

My CT is mostly in the river by the house, sometimes I sit in there for 15-20 minutes, maybe 1-2 times a week, and other days I just stay in a few minutes. Every morning I go there for swimming (very shorthly, just a few minutes), and also evening if I feel like it. The water temp is about 52-55 degrees C. right now.

Inger

# Thread: Breastfeeding...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NotAnother30 — 2012-03-31T19:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9286>

I've noticed that I cannot follow the diet to a 'T' due to the fact that I was always hungry especially following a nursing. I have to have at least 2 starches in the day for my body to be happy and to continue breastfeeding. I'm a year into feeding and still have another year to go. I've seen results but would the extra starches effect anything much?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-31T23:20:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9386>

Hi NotAnother30. While I can't say for sure because I stopped breastfeeding my children long before I heard about paleo or Dr Kruse, I can't see any reason why you cannot follow the diet when you are nursing. If you are hungry, add extra fat or protein (or both!) to your diet. That will do far more for hunger, your health (and your baby) than starches will. A lot of people feel more 'happy' with the starches because they are still in old, unhealthy patterns. Once you get them out, the cravings go. That being said, I don't know what exactly you mean when you say 'safe starches.'

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-31T23:35:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9394>

Bfing a 2yo. Went primal 6 mths pp My supply went up when I added more fat/protein

Shoot for the BAB, lunch & dinner. Don't worry about being in keto. Unless that where you want to go

If you don't have any major weight to loose keep your carbs btwn 20-50 g/day but keep them on the low starch side until you hit midsummer

Right now you're still a sugar burner which is why you feel better w/the starches. Try adding 1TBs of coconut oil to every meal -you may have to work up to that.

Once you get used to eating enough fat at your meals you won't have the hunger issues after each nursing session

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-31T23:56:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9402>

Good advice about the CO, shijin, if she's not already doing that.

I've been nursing for 14 years continuously - yes, continuously! - and am right this very minute nursing a two year old through her hour-long nap while I catch up on the computer work. I eat only non-starchy veggies that aren't FODMAPs or nightshades, and usualy am below 15g carbs/day this time of year. I'm not having, nor have ever had the problem you're experiencing, so I'm not sure exactly what is going on, but I just wanted to toss out that it's not a normal experience for everyone.

### NotAnother30 — 2012-03-31T23:59:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9407>

Thanks for the responses. I should have said safe starches twice a day, which are usually sweet potatoes or plaintains on a very active day and one on non busy days. I will try to increase coconut oil at every meal. I can't tolerate too much animal fats, like ribs and pork...yuck they make me sick. I've been some what paleo since october when diagnoses with a wheat allergy and was fine before. All of this started when I decreased the nursing. And before I forget, I live in Miami...could that be an issue also?

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T00:07:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9412>

I don't know if you do grassfed meats, but the fat on them is worlds different from factory farmed meats. The fat on a nice pastured pork shoulder turned into cueritos, or on a huge slab of beef ribs smoked by my husband ... OH YUM!!!

### NotAnother30 — 2012-04-01T02:58:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9609>

Come to think of it, could the rest start of my menstrual cycle cause of all of this since the onset was so sudden?

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-01T04:29:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9646>

Fwiw there ate no safe starches, unless you are truly eating seasonally & locally.

Right now for me the only thing locally is greens. I actually have out of control collards, kale & broccoli leaves in our garden that we planted last fall. Those are my carbs + broccoli, cauliflower & cabbage

As for your totm returning I was 1mth into the LR when mine came back (15mths pp). So yeah w/totm returning you can see a dip in supply and again when ovulating

As far as the hunger. I'm guessing your not eating enough protein/fat at meals

Ca you give us a sample day of what your eating

This am for bkfst I had 2egg omlette cooked in 2T of Kerry gold ghee & 4 pieces of bacon + bullet proof coffee. Lunch was another 4 pieces of bacon & dinner was a 16oz steak & greens

I'm post reset but it took me a whole 6 mths to get to this point.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-01T04:43:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9656>

I'm breastfeeding my 10 month olds and haven't had any problems being on a ketogenic diet. I have been on a ketogenic diet for a number of months, but just started the leptin Rx about about 5ish weeks ago. Like others said just make sure to have plenty of food at breakfast lunch and dinner. It's been hard to not snack, because I was used to doing it, but not impossible if you plan on big meals!

Unfortunately, I haven't been able to lose ANY weight despite the ketogenic diet, but I've finally stopped gaining weight. With each of my children I've breastfed I've put on weight. Very frustrating! Hoping once my period returns, maybe that will help my hormones normalize and I'll start to drop pounds? Who knows.

Good luck!

### NotAnother30 — 2012-04-01T22:31:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-9932>

I'm thinking its the whole change but on a typical day:

Breakfast 3 eggs scrambled with thin cut steaks cooked in coconut oil, multivitamin, cod liver oil

Lunch: 2 turkey burgers, asparagus

Dinner: Baked chicken with roasted carrots, cauliflower, broccoli

Yesterday and Friday:

Breakfast: Same

Between breakfast and lunch the shakes start (banana) in fears of hypoglycemia

Lunch: baked chicken, roasted sweet potatoes

Dinner:2 turkey burgers, 2 sunny side up eggs, asparagus

I'm not a diabetic but I know the s/s of it. I'm thinking it's hormonal changes.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-02T02:34:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-10051>

Ooo, wow, seriously I'd be very hungry with that little fat, too. I'm to the point now where if I eat poultry, I have to make a special attempt to get more fat in, and I mean animal fat or maybe coconut oil.

More fat! I bet it will do wonders for your hunger \*and\* your hormones - they're made out of cholesterol.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-02T15:45:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-10277>

Concur w/MamaG

NOT ENOUGH FAT! At bkfst I'm eating btwn 4-6 tbs of ghee and 2-3TBS of coconut oil! when I count my bpcoffee

DH loves chicken... but Now I'm like meh?? chicken... shit I'm gonna be hungry after eating that foul bird! And I usually will make some BPTea to have w/dinner to ensure I have enough fat!

I'd prefer to have beef and pork just for the fat! my fatrotein ratio is probably something like 60:35, w/the last 5 allocated to veggies when I feel like them...which is usually lunch or dinner...

up your fat....take some coconut oil, add some 100% dark chocolate and melt it over a double boiler and start having that at the end of each meal! it will make it easier to consume more fat...and you'll be satiated; and when your satiated - your body will go ok- not starving... I can start using any excess for milk production and you'll be surprise how much your milk increases...

### NotAnother30 — 2012-04-02T19:07:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-10372>

YAY! You guys were right! At 10:45 when my daily morning shakes began, went for last night's chicken and within a minute, the shakes had shimmied away. I'm totally going to increase the more fat factor. I was thinking I was eating enough already and I won't let the fact that sweet potatoes are $0.49/lb this week an excuse for buying some more. I'll let you guys know how it goes :-D

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-02T19:11:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breastfeeding.563/post-10374>

glad to see you figured it out... Fat + protein in the right quantities = milk; happy mama and happy baby! I wish I had know about paleo eating w/my dd (she wasn't able to latch/nurse) - maybe I could have pumped enough to meet her needs and we wouldn't have had to supplement w/her - of course if that had happened...ds might not be here now!

# Thread: Excess Testosterone for a Man? Labs help!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-testosterone-for-a-man-labs-help.565/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-31T20:04:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-testosterone-for-a-man-labs-help.565/post-9293>

Just got the BFs labs back and need a little help. Can anyone confirm if this is too much testosterone? All the others sex steroid hormones look low ..... so where is that high testosterone coming from?

Anyone can hack away on anything on these labs. All comments and contributions are welcome.

46 yr old male

No meds

6'1"

160#

SAD diet most of his life. Completely organic for last 10 yrs. Grass-fed meat, gluten free for a year. Grain free since Jan 1. Leptin Rx for 2 months due to high rT3 (low ratio).

Cholesterol 260 (100-199)

Triglycerides 41 (0-149)

HDL 90 (>39)

VLDL Calc 8 (5-40)

LDL Calc 162 (0-99)

These ranges are from LabCorp

Pregnenolone 113 (180-200)

DHEAs 230 (500-640)

Progesterone apparently wasn't measured

Testosterone 848 (650-827)

Testosterone, Free 14 (6.8-21.5)

Estradiol, sensitive 15 (20)

Prior to these numbers his thyroid borders on hyper. Graves disease runs in his family. Son has Graves.

CRPhs .33 (.0-3.0)

IGF-1 157 (94-252)

PSA .9 (3-70)

Iron, serum 106 (40-155)

Iron Saturation 46% (15-55)

TBIC 229 (250-450)

Ferritin 283 (30-400)

HA1c 5.4 (4.8-5.6)

Fasting Insulin 6.2 (2.6-24.9)

2 Hour postprandial Insulin 5.8 (2.6-24.9)

LH 7.2 (1.7-8.6)

FSH 5.8 (1.5-12.4)

GGT 14 (0-65)

Thank you!!!

# Thread: Did I fall off the LR?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ginastarke — 2012-03-31T20:08:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-9295>

I was on a mini vacation last weekend and as far as I knew, stayed on the LR, but I was relying on hotel food ( boil in bag scrambled eggs, sausage and bacon) coconut manna, and cocoa nibs. The morning after getting home I tested myself for ketosis (i know he says the sticks are meaningless, but I had them around and test out of curiosity) no ketosis. and I'd put on 3 pounds that took me all week to lose.

So my question is - Did I fall off? Do I need to start another 8 weeks?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-31T22:37:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-i-fall-off-the-lr.566/post-9363>

So whether you 'fell off' or not (though it doesn't sounds like it - keto sticks stop showing ketosis after a while, even when you are ketogenic), keep on going. Weight can definitely fluctuate...it's a diet for healing.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-31T23:14:05+0300

(believe me, I'm the expert in literally seeing how many days/weeks can be lost by going off-track, but it's usually sweet/wheat foods, not the oils, that really kill this particular form of progress. The oils do make me bloat and feel gross, though.)

# Thread: Cold mice affecting drug test results?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-mice-affecting-drug-test-results.575/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SimonM — 2012-04-01T03:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-mice-affecting-drug-test-results.575/post-9613>

Holy cow! "They" are finally noticing what Jack has been saying....cold biochemistry is different from heat biochem...

Comfy Mice Lead to Better Science: Are Cold Mice Affecting Drug Testing?

ScienceDaily (Mar. 30, 2012) â€” Nine out of 10 drugs successfully tested in mice and other animal models ultimately fail to work in people, and one reason may be traced back to a common fact of life for laboratory mice: **they're cold,** according to a researcher at the Stanford University School of Medicine.

Laboratory mice, who account for the vast majority of animal research subjects, are routinely housed in chilly conditions, which may affect their well-being as well as the outcome of research studies, said Joseph Garner, PhD, associate professor of comparative medicine.

"If you want to design a drug that will help a patient in the hospital, you cannot reasonably do that in animals that are cold-stressed and are compensating with an elevated metabolic rate," Garner said. "This will change all aspects of their physiology -- such as how fast the liver breaks down a drug -- which can't help but increase the chance that a drug will behave differently in mice and in humans."

In a new study, Garner and his colleagues report finding an easy solution to the problem: Simply provide the animals with the proper materials, and they'll build a cozy nest that allows them to naturally regulate their temperatures to a comfortable level. These thermally content mice would be more physiologically comparable to humans and thus might serve as more meaningful research subjects, Garner said.

"Why not let them do what they do in the wild, which is build nests? Mice can happily infest a meat freezer, with temperatures far below zero, but they survive and breed because they build these wonderful nests," he said.

The study, part of nearly seven years of work with mouse nesting behavior, is the first to "ask" mice to rate the value of nesting material in terms of temperature savings, which is an important first step in setting standards for nesting material, said Garner, whose work has focused on the well-being of the mouse. He is the senior author of the study, which was published online March 30 in PLoS ONE.

Mice, which Garner calls "one of the most fantastic animals on Earth," have evolved in the same environment as humans for thousands of years, making them remarkably adaptable, able to live virtually anywhere. For that reason, they make excellent research subjects, with hundreds of millions of them populating laboratories around the world.

Given the option, mice gravitate to temperatures of between 30 and 32 degrees Celsius (the equivalent of about 86 and 90 degrees Fahrenheit). But based on federal regulations, U.S. research laboratories are routinely kept on the cold side -- between 20 to 24 degrees C. There can be advantages to these cold temperatures. For instance, mice have aggressive tendencies that are suppressed in cooler climes. Female mice also lactate better in cooler temperatures, though their pups don't do as well in the cold.

When kept in temperatures toward the low end of this scale -- between about 18 and 20 degrees C (64-68 degrees F) -- the mice begin to show changes in immune function and their growth may be retarded. "So we're housing them right at that threshold," Garner said. "That means the mice may be compromised physiologically, potentially affecting research results."

Simply raising the temperature in the lab isn't an option, not least because the mice would then become unmanageably aggressive, he said. Rather, Garner and his colleagues looked to other options in their study, which involved 36 male and 36 female mice of three common strains. The researchers created sets of two cages linked by a small tube so the mice could move between them. One cage in each set was maintained at a chilly 20 degrees C (68 degrees F) and was equipped with varying quantities of shredded paper, which the animals could use to construct nests for shelter and warmth. The other cage was kept at one of six temperatures: 20, 23, 26, 29, 32 or 35 degrees C (68, 73, 79, 84, 90 or 95 F), but without nesting material.

The mice then had the choice of staying put and tolerating the cold, choosing a balmier cage, eating more to add fat and elevate metabolic rate, or building a nest.

Each strain and sex had slightly different preferences, the researchers found. None was content to simply sit out the cold, either moving to a toastier location, if available, or building elaborate, dome-like nests to warm themselves. The more nest-building material they had, the more they were willing to settle for a cooler clime, as the nests served to temper the chill, the researchers found.

In fact, the nest-building drive was so strong that the mice often would spend hours collecting strands of paper, bit by bit, from the chilliest cage and then transporting it to a more comfortable spot in another cage to build a sturdy little home.

Garner said these mice decided they wanted to have it all, choosing a warm spot and building a nest as well. "Naughty little rascals" is how he described them. "They would go on holiday somewhere warm AND take their nest with them," he said. "Some people like to take a pillow on holiday and some don't. These mice were packing their own pillow."

The fact that some mice moved nesting material to the warmer cage means that the nests serve a function beyond warmth, argued Garner, perhaps providing physical comfort, or a form of protection that decreases the animals' anxiety and stress levels.

The nest-building mice tended to eat less, as they didn't need the extra calories to satisfy their higher metabolic demands, the researchers found. In general, the females preferred warmer temperatures than the males -- by about 5 degrees: they are smaller and have less fat to generate heat.

The researchers concluded that the mice could manage with 6 grams of nesting material but sometimes could use as much as 10 grams, suggesting the larger amount be supplied routinely in research labs.

Another benefit of the nests is that they facilitate researchers' work with the mice -- it's easier to pick them up as well as observe them. "The shape of the nest tells an experienced person whether the animals are too hot or too cold, whether they are sick or whether they are about to give birth," Garner said. "Once you learn how to 'speak mouse nest,' the nest is a wonderful tool that anyone can use to assess the general state of the mouse."

The study's first author is Brianna Gaskill, PhD, at the Charles River Laboratories. Garner and Gaskill collaborated with colleagues at the University of Calgary, Purdue University and the Environmental Protection Agency. The study was supported by a grant from the Universities Federation for Animal Welfare, the Charles River Laboratories (which donated the mice used in the study) and Fiber Core, which donated the nesting material.

Story Source: the above story is reprinted from materials provided by Stanford University Medical Center. The original article was written by Ruthann Richter.

Journal Reference: Brianna N. Gaskill et al. Heat or Insulation: Behavioral Titration of Mouse Preference for Warmth or Access to a Nest. PLoS ONE, 2012; 7 (3): e32799 DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0032799

Source: [sciencedaily.com](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/03/120330205921.htm)

### rocketmom — 2012-04-03T19:57:11+0300

I did my first CT last night. Ugh. I hated the face dunking. I did it twice and only lasted about a minute! I kept the water cold and tried again. Same thing I did two dunks for about a minute each. I iced my upper back as I am developing that dreaded dowager's hump. That was fine altho I was uncomfortable. I will try the face dunking again. I did have nice rosy cheeks, nose and chin though!

I stayed the same as far as weight goes this am. I have a real challenge this evening. I am attending a going away party which is a potluck. I am bringing a veggie tray. I will eat before I leave the house and make myself a fresh lemonade with stevia. That should hold me til I am able to make an appearance and slip away...

dennise

For the past week I've been having an eye twitch that is driving me bonkers. It's been off and on in just my right eye. I have been taking my magnesium every night, so I don't think it's a mag deficiency. Any other thoughts? Could it be from the CT? Missing another mineral? HELP!

Hi! This is my first post, although I've been a lurker on the MDA thread and then here since January. When I first started the reset and CT I had twitching around both eyes -- at times a LOT -- for about three or four weeks. And then it suddenly went away. I think it may have been a form of detox.

Jenny

### TheKid — 2012-04-01T14:51:09+0300

Somewhere before doing the Leptin Rx and CT, the twitching stopped. So I attributed that to just over improvement in health. I do know that sleep can be a factor, and I do focus on getting enough sleep. So you might look at how you're sleeping.

### shilohman — 2012-04-01T23:16:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-twitch.577/post-9949>

> **Trisha;9828 wrote:** Doing the Leptin Rx. Eating lots of grass fed beef, lots of eggs, some pork, a bit of chicken. Veggies mainly consist of lettuces and spinach. Occasionally I have cucumbers, avacado, cabbage, and broccoli. I use coconut oil, olive oil (for salad dressings), the drippings from bacon, and butter.
>
>
>
> Supplements/meds - Multivitamin, K2, B complex, pqq, fish oil, krill oil, bitter melon, magnesium, D3, iodine, armour thyroid
>
>
>
> I've been doing CT for about a month and progressing along well.
>
>
>
> No testing.

Trisha, if you can, get your omega 6/3 tested. Why? Because you probably have an high 6/3 ratio (we all do considering what we have eaten in the past). Even if you can not get tested, just assume that yours is very high. Why? Because of what you have eaten in the past and some of what you are eating now.

1\. eggs. Are these from free range fed chickens? If not, then the eggs are loaded with high omega 6 because they are fed soy or corn feed. You gotta have free range.

2\. pork. Is it grass fed? If not, then it is very high in omega 6. Cut the pork.

3\. chicken. Very high in omega 6, cut the chicken.

4\. lettuces, probably O.K. except iceberg lettuce. Iceberg has high preclorate (spelling?) which is a toxin. No iceberg.

5\. Krill oil & Fish oil? Go read Dr. K's blog on fish oil and do a search for what he says on krill oil. Actually, I just remembered what he said about krill. Krill oil is the #1 Rx that he prescribes at his office. My mind is fuzzy right now, but I thing the omega 3 in the Krill oil is in a phospholipid (spelling?) while the fish oil omega 3 is in triglyceride form.

The body has more uses for omega 3 from krill than fish oil.

You really need to get tested of omega 6/3, because you do not want to have a higher 3 than 6, because then that causes other problems. But for now, Its probably safe to assume you are high in 6. So, eventually, you will want to stop the Krill and get your omega 6 and 3s from your diet.

So, yes the eye twitch is probably from your CT detox and you are releasing a lot of estrogen and omega 6 into your body. Dr. K recommends a lot of mushrooms, which are high in B vitamins and counteract the estrogen. I eat a lot of mushrooms and take extra B complex.

Dr. K, says that excess omega 6 can cause nerve problems, which I am generalizing to any eye twitch.

Get your omega 6/3 in balance ( 4/1 or better) and get tested at sometime.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T06:07:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-twitch.577/post-10173>

shilohman - thanks for the input!

The eggs are free range eggs. I get them from a couple of local farms in the area, it's nice that it's one thing I don't have to pay much more than eggs from the store!

The pork is just from the store. We recently purchased part of a grass fed cow and are hoping in another month or so to do the same with a pig. Probably will just cut it out until we can buy our own pig.

The chicken we eat is very minimal, but I'll try to just cut it out for now.

I never buy iceburg - yuck!

Now I'm wondering if another thing I've been experiencing is also a nerve thing. I've just the last few days been having trouble with my wrists when I CT. They are fine after they get warmed up, but when I move my wrists in the water and as soon as I get out it almost feels like electricity shooting up my arm. It also causes me a lot of pain in my wrists when I move my fingers in the water. It's incredibly bizzare. I thought maybe it was just my wrists/arms needing to get used to the water, but it also strikes me as odd that it's just recently started happening and I've been doing CT fully submerged for a while now.

I'll be requesting my omega 6/3 checked next month when I'm at my doctor. It will only be the second time I see him, but he struck me as a very progressive thinker and willing to work with me when I saw him for the first time!

# Thread: Children  the Leptin Reset
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T19:15:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-9834>

So this is a thread for questions and experiences with kids doing the leptin reset. I don't know of any doing it! I have lots of questions about it, like

\- How young can you become leptin resistant? Are some born that way?

\- How young is it practical to start the leptin reset? Not for newborns, obviously, but what about 5yo's? Better guideline?

\- Should the protein amount be tweaked? I know that the 50g worked for 15yo girls, but what about pre-pubescent children or even toddlers, with smaller bodies?

\- Are the signs of LR different for children? I have one who doesn't have a fat belly, but he's always borderline on the BMI-for-age charts, and very fluffy overall. 10lb at birth (I wasn't GD, but that alone seems a miracle). Is physical size the only factor?

\- Will the leptin reset benefit other children (like my paleo 13yo with occasional headaches, body pains, & usual low energy)? Or should they do the "regular program" like non-LR adults? (I'll start a separate thread for that.)

\- Are the meal timing rules (not just no snacking, but distance between sleep & meals) the same?

I know I need to work much harder on meal timing for my children, which is a challenge to get them finished eating 3.5hrs before bed, since some of them sleep so much!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T19:17:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-9837>

Here's how I pulled details from the LR for my children:

The rule for my kids is that they all have to have protein that is at least 1/6 of their body weight (I started this rule when I did Radiant Recovery) for each meal. Sounds picky, but what it really means is that all have to have 1 (or 2, for the big kids) eggs (or equivalent in meat) for each meal, including breakfast, which is the one they most resist. (And since I started the LR, I moved them all up to 2 (or 3) eggs/oz-meat.

They may not ever skip breakfast, but they may skip lunch (but will not get a late afternoon snack just b/c they're hungry if they do).

What I've seen is similar to what I saw in me adding the BAB - the little ones who were melting down each afternoon stopped as soon as I required them to have an egg each morning. Just like that. Cravings started to diminish.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-02T14:16:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-10257>

I use the 2-3 oz rule for protein at each meal. 3 oz for the 9yr old (severely underweight at 55lbs) and 2 oz for the 6 yr old (47lbs). The do eat this at breakfast and about the same at lunch at school. Sometimes the lunch comes home unfinished. They are ready to eat dinner at 5. Regrettably dad doesn't get home 'til 6. So I do indulge them with a snack as for the most part it is an appetizer they are snibbling off the food we are preparing. With late afternoon snack/dinner, I am using a time zone approach. Dinner is just a leisurely 2 hours from about 5-7 where they are eating, getting homework done, helping make the dinner and eating it. There may also be some playtime in there. I just don't stress that it isn't regimented at the end of the day. Too much insanity happens between 3-7 pm in most American households and I strongly believe that one of the keys to health is eating real food, in your own home, staying connected to the people you love.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-02T15:35:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-10274>

excellent Thread Mama G!

W/the kids They have to have a protein at bkfst... sometimes this is hard b/c DH still hasn't given up cereal.. (ughh - he won't read the literature, and thinks his diet is fine and that wheat is good for you!)

every morning the kids have eggs for bkfst - dd usually eats one, ds will eat 1-2 depending how hungry he is... sometimes my HAB becomes a SAB (Small Ass Breakfast) b/c he'll polish off 2 eggs + 1/2 my breakfast!

I'm considering getting the kids food allergies tested (IgA and all) via blood work to see what they really are allergic too... I was looking at DD's tongue and I think she might have some candida over growth (DH as well) but the challenge is getting DH/MIL/my parents & DCP on board to limit her sweets....

some days I feel like I'm fighting a loosing battle - and I just want to throw all the crap (DH has) in the house.... grrrrr - it was nice these past 40 days not fighting him regarding giving the kids wheat - b/c he wasn't eating it...

### JasonLC — 2012-10-03T15:50:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-the-leptin-reset.582/post-56051>

[](http://http://vfl.ru/fotos/b98c122e999589.html)

Application letter of the parents

When you hear about someone's illness, in private, you hope that the trouble will avoid you and your relatives. Alas, fate was not kind to our family. It suddenly hit someone, who has not even had time to understand life, our son.

The christen-name, we made meaningfully, because the child was long-awaited, and, of course we predicted him a bright and happy future. Say more, and now we are sure of it, in spite of the terrible diagnosis of doctors to Yaroslav.

Left adrenal neuroblastoma with para-aortic lymph nodes lesions, multiple lesions of bones, N-myc amplification, 1r36 - positive ... It is difficult to express in words the horror, which we experienced after learning about all this.

From the date of diagnosis, our little son was carefully examined in the Cancer Center, was conducted an operation on tumor elimination, followed by six courses of chemotherapy and transplantation of bone marrow stem cells. But fate is still unrelenting. Short remission was replaced by redevelopment of tumor. After the first surgery was the second one. However, as the next chemotherapy.

Unfortunately, our medicine is unable to overcome this disease, because the tumor affected the skeletal system. And we can not see all hardships of our little son, and therefore are looking for funds to conduct an extra examination and treatment in Germany. This is the only chance for Yaroslavushka to survive.

It is a vast sum of money, even for wealthy people, which we are not. And so we can do nothing except relying only on the compassion and generosity of everyone who knows our history. Our child is weakening day by day, but he is one head elder than his coevals, because he understands that he must fight for every moment of his life.

We do not just ask, we entreat – offer us a helping hand! Support our young and very friendly family, do not let us lose the only chance to save our little son! Each of your Every dollar may play a decisive part in the struggle for life of Yaroslavushka!

Sincerely Yours,Natalia and Vasilij Javorskie

In his two years small Yaroslav has experienced much. The child has the most insidious form of cancer - left adrenal neuroblastoma with para-aortic lymph nodes lesions, multiple lesions of bones, N-myc positive and a lot of obscure medical terms. Insidious, because very often children get in the hospital only at the III or IV stages. This disease affects only children, the most dangerous age - up to 5 years. But about namely this horrible disease is said: "Childhood Cancer is curable!"

In the case of Yaroslav, unfortunately, short remission was replaced by redevelopment of tumor. At this time doctors had predicted two variants of the situation development. The first - the child recovers from next chemotherapy, and he will need only a special course of MIBG therapy. The second - the bone marrow of the boy will have to be transplanted, with all attendant procedures and preparations for surgery. A visit to a German hospital is necessary in both cases, which means a corresponding amount to pay for treatment. The only difference is that in the first case – it is $30k, in the second case –it is $50k. The miracle did not happen and at this time – it is necessary to transplant the marrow bone….

Friends, Yaroslav's life and his chance of recovery are in our hands. We ask everyone very much, who reads these lines, not to remain indifferent to the fate of the boy and to help him to win the disease.

[More information...](http://http://igg.me/p/246090?a=848746)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Primal diet for the non-LR children
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T19:21:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-9839>

This thread is a place to discuss feeding (& hacking) the non-leptin resistant children. So maybe they aren't fat & lethargic, maybe they were the oldest and you were in great shape when you had them, maybe they were LR & now you fixed them.

How are we feeding them? Tips, questions, & experiences welcome, especially from the doctor himself!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-01T19:31:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-9852>

Some of our journey ...

I have weaned my children from a CW Healthy (high whole grains, low sugar, low fat) diet over five years. I wouldn't recommend going \*that\* slowly, but slow has certainly helped.

\- First I had my oldest go through the pantry hunting for anything with HFCS, BHT, TBHQ, artificial colors, artificial flavors, or artificial sweeteners. We purged them, Trader Joe's opened, and we easily found non-chemically substitutes for everything.

\- Next we switched from lowfat dairy & whole grain cereal to raw whole dairy & home-cooked soaked oats & sourdough bread.

\- Then we kept increasing fat & protein, while lowering even "healthy" sweets & hidden sugars. Officially "WAPF" in our outlook at this time, we added fermented CLO, fermented veggies, & bone broths (when I remembered).

\- We kept decreasing processed foods while out of the house (none in by now), and I started decreasing grains for us. I instituted a "two servings per day of whole grains" - no more. In reality, it was usually less at home, and no more even on vacation. They learned how to use their quota well.

\- I made a rule that every child had to have 1 (or 2, for the big kids) egg or 1oz of meat per meal, including (and especially) breakfast. This made a big difference in meltdowns and a good difference in cravings.

\- We started GAPS. I made the big kids investigators and spies (spying out symptom changes in themselves and each other). They went along well for the eight months we did it. By then, they were used to life without even potatoes or rice, and were thrilled when I returned to allowing them to have two servings of grains ON SPECIAL OCCASIONS OUT OF THE HOUSE (and including dessert). It was great for making them think that was a privilege, lol. GAPS made tremendous gains in my children's borderline ODD/SID, tics, etc.

\- When I started the LR for myself, I increased their protein to 2 (or 3) eggs or oz of meat per meal (this is so easy to eyeball - it's like two slices of applegate farms deli roast beef), and started cooking sausage every day, which they naturally had and loved (but they weren't allowed to skip the eggs for bkfst).

My next step is going to have to be removing wheat altogether. Now that I see how bad my ggut is from wheat (from my labs), and they have been w/o wheat for months at a time, it's clear that it's related to their moods, accidents, tics, BMs, everything. I'm going to test a few to make it easier for the grandparents to comply with adn get it going.

They also get 1/2 - 1-1/2 fruits per day (whole). This I plan to move even more seasonal. I'm also going to stop forcing them to eat veggies in the winter, IF they eat plenty of fat & offal & get cold. They love going lightly clothed out into cold weather, anyway, so they should be good on the last one.

They aren't keto, but have no grains, and starches are rare. Their last well visit did show ketones in some of their urine, so even what we're doing is getting them there. (Dr. was displeased, I'll be sure and stuff them with sweet potatoes the day before they go again, lol.)

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-02T18:24:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-10342>

> **MamaGrok;9901 wrote:** This thread is a place to discuss feeding (& hacking) the non-leptin resistant children. So maybe they aren't fat & lethargic, maybe they were the oldest and you were in great shape when you had them, maybe they were LR & now you fixed them.
>
>
>
> How are we feeding them? Tips, questions, & experiences welcome, especially from the doctor himself!

Hi MamaGrok,

Thank you for sharing your family's process. Here is what we have been doing:

1\. I started the SCD/GAPS/modified paleo diet over six years ago. I did so well on the diet that after nearly a year on the diet I put my youngest daughter on the diet too. She had serious asthma and had been on medication since infancy. The rest of the family followed mostly for the convenience of the parents. My youngest daughter's asthma, hallucinations and behavioural problems resolved very quickly on the diet.

2\. I learned about the Weston A Price Foundation and incorporated all of their nourishing food preparation methods. Of course, I avoided all the foods restricted on the SCD/GAPS.

3\. The promise of the SCD/GAPS is that the gut heals and people can go back to eating some of the restricted foods. Over the last two years, we have tried to reintroduce foods, with very limited success. I used the WAPF methods of food preparation. It did not work for me but some foods my daughter could eat. The only food that was reintroduced successfully was raw dairy products. This is were I started to jump from the SCD/GAPS camp to the modified paleo camp. Except for the "safe" starches, which we don't eat, the modified paleo diet (Swiss paleo) works for most members of my family.

4\. Eating out of the house was always a problem. I have found the greater society to be the biggest hurtle. Now that my youngest daughter is nine she has enough self-control not to eat foods off her diet. She feels better and doesn't like having the spiders crawling all over her ceiling at night. We don't like living with her frightful visions either.

5\. I have noticed a split in my family. My husband and eldest daughter need more carbohydrates and my youngest daughter and me need very little carbohydrate. My eldest daughter really doesn't like vegetables and will slip into ketosis very easily. She is very healthy; her only "problem" is a vasovagal syncope, which just means she is prone to fainting. We give her brown rice to eat to keep her out of ketosis, which seems to make the vasovagal problem worst. My husband also eats brown rice when he is very active.

6\. Here we are at the presence. I think a seasonal ketogenic diet, CT, and balancing circadian cycles will work well for me. Children are growing so fast, I feel most comfortable letting them choose from an assortment of quality whole foods. I would not actively mess with their macro-nutrient mix. Getting the lights out at night makes perfect sense to me and I think it will be good for everyone. CT is also just for me. My children and husband can watch me and see how the experiment goes.

The biggest help for me has been careful observation and documentation of my child's process. I like the term, biohacking. My youngest daughter has early warning signs that something is not right with her. I have learned to be very observant of any of these changes. Every child is different and the parents of a sick child will have to observe their child carefully to identify these early signs and learn to take quick action. I am grateful I started young with my daughter. I think this process becomes more difficult with young adults (preteens and teenagers).

### tweety — 2012-04-06T03:15:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-12022>

We went from eating a "healthy, whole grain, low fat, low meat" diet to a WAPF diet over a few months. Once I found raw milk and tasted it for the first time I was all in, and so was my family! But then vanity (weight gain) led me to paleo, and then to all the things I have learned since regarding gluten. Over the last 18 mos I have slowly switched everyone over.

First we allowed the kids to have grains or junk items only when out and with friends. Then I became more uncomfortable with that, and started sending them with their own treats that I made. Then I found Leptin Rx, and slowly my husband converted too.

The kids still eat starches, and occasionally corn. And yes they eat fruit. Some days I can make it all three meals with just meat, fat, and veggies. But, some days I get flustered and don't know what to feed them or don't have enough time to prepare it. My oldest, almost 9, eats as much as my husband and I do, which is alot! And my youngest is not far behind. Its hard to feed the family on a reasonable budget because its like feeding four adults! So, we fall back on starches to help.

Its something I hope to work on this year. Seasonal eating makes all the sense in the world to me. We already belong to a couple of CSAs, and get other things from a farmer. So I am working on eliminating the grocery store impulse buys (like tomatoes in winter, or bananas and strawberries in winter too.)

As far as LR or not, I don't know. I do think they have candida issues to deal with because I did when I gave birth to them. And they may very well have cortisol or adrenal issues for the same reason. But I am trying to take a life long, gradual approach so that to them its just the way to live. I hope they don't need intervention like I did!

### BJK77 — 2012-04-06T15:51:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-12094>

I'm fairly happy with my children's diet at this stage. We've finally reached a point where I feel like I'm getting good, nutrient dense foods in them. The only obstacle we really struggle with is not being able to control what they have when they're with friends/family. The truly sad part is that usually my kids will try to avoid the "bad" foods, but at 7 and 11 years old, you're kind of at the mercy of the adult! They can't exactly go out and buy their own food.

At least my family has gotten somewhat better and understands that my son absolutely cannot have gluten. We've never confirmed if it's true celiac or just an intolerance, but he definitely is taking after me with the gluten issue.

Their typical diet is something like this:

\- Breakfast: My 7-year old is an awesome BF eater. He'll usually eat 3-4 pieces of sausage and a 1-egg cheese omelet or sometimes a pumpkin bar (mostly egg/pumpkin and a tiny bit of almond flour). My 11-year old is working on increasing her BF, but just doesn't have an appetite yet for it. She generally eats 1 pc of sausage with an avocado and an egg yolk - she hates the whites and since they really aren't the nutritional powerhouse of the egg anyway, I don't push it.

\- Lunch: They both do well without snacking and have decent lunches. We'll do meatballs, salmon patties, salads topped with chicken, burgers, etc when we're at home. On school days they pack bologna and cheese rollups, an apple, jerky, pumpkin bars, homemade yogurt, etc. Not the best, but a definite improvement over just a year ago. If they have an apple with their lunch, this is the only fruit they get throughout the day.

\- Dinner: There is no exception here for a "kids" meal. They eat what is made for dinner - always a full paleo meal. I will sometimes make smoothies for "dessert" - raw egg yolks, raw milk or coconut milk, a little kale, maybe an avocado and some berries. Sometimes I'll just do an avocado, coconut milk and some cocoa powder for a chocolate shake. They also eat 85% dark chocolate as a "treat/dessert." We started out at 55% and just kept working our way up. I'm hoping to eventually get them to 100%!

They have been doing so well with not snacking and finishing dinner 3 hours before bed and I really think it's helping their sleep. My son gets no grains at all, but my daughter will have the occasional piece of pizza or pancake once or twice a month when she has a sleepover at a friend's house.

How we got here:

\- It has been a gradual process over the last couple of years ever since I started my decline in health and simultaneously developed several food intolerances (leaky gut!)

\- I knew from the get-go that I would need the kids' buy-in to improve our diet. It isn't one of those things you can force on them and just hope they accept. They aren't dumb! They can be reasoned with and educated to make the right choices. I made it a priority to involve them in all aspects of our new dietary lifestyle. They read labels with me (my son was too young, but we would point out to him the HUGE words in some of the ingredient lists and explain that these things were not real food, but chemicals). I taught my daughter how to recognize real food from fake, processed, chemical junk. My son would count the # of ingredients in processed things and at first we started out with a rule of not consuming anything with greater than 5 ingredients.

\- Eventually we pretty much cut out ALL processed foods. We started to talk more about some of those 5 or fewer ingredients found in processed foods that can be very damaging to our health even if organic, i.e. corn, canola oil, etc. We started to make more of our own foods (the kids helped) - yogurt, mayonnaise, mustard, etc. I think they felt a sense of pride and started to realize that the only way to know what's in our food is if we make it ourselves.

\- I took them to a local chip/pretzel manufacturer where they toured the production plant to see how weird it was to have food made in a factory! We went home and made our own sweet potato chips and they thought that was fantastic!

\- They help out with our small garden and get to see exactly how food is grown. They're always much more likely to taste and try new foods when they've been involved in the growing process.

\- I've taken them with me many times to the local farms and farmer's markets where we get our meats/fats/dairy products and some produce items. We have conversations with the farmers all the time about their practices and then we talk about factory farms and conventional farming operations so they understand the difference. They get to see how the animals are cared for and treated. They've realized that, as important as this is for their health, there are also a lot of moral, ethical and environmental considerations as well.

\- When they do go with me I always encourage them to pick out something new for us all to try as a family - sometimes it's a fruit or veggie and other times it might be a different cut of meat. They don't always end up liking everything, but it's a great way to make them feel like they do have some control over what they're eating.

\- I still make them "treats" for holidays/parties, but again they help with them and we talk about the substitutions we're making compared to what most people have or what would be found in a grocery store. We use only almond and coconut flours. I won't have any gluten in my house! We use honey as a sweetener and very rarely maple syrup, but never any kind of sugar.

\- We're always working on improving and still have a lot of work to do, but I'm proud of where we're at right now. Definitely better than we were a year ago and even better than 6 months ago . . .

\- They both take their fermented cod liver oil and probiotics every night without complaints anymore! My son loves liver so I try to get that in him 1-2 x/week. He also loves sardines! My daughter isn't quite there yet, so I'll hide liver in meatloaf, chili, etc. I just know one day she'll love it too . . . ok, maybe I'm a bit delusional, but I can hope, can't it?!?!

\- The only area I'm struggling with is broth. Any suggestions on how to get more in them? They just don't like it at all and this is after really trying to get them to drink it for the last year now. I do cook veggies in it and make a lot of soup, so they get it in them that way, but as far as drinking it straight, it just doesn't happen!

This post ended up much longer than I anticipated! Hope someone finds some helpful tips in it and I can use any advice for improvements too!

### Sem — 2012-06-02T21:54:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-29092>

Love this thread. I've been trying hard to improve my children's diet. What a journey. I've cut out gluten by now they just want to eat rice pasta and corn chips. Ugh. The hardest part is dealing with relatives, friends, social and school functions. It's hard to feed them meat and veggies all day.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-02T22:20:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-29096>

> **Sem;29192 wrote:** Love this thread. I've been trying hard to improve my children's diet. What a journey. I've cut out gluten by now they just want to eat rice pasta and corn chips. Ugh. The hardest part is dealing with relatives, friends, social and school functions. It's hard to feed them meat and veggies all day.

oh I hear ya. I find myself avoiding things with food as even a year later - I still get crying and "your so mean mommy" when I don't let him partake in cakes or treats at functions. He's 6, I figure this is the most important time in his development - so I just say, No, mommy's not mean - she just loves you so much.

We've discussed how this is "new" information that other families just don't realize yet. Compare it to the way smoking was back in the day.

I will do rice now and then when eating out - like we go to chipotle and he gets a bowl with rice. He also loves sushi, so I just go with the rice on that. We bake almond flour stuff for treats now and then, and the poor child gets a LOT of sweet potatoes.

But yea, it's H A R D. My worry for awhile was contributing to bad eating behaviors like binging on candy when it's available or when he grows up seeking out junk food cause he never got it as a child. I've come to realize that those kinds of eating disorders are not "given" to someone. Their brain is broken in a particular way for that.

The best I can do is force a healthy body and mind on him now, so hopefully he will be equipped to handle the temptations later on.

### Sem — 2012-06-03T00:20:39+0300
