### MamaGrok — 2012-06-03T01:50:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-29141>

We did GAPS (I dubbed them my detectives & scientists beforehand), so they were thrilled to get anything but soup after the first week, lol. I have a solid rule that everyone must eat protein for every meal. Where "one unit" = 1 egg or 1oz meat, I insisted on 1 unit per meal for the little guys and 2 for the older kids at first, but now they're up to at least 3 units each.

Sausage! Find a pastured pork farmer who is accessible to you - theyr'e not all that rare anymore. Make your own from beef or ground pork using an online recipe (it's just the meat + a few seasonings, no biggie).

Kids can't do their best, can't control their moods, and can't stop sugar cravings without protein to start the day. My rule at first was "protein first" at every meal, and nothing else till they finished it, till they got used to that . Then those who were old enough and/or trustworthy enough were allowed to eat protein along with their other food.

I'm not mean about rules; I just sit down with them when I have a new idea and say, "Hey, guys, I learned something new that should help us all feel better and have more fun - blahbiddyblahblah." That's it; whining changes nothing and I just do what I said I'd do.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-03T02:24:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-29145>

With no grains as an option - there really are no choices round here but protein for bfast - sem, my guy took to sardines and liverwurst right away. I still have to choke em down, but kids seem good with it. He likes to open the can.

Im also a big believer in "if they are hungry enough". When he refuses to eat what I fix, I use it as an opportunity to tell him that it's ok to be hungry - and skipping meals is good for ya now and then. That ties into our transition to eating before 6 p, And nothing after. If he doesnt eat whats served at dinner - he gets the "thats great - its good to be hungry! Ill make you a nice bif bfast tomorrow.

### [email redacted] — 2012-06-03T02:25:50+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-04T06:14:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/primal-diet-for-the-non-lr-children.583/post-29323>

> **Sem;29228 wrote:** Cavemam - I hear you. So hard. My kids have multiple food sensitivities too so I should probably do GAPS with them. Maybe while I have more control over the summer.

+1 to what MamaGrok and Cavemam have said. Also, I find it essential for my children to have good fat (in big quantities) with the protein for breakfast. If your kids can do dairy, I use yogurt for this. Plain, organic yogurt. I melt butter and CO (probably a tablespoon of each, I don't measure) and then split it into 2 cups. When it's cooled a bit (but still melted), I add cinnamon and a small bit of raw honey and mix well. (You can add other things here easily... we've put DE in, as well as l-glutamine and probiotic powders). Then I put in a few heaping tablespoons of yogurt and then mix the hell out of it. It actually whips up into a bit of 'icing' texture but retains the yogurt taste. My kids eat this every morning (with an organic sausage of some sort, typically lamb and rosemary is their fave).

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-02T18:25:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-intense-and-condensed.584/post-10343>

Interesting relationship - but PCOS isn't just about T - its also about Estrogen Dominance. its the estrogen- progesterone mismatch that can drive the T. You have to fix the E balance if you want T to be at the right place. If you look at most women who have PCOS- they have excessive subq abdominal fat, love handles, back fat, face fat all driven by Estrogen Dominance; T is what causes the xcess hair growth; but its the E that's the killer - literally increasing risks of cancer (Breast, ovarian, cervical)

FWIW: my neurologist informed me that b/c I had migraines -the chance of having Bipolar also increased b/c it impacts the same region of the brain (epilepsy is also supposed to originate in the same area as well) - however Dr can validate that.

I've been dx w/Bipolar disorder - I was on depakote, topamax, lithium - all of which I had a negative reaction to... after a sever episode - my Neuro took me off all meds, sent me to a chiropractor and told me to go gluten free - as it was my only options for treating the disease b/c I couldn't take the drugs...

Now is it entirely a possibility that Bipolar disorder (or other such brain diseases) are the result of neolithic thinking? IMO you bet. Is it possible there's a relationship btwn Bipolar and PCOS and possibly just a different manifestation of the disease - sure (But then you need to explain the % of men who are Bipolar)

It would be interesting to see what Lucy's (L8F) take on this is -since this is in her "lane of expertise"

>

>
> Now is it entirely a possibility that Bipolar disorder (or other such brain diseases) are the result of neolithic thinking? IMO you bet. Is it possible there's a relationship btwn Bipolar and PCOS and possibly just a different manifestation of the disease - sure (But then you need to explain the % of men who are Bipolar)
>
>
>
> It would be interesting to see what Lucy's (L8F) take on this is -since this is in her "lane of expertise"

I was doing some research on Bipolar and I found a LOT of people saying that their symptoms became so much less when they got off of gluten. Sometimes Hashimoto's Thyroiditis is mistaken for Bipolar (the hyper swings can mimic manic episodes). What is the biggest trigger for thyroid antibody attacks? Gluten. There is also a huge connection between Hashi's and PCOS: [http://www.ovarian-cysts-pcos.com/news14-pcos-thyroid.html-](http://http://www.ovarian-cysts-pcos.com/news14-pcos-thyroid.html-) so again, a possible underlying gluten connection.

My sis was diagnosed as Bipolar (though I actually doubt she really is and so does she- she was diagnosed in the middle of a very difficult life situation) but she also has Hashi's and hasn't been diagnosed with PCOS but totally fits the classic profile of symptoms.

Now, I'm not saying it's that simple, but I think that there are enough people with the genes that predispose them to gluten sensitivity that we are seeing multiple autoimmune inflammatory reactions in many people and often an overlap of several pathologies...

### chocolate — 2012-04-02T18:52:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-intense-and-condensed.584/post-10363>

My kid was diagnosed bipolar and she has liver problems instead. She has no genetic predisposition against gluten, but she does against the manufacture of fat. If you manufacture fat, you stop making shgb... so anyway... the gluten free was what got us on this track in the first place. She is not bipolar, but she was simply diagnosed based on a family connection.

The estrogen is also from sucrose, fructose, glucose, lactose causing magnesium dump. I think the progesterone (needs magnesium) calms it. I understand that you don't necessarily make more estrogen, it is bound or unbound. If you do the zinc dumping, you can't make testosterone. So really, there are not any populations that would be ok for gluten, I would think. Does anyone do well with hormones flying loose? There is an under 30 great great mother that died of breast cancer in our gene pool.

But I would love for anyone to correct me. I miss steps.

### chocolate — 2012-04-08T05:49:12+0300

[http://women.webmd.com/metformin-glucophage-for-polycystic-ovary-syndrome](http://http://women.webmd.com/metformin-glucophage-for-polycystic-ovary-syndrome)

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCwQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fen.wikipedia.org%2Fwiki%2FCaloric\_restriction\_mimetic&ei=XfyAT5unHcaeiAKAgbm0Aw&usg=AFQjCNE6DiJgOxlbWKoRQtO67ASkmZQQEw&sig2=kBDo0d17TeL9NPEU45iWCw](http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCwQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fen.wikipedia.org%2Fwiki%2FCaloric_restriction_mimetic&ei=XfyAT5unHcaeiAKAgbm0Aw&usg=AFQjCNE6DiJgOxlbWKoRQtO67ASkmZQQEw&sig2=kBDo0d17TeL9NPEU45iWCw) calorie restriction mimicker

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=3&ved=0CDsQFjAC&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.antiaging-magazine.com%2Farticles%2Farticle\_1\_1.html&ei=XfyAT5unHcaeiAKAgbm0Aw&usg=AFQjCNGGi3ahHjy7pdHh8D6vHJi57fAUWA&sig2=rJ4Sz1wnaFAjmZwTaX\_H-g](http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=3&ved=0CDsQFjAC&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.antiaging-magazine.com%2Farticles%2Farticle_1_1.html&ei=XfyAT5unHcaeiAKAgbm0Aw&usg=AFQjCNGGi3ahHjy7pdHh8D6vHJi57fAUWA&sig2=rJ4Sz1wnaFAjmZwTaX_H-g) antiaging

### chocolate — 2012-05-09T05:55:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-intense-and-condensed.584/post-22483>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18804205](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18804205)

Melatonin and the ovary: physiological and pathophysiological implications.

Tamura H, Nakamura Y, Korkmaz A, Manchester LC, Tan DX, Sugino N, Reiter RJ.

Source

Department of Cellular and Structural Biology, University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio, 7703 Floyd Curl Drive, San Antonio, TX 78229-3900, USA.

Abstract

OBJECTIVE:

To summarize the role of melatonin in the physiology and pathophysiology of the ovary.

DESIGN:

Review of literature.

SETTING:

University Health Science Center.

RESULT(S):

Melatonin plays an essential role in the pathogenesis of many reproductive processes. Human preovulatory follicular fluid (FF) contains higher concentrations of melatonin than does plasma, and melatonin receptors are present in ovarian granulosa cells (GC). Melatonin has been shown to have direct effects on ovarian function. Reactive oxygen species and apoptosis are involved in a number of reproductive events including folliculogenesis, follicular atresia, ovulation, oocyte maturation, and corpus luteum (CL) formation. Melatonin and its metabolites are powerful antioxidants; the primitive and primary function of melatonin may be its actions as a receptor-independent free radical scavenger and a broad-spectrum antioxidant. **A large amount of scientific evidence supports a local role of melatonin in the human reproductive processes. The indole also has potential roles in the pathophysiology of endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), and premature ovarian failure (POF).**

CONCLUSION(S):

We summarize the current understanding of melatonin's essential functions in the human ovary. Melatonin could become an important medication for improving ovarian function and oocyte quality, and open new opportunities for the management of several ovarian diseases.

### chocolate — 2012-05-14T01:48:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-intense-and-condensed.584/post-23770>

[http://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1803&context=usdaarsfacpub](http://http://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1803&context=usdaarsfacpub)

cattle study on male hormones during estrous cycle

Androgens appear to be involved in the regulation of follicular growth (Louvet, Harman, Schreiber

& Ross, 1975) and in the regulation of the hypothalmic-pituitary axis (Martensz, Baird, Scaramuzzi

& Van Look, 1976). Short (1962) isolated androgens from the bovine ovary and Shemesh &

Hansel (1974), Kanchev, Dobson, Ward & Fitzpatrick (1976) and Kanchev & Dobson (1976)

noted increases in peripheral testosterone concentrations before oestrus and corpus luteum (CL)

regression, but no relationships were found with androstenedione during the oestrous cycle of the

cow. Peterson, Fairclough & Smith (1978) found no change in testosterone values before CL

regression or at oestrus in the cow. Relationships of dehydroepiandrosterone are totally unknown in

the cow although dehydroepiandrosterone may be important in bovine ovarian steroidogenic

pathways (Lacroix, Eechaute & Leusen, 1974). We have therefore examined the secretion of

androstenedione, testosterone and dehydroepiandrosterone from the ovary of beef cows.

Ovarian vein androgen concentrations

As shown in Text-fig. 1 and Table 1, androgens tended to increase as the CL regressed although

statistical regression analysis of androgen changes over days detected no significant changes. There

were no differences in androgen levels between time periods within days. Within-animal correlaÂ¬

tions between androstenedione and dehydroepiandrosterone (0-26), androstenedione and testosterÂ¬

one (0-24) and testosterone and dehydroepiandrosterone (0-14) were detected (P < 0-05); but there

were no relationships of androgens with ovarian blood flow or day (P > 0T0). **Decreases in ovarian

blood flow were associated with CL regression (from 25 to < 8 ml/min), and limited blood

sampling and androgen analysis from the ovarian vein after regression of the CL.

Ovarian androgens displayed wide fluctuations within day (Text-fig. la). The sampling period

was divided into four intervals (Days —17 to

— 14, —13 to

— 9, —8 to —4 and

—

3 to + 1 for 2

analysis of peaks of secretion. Although the majority of androgen peaks were found during Days

—

8 to

—

4 (1^1 days before CL regression), **2 analysis with orthogonal comparisons failed to detect

significant differences among any of the periods.**

The point, I think is that the hormones stay and the blood flow changes.** Making ovulation not so simple as just the right labtests. An increased blood flow is required. I think.

### chocolate — 2012-05-16T03:23:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-intense-and-condensed.584/post-24463>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/05/120502184833.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/05/120502184833.htm)

ScienceDaily (May 2, 2012) — A paper published May 2 in Biology of Reproduction describes the effects of brief prenatal exposure to plant estrogens on the mouse oviduct, modeling the effects of soy-based baby formula on human infants. The results suggest that exposure to estrogenic chemicals in the womb or during childhood has the potential to affect a woman's fertility as an adult, possibly providing the mechanistic basis for some cases of unexplained female infertility.

> **chocolate;23852 wrote:** [http://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1803&context=usdaarsfacpub](http://http://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1803&context=usdaarsfacpub)
>
>

> cattle study on male hormones during estrous cycle

I do love your contributions Chocolate - but it would be awesome if you could include a quip about relevance and what parts were interesting. Sometimes the title are a little lacking in a hook! heh.

### chocolate — 2012-05-16T03:59:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-intense-and-condensed.584/post-24481>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17490658](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17490658)

Follicular growth and development under continuous gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist administration.

Ovarian follicle and cyst formation have been recognized as an advance phenomenon associated with GnRH agonist administration. **With the use of GnRH antagonists, pituitary suppression is immediate and no flare effect and follicle growth are expected. We describe two patients who developed a dominant follicle and presumably ovulated in response to hCG triggering under continuous sole administration of a GnRH antagonist.

DESIGN:

Case report.

SETTING:

An IVF unit at a university hospital.

PATIENT(S):

Two young healthy female patients undergoing IVF because of male-factor infertility.

INTERVENTION(S):

Continuous daily administration of a GnRH antagonist from menstruation with the aim of achieving ovarian suppression.

MAIN OUTCOME MEASURE(S):

Endocrine and ultrasound characteristics of follicular growth.

RESULT(S):

Both patients developed a dominant follicle under sole administration of a GnRH antagonist, accompanied by a gradual rise in serum estradiol and endometrial thickness which culminated in a spontaneous LH surge. Ovulation was triggered by hCG and mid-luteal progesterone levels were suggestive of ovulation.

CONCLUSION(S):

We describe for the first time the development of a dominant follicle and presumable ovulation under continuous administration of a GnRH antagonist. Serum gonadotropin concentrations indicate that the pituitary failed to suppress in both patients. The exact mechanism of this phenomenon remains to be elucidated.**

The pituitary was bypassed...that is what is significant to me. In the cattle article, the hormones were lying in wait. I accidently omitted the section. I will go back, read it, and frame a (hopefully) intelligible comment.

### chocolate — 2012-05-16T04:07:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-intense-and-condensed.584/post-24487>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17490658](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17490658)

Follicular growth and development under continuous gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist administration.

Ovarian follicle and cyst formation have been recognized as an advance phenomenon associated with GnRH agonist administration. With the use of GnRH antagonists, pituitary suppression is immediate and no flare effect and follicle growth are expected. We describe two patients who developed a dominant follicle and presumably ovulated in response to hCG triggering under continuous sole administration of a GnRH antagonist.

DESIGN:

Case report.

SETTING:

An IVF unit at a university hospital.

PATIENT(S):

Two young healthy female patients undergoing IVF because of male-factor infertility.

INTERVENTION(S):

Continuous daily administration of a GnRH antagonist from menstruation with the aim of achieving ovarian suppression.

MAIN OUTCOME MEASURE(S):

Endocrine and ultrasound characteristics of follicular growth.

RESULT(S):

Both patients developed a dominant follicle under sole administration of a GnRH antagonist, accompanied by a gradual rise in serum estradiol and endometrial thickness which culminated in a spontaneous LH surge. Ovulation was triggered by hCG and mid-luteal progesterone levels were suggestive of ovulation.

CONCLUSION(S):

We describe for the first time the development of a dominant follicle and presumable ovulation under continuous administration of a GnRH antagonist. Serum gonadotropin concentrations indicate that the pituitary failed to suppress in both patients. The exact mechanism of this phenomenon remains to be elucidated.

# Thread: CT and allergies
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-allergies.585/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pardus — 2012-04-01T19:49:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-allergies.585/post-9866>

Hi all,

When I got to the office on friday, one of my co-workers was wearing a really strong perfume which made my nose runny and my eyes water. It was a pretty bad reaction.

I decided to go outside and get some fresh air. It was around 25F, a little windy but not too bad so I went without a jacket and took a walk until my symptoms disappeared, about 30 minutes.

When I came back into the office, I could still smell the strong perfume, but my allergic reaction didn't return!

Anyone else notice their allergies improve with CT? This might be useful with spring and the coming allergy season...

### Lahrie — 2012-04-01T21:08:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-allergies.585/post-9903>

That's great to hear! I was hoping it would have some benefit for allergies too!

I added face dunks back in to my cold baths. It seems to make my sinuses drain more efficiently. For years the cycle has been allergies-plugged sinuses-infection-antibiotics. Repeat for several years. Then, have surgery-nasal spray steroids/allergy Rx-start cycle over again. I do not want to continue that crazy cycle.

Getting my sinuses to drain properly heads off the infection which means the rest does not need to recur. Hopefully, the allergies will disappear.

I still have a difficult time keeping my face in the water. The back of my throat doesn't seal shut when I hold my breath and water still gets in. So, I hold my nose shut with my hand. Hopefully, this doesn't short circuit anything. It's helping me for now though.

### Destiny — 2012-04-01T21:20:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-allergies.585/post-9909>

> **Lahrie;9966 wrote:** That's great to hear! I was hoping it would have some benefit for allergies too!
>
>
>
> I added face dunks back in to my cold baths. It seems to make my sinuses drain more efficiently. For years the cycle has been allergies-plugged sinuses-infection-antibiotics. Repeat for several years. Then, have surgery-nasal spray steroids/allergy Rx-start cycle over again. I do not want to continue that crazy cycle.
>
>
>
> Getting my sinuses to drain properly heads off the infection which means the rest does not need to recur. Hopefully, the allergies will disappear.
>
>
>
> I still have a difficult time keeping my face in the water. The back of my throat doesn't seal shut when I hold my breath and water still gets in. So, I hold my nose shut with my hand. Hopefully, this doesn't short circuit anything. It's helping me for now though.

My husband has been on Claritin D 24 hrs all year round for many years. He also was using a lot of Afrin to clear any extra congestion during the day. Two weeks into doing CT, he stopped using both. I could not believe it!

# Thread: temp of your baths?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-01T23:28:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-9958>

Hi, please let me know how you started your baths and at what temps? what is the lowest temp of a bath?

when you add ice, does it go down to the 40s? and is that desirable? or do we keep the water at 50-55F and no lower?

Today was my first bath at 63F.

### Chrisgraff — 2012-04-01T23:42:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-9969>

1\. Face dunks (50ÂºF) and swimming pool (45m @ 69ÂºF) for a week

2\. Bath, no ice (30m @ 64ÂºF) for a week

3\. Bath (30m @ 60ÂºF) + bag of ice on chest w/T-shirt for three or four days

4\. Bath (30m @ 56ÂºF) + ice on chest (no shirt).

working my way down...

Dr. K made a comment last week that, "50Âº water is perfect". I don't plan on going lower than that.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-02T01:21:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10019>

I get my water down to 54 w/all the ice my fridge can make in a day and two ice blankets, one below and on my back and one on top of me.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T01:33:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10023>

45F from the tap, I add some warm to bring it to 50F, right now, 30 minutes into a CT it is 50.1F but skin is 49F

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-02T01:34:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10024>

got it, so stay above 50F

### mistigi — 2012-04-02T03:03:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10073>

My bath is between 51-55F but it takes around 30lbs of ice to take it there. I use one solid frozen block of ice (4 gal of water) which is not ideal as it does not fully dissolve throughout my bath (60 min).

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-02T03:31:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10083>

I have at times only been able to get my bath down to high 50s when ice has been at a premium in my house (I'm the only one who uses it-hahaha). For me there seems to be a huge difference between 54 and 59. I noticed today that although I was cold I got numb quicker at 54 and the col went away much faster. I didn't shiver at all. I've been doing this for 6 weeks so hopefully most of the shivering will go away by now. So I am going to try to keep it temp below 55 for maximum comfort!

### WestmorelandGrape — 2012-04-02T03:58:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10106>

I am blessed with 52 degree Michigan well water. Works for me without adding any ice. Once the tub is full, I let the faucet trickle to keep the tub full and to keep the water cool. I am up to 45 minutes a day and seeing changes! Good luck, everyone!

### optimeyes — 2012-04-02T04:17:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10129>

I'm in Florida, the water is coming out of the tap at 70-80 degrees and when I add ice (face bath) it melts and gets as cold as 60. LOL!!! Our pool is abt 85 degrees unheated. The CT presents quite a challenge here  I don't care for the face bath, I might just try cold baths and try to work the temp down.

### Mikey7 — 2012-04-02T05:26:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10152>

My trash can? I say been 50s of late since the ambient temp been around that temp, but I still add ice.

### ste colombe — 2012-04-02T05:46:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10160>

Im in Hong Kong so the temperature here is nearly always tropical (its still winter and 26C ....). I fill up huge plastic bottles that I cut open and freeze. I have about 6 of those, the water gets down to 10c and then I have to add a bottle every so often during the bath to maintain the cold. Being a girl, I cover my boobs with my hands as I dont want them to disappear and lay in the bath mainly covered, except for my upper chest, i stay there for about 30 mins, sometimes more depending if I can keep the water tempeature cold. Its going to get worse soon, so I'll be using even more ice. I have always taken cold showers, so I just jumped into the ice bath first off and started at 10 minutes and worked my way up from there....

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T11:19:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10241>

My tap water is around 45 - 50F at the moment - I doubt it will get much warmer! So I don't add ice or anything to it, but still haven't managed to get my skin temperature down below 55.9F. In the depths of winter, my tap water tends to be about 3 - 4C/39F, so I guess I'll have to add some hot water then to bring the temperature up, unless I'm cold adapted by then!

### Qodesh — 2012-04-03T23:02:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10938>

I started with a couple face dunks at what I thought was cold. (70 degrees here in S. Florida.) and cold (same temp) showers.

I spot ice in between.

My baths are about 2-3x /week. started at 70, then got it down to 65 for and hour. My feet took a long time to warm up.

My last bath was 58, feet in for 30 minutes. No prob! Not even the feet. I even felt warm until the final 5 minutes.

I plan to take it down slowly. I'll see if I can get enough ice to bring it down to 55-60 for a couple more times while I bring the time to an hour.

Then down in temp again. 50 for an hour.

This is my plan

### hellojtm — 2012-04-04T00:29:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10998>

my tap water is currently 52-53f. i've added ice to get it down to 48f but doesn't seem worth it to me. although i agree with a previous poster that there may be some benefits to \_even\_ colder if it numbs you quicker

# Thread: Getting senior loved ones to become optimal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-04-02T00:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-9985>

Does anyone have ideas on how to get older folk to drop the margarine and pick up the butter? To lose the flour and sugar?

Many folk have had their gall bladders removed, and so going to a high fat diet, if they were to try it, can be difficult.

### .lina — 2012-04-03T18:39:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-10808>

maaan, do i ever sympathize with this! i have been a living example of how amazing you can

feel with this kind of eating, embracing butter and shunning carbs, and STILL last week, my

dear mother calls me and gleefully says my dad has signed up for a course which entails

him exercising rigorously and drinking nothing but liquids for weeks. he's almost 60, i kind

of fear for his health :/

but any kind of weightloss will be good for him of course... and my parents have both cut

down on carbs so my hopes are that once he finishes this, he comes into full-on lowcarbing

and not being butter-shy.

does anyone know how it can affect a body to make such a transition though? VLCD to a

low carb and high fat diet -- will there be any positive results on his weight or will it take

longer than had he gone from SAD (well, swedish...) to lowcarb? i don't want him to be

discouraged and quit it before it starts being awesome, and many seem to drop off it at

first sign of ketosis discomfort kicking in.

best of luck to you and your oldies

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T11:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-11655>

I hate this: reading about 'senior loved ones' and then read "almost 60'!

Might I suggest 55-75 might be 'middle aged loved ones.' (75-85= I'll let you know when I get there, in 14 years)

Lots of love, Localad. xxx

### .lina — 2012-04-05T12:23:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-11661>

haha so sorry! let's just say i define it by him as being MY senior?  but you are right of course,

he's but a spring chicken compared to his potential. it just becomes much more evident with

age making people appear much older when there's poor health involved. he's visiting me tomorrow

for the whole easter weekend and is allowed to eat one solid meal per day which me & the bf cooks,

and we're gonna try and really sell in lowcarb with all the deliciousness and healthinessssss

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T13:43:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-11670>

Took me a year of 'paleo' "baking" etc, (you know, all the almondy, cauliflowery, grated zucchini stuff) to get unhooked from a lifetime of grainy 'crusty goodness'. (I don't bother with 'paleo baking' these days.) Take it steady with your Dad, it's worth it!

[](http://http://s70.photobucket.com/albums/i97/nmhsu/?action=view&current=SWS_George_Phillips-3.jpg)

60, not out.

### DarleenMB — 2012-04-06T03:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-12021>

thanks. At 65 I highly resent references to "elders in their 60's". I am NOT there YET.

As to your folks... give 'em a copy of Wheat belly. I snuck a copy on to hubby's Kindle and he actually read it. That was in December. He announced he would give up wheat but not until the first of the year because "it's the holidays."

He was true to his word. I also have several paleo and low-carb cookbooks lying about.

Today he got on the scale and he has EFFORTLESSLY dropped 12 pounds. He was SO proud of himself announcing he "thought" he might have lost some because his jeans felt loose.

### Marsha — 2012-04-06T04:41:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-12039>

I'm with you, DarleenMB and localad!

I'm 65 and must admit that I have felt, at times during this K/Paleo and CT journey, that it's probably too late for me. I mean, how much longer will my telomeres grow with this very late start??!! Man, I wish I'd known about all of this before the vegetarianism, RAW, whole grains and low fat, as well as, decades of SAD before that!

But I _didn't know before and I _have started now__ __- so move over you 'young whipper - snappers' and make room for your so called 'elders'! My mind and heart feel like I'm a more wise 35 - 40 year old!

Today I had an awesome CT session - whole hour in 53-54 degree water rockin' out to _Florence and the Machine_, Young the Giant,_ as well as, The Stones and Led Zeppelin_! (Nearly dropped the I-Pad in the tub yesterday, so reading is out and the i-pod is in!)

That being said - be an example for your parents/grandparents. Let them know how good your life is becoming - presenting wonderful dishes made with love and butter will help, too!

It's amazing how things can change when one knows that another cares enough to say the truth and follow through. I wish my kids had known about this way of living and convinced me by their example - I would have been won over! Now it's the other way around - I have to set an example for them!

### Petra — 2012-04-12T12:39:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-14163>

Everybody has it's own ways of taking care.

For the people I love, the best thing I can do for them, is be the best ME.

What really annoys me, are those folks for whom their diet is like a religion that needs to be forced upon me.

Show me a student, I will be the teacher. I go with that.

Petra

# Thread: What do we know about brown adipose tissue
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 64

### AKMan — 2012-04-04T21:44:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-11421>

Just read this on [www.physorg.com](http://http://www.physorg.com)

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-01-brown-fat-calories-adult-humans.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-01-brown-fat-calories-adult-humans.html)

Brown adipose tissue (often known as brown fat) is a specialized tissue that burns calories to generate body heat in rodents and newborn humans, neither of which shiver.

Recently, adult humans have also been found to possess brown fat. This fact piqued the interest of researchers seeking to combat the obesity epidemic, the thought being that if they could develop ways to increase the amount of brown fat a person has that person will become slimmer. One hitch to this idea is it has never actually been shown definitively that brown fat in adult humans can burn energy.

Now, a team of researchers — led by AndrÃ© C. Carpentier, at UniversitÃ© de Sherbrooke, Sherbrooke, Quebec; and Denis Richard, at UniversitÃ© Laval, Quebec City, Quebec — has provided this evidence, showing that when healthy adult men are exposed to cold their brown fat burns energy to generate body heat. However, it did not burn energy at warm temperatures.

As Barbara Cannon and Jan Nedergaard, at Stockholm University, Sweden, discuss in an accompanying commentary, these data have significant implications for the human obesity epidemic. In particular, they note that the data generated by Carpentier, Richard, and colleagues indicates that developing ways to increase the amount of brown fat a person has is unlikely to make that person slimmer, what is needed is a way to make sure that the brown fat is active and burns calories.

### AKMan — 2012-04-04T21:52:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-11423>

I can't get enough of these! Here is another one: [http://www.jci.org/articles/view/60433?key=5e3684aee3d55b74adc8](http://http://www.jci.org/articles/view/60433?key=5e3684aee3d55b74adc8)

This is the main point to me: Your CT activities should be done without shivering. Shivering stops BAT activity, as found here:

In summary, the present study demonstrates that a cold-exposure stimulus designed to minimize muscle-mediated shivering thermogenesis enhances BAT oxidative metabolism as well as glucose and NEFA uptake in adult humans. The enhanced BAT activity was associated with a 1.8-fold increase in whole-body energy expenditure. We found a significant inverse relationship between BAT volume of activity and shivering and a significant increase in BAT radio density within 3 hours of cold exposure, indicating rapid reduction in BAT triglyceride content. The present results demonstrate that BAT is undoubtedly involved in nonshivering thermogenesis in humans.

Note: This study was done with 18 deg C (65 deg F) water.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T02:16:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-16397>

[http://www.mendeley.com/research/effects-diet-cold-acclimation-lipid-composition-rat-interscapular-brown-adipose-tissue/](http://http://www.mendeley.com/research/effects-diet-cold-acclimation-lipid-composition-rat-interscapular-brown-adipose-tissue/)

**

Effects of diet and cold acclimation on lipid composition of rat interscapular brown adipose tissue.**

The effects of diet and of cold acclimation on lipid composition of interscapular brown adipose tissue (BAT) were studied in rats. Three groups of animals were acclimated for 20 weeks to 28 degrees C (controls), 5 degrees C (CA) or to a daily fluctuating temperature (Cy). They received either normal fat (5 p. 100 lipids) NF or high fat (26 p. 100) HF diets. Cold acclimation increased the tissue weight and its protein, phospholipid, free fatty acid and cholesterol content. It decreased the total lipid proportion in the tissue. HF diet led to an increase in lipid content and to a decrease in the protein content only in control and Cy groups. The mean chain length of triglyceride or phospholipid fatty acids was increased by HF diet and cold acclimation. The proportion of unsaturated acids was not changed in cold acclimated rats, but the amount of arachidonic acid bound to phospholipid was significantly increased. It is concluded that the total amount of lipid in the diet acts on the synthesis of BAT lipids. The cold dependant changes in lipid composition which are similar but less important in cyclic cold acclimation than in constant cold adaptation seem to be proportional to the increase in the tissue energy metabolism.

I'm still thinking fat's perty good for a cold adapted gal.

BTW There are tons of cold adapted rat studies from the 60's on. Everything changes in the cold. I am a coarse communicator, but there is some good stuff on epididimys fat and growth hormone.

### chocolate — 2012-05-08T19:19:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-22280>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20557470](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20557470)

Significance and application of melatonin in the regulation of brown adipose tissue metabolism: relation to human obesity.

Tan DX, Manchester LC, Fuentes-Broto L, Paredes SD, Reiter RJ.

Source

Department of Cellular and Structural Biology, the University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio, San Antonio, TX 78229, USA.

Abstract

A worldwide increase in the incidence of obesity indicates the unsuccessful battle against this disorder. Obesity and the associated health problems urgently require effective strategies of treatment. The new discovery that a substantial amount of functional brown adipose tissue (BAT) is retained in adult humans provides a potential target for treatment of human obesity. BAT is active metabolically and disposes of extra energy via generation of heat through uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria. The physiology of BAT is readily regulated by melatonin, which not only increases recruitment of brown adipocytes but also elevates their metabolic activity in mammals. It is speculated that the hypertrophic effect and functional activation of BAT induced by melatonin may likely apply to the human. **Thus, melatonin, a naturally occurring substance with no reported toxicity, may serve as a novel approach for treatment of obesity. Conversely, because of the availability of artificial light sources, excessive light exposure after darkness onset in modern societies should be considered a potential contributory factor to human obesity as light at night dramatically reduces endogenous melatonin production. In the current article, the potential associations of melatonin, BAT, obesity and the medical implications are discussed.**

I saw the full-text of this study recently. It's implications are huge.

It demonstrates quite readily that melatonin regulates BAT activation. Unfortunately, most humans are melatonin-deranged due to light abuse, that is getting doses of artificial light that interfere with normal circadioan rythms for melatonin production.

This study implies that BAT growth is more tied to light cycles and normal melatonin secretion than to cold. It makes sense, when the light cycle is shorter, as in Winter, more melatonin is produced, activating more BAT.

This study also goes into detail about BAT as a mechanism for 'overeating thermogenesis'. When we take in too many calories, BAT activates to burn these calories as heat regardless of temperature (cold thermogenesis).

When I first read this study, I thought--cool, I'll start popping melatonin pills before bed, but I don't think it will be that simple. The melatonin that the body produces is done in a certain sequence, starting off slowly, increasing around midnight, then tapering til morning. Melatonin supplements provide a huge slug of melatonin that is mostly gone by midnight. You'd need a super-formulated time-release type pill to mirror mother nature.

### MJ* — 2012-05-10T19:03:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-22881>

N~Doro that first post was interesting, could peppermint soap do the trick?:

Saturday, May 5, 2012

Dr. Oz, Pain, Hot/Cold Receptors

The production of endorphins in tissues in response to nerve stimulation relieves inflammation and pain. Dr. Oz and the medical community seem to forget that hot and cold receptors in the skin can be readily triggered by natural products in foods, to stimulate the release of endorphins and treat inflammation in nearby tissues.

Hot and Cold Don't Easily Penetrate the Skin

Athletic trainers commonly apply hot and cold packs to avoid swelling and inflammation from injuries, and they are always discussing the virtues of various hot/cold regimens. They ignore the extraordinary efficiency of the circulatory system in regulating tissue temperatures and avoiding temperature changes. They also ignore the fact that chemical "hot and cold" salves and ointments are effective without actually changing the temperature of the skin. The essential observation is that triggering hot and cold sensing nerves is more important than changing the temperature of the damaged tissues.

Common Food Molecules Activate Hot/Cold Nerve Receptors

Peppers are hot, because they contain capsaicin that binds to protein receptors on nerves in the skin, which results in the brain sensation of heat. Camphor and castor oil bind to the same receptors. Menthol binds to corresponding cold receptors. Vicks Vaporub has both menthol and camphor, and therefore stimulates both hot and cold sensors. Vicks is also an effective treatment for tissue inflammation.

Vicks and Castor Oil are Effective Treatments for Pain and Inflammation

A bee sting or a burn on a finger will produce reddening, swelling and pain, that can be quickly alleviated by applying Vicks to the wrist. The hot and cold sensors of the wrist would be stimulated and the returning nerve signals would be generally detected in the whole hand and produce endorphins that would calm the inflammation and sooth the injured finger. In a similar way, an inflamed joint can be treated by topical menthol and castor oil, and lower abdominal discomfort can be alleviated by castor oil applied to the belly.

Tendonitis can be Treated with Peppermint Soap

I have treated a persistent tendonitis in my shoulder by applying Dr. Bronner's Peppermint Soap liberally to my shoulder and waiting a few minutes before continuing my shower. This gentle, persistent treatment produced relief within a week. This was a cure for this persistent inflammation and pain. It also works on joints.

What Dr. Oz needs to communicate is that there are simple ways to stimulate hot/cold receptors that have nothing to do with changing the temperature of deeper tissues, but these treatments are very effective in stimulating general endorphin production that reduces troublesome inflammation and pain. As an addendum, vagal stimulation, i.e. through yoga postures such as shavasana or the Valsalva maneuver, can produce a reduction in general inflammation.

### chocolate — 2012-05-13T21:00:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-23724>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21820402](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21820402)

Characteristics of the turnover of uncoupling protein 3 by the ubiquitin proteasome system in isolated mitochondria.

Mookerjee SA, Brand MD.

Abstract

Uncoupling protein 3 (UCP3) is implicated in mild uncoupling and the regulation of mitochondrial ROS production. We previously showed that **UCP3 turns over rapidly in C2C12 myoblasts, with a half-life of 0.5-4h, and that turnover can be reconstituted in vitro. We show here that **rapid degradation of UCP3 in vitro in isolated brown adipose tissue mitochondria required the 26S proteasome, ubiquitin, ATP, succinate to generate a high membrane potential, and a pH of 7.4 or less.** _Ubiquitin containing lysine-48_ was both necessary and sufficient to support UCP3 degradation, implying a requirement for polyubiquitylation at this residue. The 20S proteasome did not support degradation. UCP3 degradation was prevented by simultaneously blocking matrix ATP generation and import, showing that ATP in the mitochondrial matrix was required. Degradation did not appear to require a transmembrane pH gradient, but was very sensitive to membrane potential: degradation was halved when membrane potential decreased 10-20mV from its resting value, and was not significant below about 120mV. We propose that matrix ATP and a high membrane potential are needed for UCP3 to be polyubiquitylated through lysine-48 of ubiquitin and exported to the cytosolic 26S proteasome, where it is de-ubiquitylated and degraded.**

lysine 48 is a player in the iron reactions also [http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/ja209297n](http://http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/ja209297n)

Control of the Evolution of Iron Peroxide Intermediate in Superoxide Reductase from Desulfoarculus baarsii. Involvement of Lysine 48 in Protonation

# Thread: Dietary Menthol as Cold Thermogenesis / Cold Adaptation Supplement?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gambolputty — 2012-04-02T17:48:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-10312>

I came across this study and found it quite interesting so I thought I would share it here. I am presently at the face dunking stage of the CT protocol. I would love to think that adding menthol to my diet could help speed the CT process. Peppermint oil is roughly 30-50% menthol. It is available in capsules, but often blended with other oils for help with gastric issues. It would be great to find a source for 100% Peppermint Oil capsules.

Anyway, I would love to hear feed back from more knowledgeable members. I posted this info at MDA and it attracted a gaggle of Trolls.

Activation of the cold-sensing TRPM8 channel triggers UCP1-dependent thermogenesis and prevents obesity

Abstract

Brown adipose tissue (BAT) is an energy-expending organ that produces heat. Expansion or activation of BAT prevents obesity and diabetes. Chronic cold exposure enhances thermogenesis in BAT through uncoupling protein 1 (UCP1) activation triggered via a β-adrenergic pathway. Here, we report that the cold-sensing transient receptor potential melastatin 8 (TRPM8) is functionally present in mouse BAT. Challenging brown adipocytes with menthol, a TRPM8 agonist, up-regulates UCP1 expression and requires protein kinase A activation. Upon mimicking long-term cold exposure with chronic dietary menthol application, menthol significantly increased the core temperatures and locomotor activity in wild-type mice; these effects were absent in both TRPM8−/− and UCP1−/− mice. Dietary obesity and glucose abnormalities were also prevented by menthol treatment. Our results reveal a previously unrecognized role for TRPM8, suggesting that stimulation of this channel mediates BAT thermogenesis, which could constitute a promising way to treat obesity.

Link:

[http://jmcb.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2012/02/17/jmcb.mjs001.full](http://http://jmcb.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2012/02/17/jmcb.mjs001.full)

A note on safety: While menthol is GRAS for human consumption, there are limits. Please note: Ingested pure menthol can be poisonous - as little as a teaspoonful (1 gram per kilogram of body weight) can be fatal ([http://www.rx.com/reference/natural/Peppermint.jhtml](http://http://www.rx.com/reference/natural/Peppermint.jhtml)). The estimated acceptable daily intake is up to 200 micrograms per kilogram of body weight (Martindale, The Extra Pharmacopoeia, 1993). Dr. James Duke at the USDA Agricultural Research Center estimates that the LD50 (dose at which half the animals die) for menthol is higher than coffee, oral LD50 in rats of 192 mg/kg (CRC Handbook of Medicinal Herbs, 1987).

### primat — 2012-04-02T18:03:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-10323>

So that's why the menthol cigarettes kept me skinny back in the days

### janagram — 2012-04-21T02:25:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17268>

Oh, man, those Kools!

### MJ* — 2012-04-21T16:15:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17381>

Gambolputty first welcome to this forum, I see this is your first post. I don't think you'll find trolls here. Everyone's here because they want to be optimal! I like this theory, it's really interesting. I'd like to hear what Dr. Kruse thinks too. I wonder also is there menthol in peppermints? My father lived to be almost 90 and he ate a lot of cake and bad stuff and he ate peppermint patties everyday, and he never had a weight problem!

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T16:59:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17408>

I took some pure little tiny balls of peppermint that are supposed to be an anti-parasitic. I got them a few years back.

### AKMan — 2012-04-21T19:23:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17473>

I think the idea has merit, but keep in mind this was a study on mice. You can also find studies with capsinoids, the non-heat part of hot-peppers that also activate thermogenesis. I try to add a little cayenne to about everything I eat, not sure if it helps, but I like it so I do it. Same could be said for menthol. I doubt it is the magic bullet, but if it fits in your diet and taste, give it a try.

I'd be willing to bet that study got the attention of a drug manufacturer and they are scrambling to isolate compounds they can market for obesity.

Thanks for posting!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-21T20:19:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17488>

I've used spirit of peppermint (in water) now and then for years. Wish it meant I was not fat!

You can grow peppermint and tincture it, or use it in cooking. It is frequently used in Middle Eastern cuisine.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T23:54:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17550>

[http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Spearmint/fulltext%285%29.pdf](http://http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Spearmint/fulltext%285%29.pdf)

Something jumped into my head about this.....it reduces testosterone, which is good for the ladies and the facial hair....I am finding that these things are very rarely ac/dc. Just like the berries are good for male Parkinson's and no improvement for females.

A little excerpt:

**A significant decrease in triglyceride levels was found

after the consumption of spearmint tea in hirsute

women. Women with endogenous hyperandrogenism

often have high triglyceride levels associated with insulin

resistance and obesity. Partial androgen suppression

or the pure antiandrogen treatment with flutamide in

PCOS results in no change in insulin resistance or

lipoprotein levels, indicating that lipoprotein abnormalities

are due to insulin resistance and not to the androgen

levels (Vrbikova et al., 2004; Wild et al., 1992).**

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-22T00:12:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17556>

Very interesting, Chocolate -- thank you.

Note that the study uses spearmint tea instead of peppermint. Might not have quite the same effect.

One can grow spearmint too, of course.

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T00:33:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17564>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15941008](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15941008)

Toxicol Ind Health. 2004 Sep;20(6-10):119-22.

**Effect of Mentha piperita (Labiatae) and Mentha spicata (Labiatae) on iron absorption in rats.**

Akdogan M, Gultekin F, Yontem M.

Source

Department of Biochemistry, Suleyman Demirel University, Faculty of Medicine, Isparta, Turkey. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

Abstract

AIM:

The effect of Mentha piperita (Labiatae) and Mentha spicata (Labiatae) teas, which contain different phenol compounds, on iron metabolism was studied. These teas grow in different areas of the city of Isparta, Turkey. These herbals were given to the rats in tea.

METHODS:

Forty-eight male Wistar albino rats weighing 200-250 g were used for this study. The rats were divided into four groups of 12 animals: Group I received no herbal tea (control group); Group II received 20 g/L M. piperita tea; Group III received 20 g/L M. spicata tea; Group IV received 40 g/L M. spicata tea. Herbal teas were prepared daily and provided at all times to the rats over 30 days as drinking water.

RESULTS:

M. piperita tea caused a decrease in serum iron and ferritin levels (P < 0.05), and caused an increase in unsaturated iron-binding capacity (UIBC) (P < 0.01). M. spicata tea caused no significant change in serum iron, ferritin levels and UIBC (P > 0.05).

CONCLUSION:

Both herbal teas inhibited Fe absorption. Inhibition caused by M. spicata tea was dose dependent. Therefore, when drinking these teas, their effect should be considered, especially for children and anemic patients.

PMID:

15941008

\[PubMed - indexed for MEDLINE\]

### Tizawee — 2012-04-22T00:38:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17567>

Peppermint oil capsules are already in use for relief from IBS symptoms. Not sure how well they work: I just googled peppermint and found it.

I have been using peppermint oil orally for about 18 months. Ordered it from Amazon. I use it to brush my teeth, to rinse afterwards and as a breath freshener throughout the day. I don't swallow the morning routine stuff but do swallow the breath freshener. I was under the impression that one has to be careful with how much is ingested but can't find the reference that told me. I have been thinking about mixing it with almond oil and trying it for oil pulling.

For anyone thinking about using straight peppermint oil orally, be aware that it's v-e-r-y strong. Until you get used to it, put one drop on your tongue and swish it around your whole mouth. If you swallow it, do so in little tiny amounts at a time. You'll know if you got too much: it burns your mouth like eating too many of those hot cinnamon candies and opens up your sinuses like a dose of fresh horseradish. Once you get used to it, you can use a few drops at a time. Oh, it makes you burp, too.

It's also good topically because it kills germs instantly.

Hope this helps,

Elin

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T00:41:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17570>

> **piano-doctor-lady;17627 wrote:** Very interesting, Chocolate -- thank you.
>
>
>
> Note that the study uses spearmint tea instead of peppermint. Might not have quite the same effect.
>
>
>
> One can grow spearmint too, of course.

[http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Spearmint/fulltext%285%29.pdf](http://http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Spearmint/fulltext%285%29.pdf)

Before planning this study, it was observed that

some male patients who live in Yeni8arbademli town

were admitted to hospitals with complaints of diminished

libido after daily consumption of four cups of

tea steeped with M. spicata or M. piperita. Therefore

this study investigated the effect of spearmint

tea on the androgen level in female patients with

hirsutism and a significant decrease in FT levels was found.

Maybe they have different effects but they inspired the test , and the peppermint and spearmint both seem to help the hirsutism and free testosterone. I'm loving that it slows iron absorption. I think its a go for gals.

### MJ* — 2012-04-22T17:04:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17732>

According to this [article,](http://http://www.nutrition-and-you.com/spearmint.html) peppermint has a much higher content of menthol than spearmint:

"The leaves and herb parts contain essential oil menthol. However, unlike in peppermint, spearmint does not contain high amounts of menthol (0.5% compared to the 40% in peppermint), making it least pungent and subtly fragrant herb in mint family. "

# Thread: How much coconut oil is considered too much?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### usmile777 — 2012-04-02T22:01:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10454>

I am doing Leptin Reset RX since day 1 of P3, now is day 8.

I am having 2 tablespoons of cocoa crack with every meal plus all the cooking with CO, total 6-8 table spoons per day. Is it too much? 30% of my daily calories come from CO. Just want to make sure I don't overdo it.

Thank you!

### usmile777 — 2012-04-03T17:35:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10778>

I already found my answer, if anybody else is interested - 8 table spoons max.

### usmile777 — 2012-04-03T19:00:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10822>

August -I am confused about CO and HCG as well. Currently I am not on HCG, just finished my 500 cal phase 10 days ago. I am hoping that CO is OK for LR to do actual stabilization.

Not sure if this thread belongs under HCG sub-forum.

### usmile777 — 2012-04-03T19:39:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10842>

It's already under HCG section

### ASHO — 2012-04-03T19:56:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10847>

> **usmile777;10847 wrote:** I already found my answer, if anybody else is interested - 8 table spoons max.

Where did you find this information of 8 table spoons max?

Thank you.

### quelsen — 2012-04-03T20:33:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10870>

too much CO is when you cramp and pass out. ( as I have done)

Too much CO is when you begin to gain weight in a ketogenic situation all other macro-nutrients being strictly controlled.

In short there is no such value as too much unless you meet the first statement.

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-03T20:46:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10873>

> **usmile777;10523 wrote:** I am doing Leptin Reset RX since day 1 of P3, now is day 8.
>
> I am having 2 tablespoons of cocoa crack with every meal plus all the cooking with CO, total 6-8 table spoons per day. Is it too much? 30% of my daily calories come from CO. Just want to make sure I don't overdo it.
>
> Thank you!

I do not know what your total caloric count is but if you are keeping it low, you are getting way more then 30%

1 tablespoon is approx 126 calories x 8 is 1008 calories of CO ( 8 tablespoons is 112 grams of fat x 9 calories ). So unless you are eating 3000 plus calories a day, you are getting a much higher percentage of fat in your diet.

### usmile777 — 2012-04-03T21:01:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10878>

Quelsen - interesting theory about CO.

Continuum Fitness - what is the formula - Fat-Protein-Carbs? I know I have to keep under 50g of carbs per day. Is it pretty much 70% from protein and 30% from fat during Leptin Reset Rx? My calorie consumption is around 1700 a day.

Thank you for your help!

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-03T21:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10884>

> **usmile777;10947 wrote:** Quelsen - interesting theory about CO.
>
> Continuum Fitness - what is the formula - Fat-Protein-Carbs? I know I have to keep under 50g of carbs per day. Is it pretty much 70% from protein and 30% from fat during Leptin Reset Rx? My calorie consumption is around 1700 a day.
>
> Thank you for your help!

I personally try to keep a 50% fat, 40% protein, 10% carb. Now I am not resetting but even when I did I kept in this range.

so based off your 1700 calories

850 calories of fat or 94 grams

680 calories of protein or 170 grams

170 calories of carbs or 42.5 grams

using your numbers of 70% protein 1700x 70% = 1190 calories or 297.5 grams of protein

1700 x 30% of fat is 510 calories or 56 grams of fat. So if you are eating 8 table spoons of CO, well see my numbers above. your calculations are way off. you are getting 60% of your fat from CO or 1008 calories/1700 plus the amount of fat mixed in with your animal protein.

### usmile777 — 2012-04-03T21:35:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10891>

Continuum Fitness - thank you for sharing what you do.

I am figuring things out, still having hunger issues after being on 500 cal/day for 28 days, and it seems like fat is really helping me to keep my hunger under control.

Would you adjust the ration of 50% fat, 40% protein, 10% carb if your client was a woman? Appreciate your help.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-03T21:37:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10892>

You only do 50% fat? I never let my fat go that low. I keep it about 65-70%. It's helped keep me very stable after a 75 pounds loss on hcg.

### usmile777 — 2012-04-03T21:42:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10894>

Hi Colleen

### quelsen — 2012-04-03T22:01:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10901>

### colleencoble — 2012-04-03T22:08:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10904>

I've put my stuff into fitday every now and then to see where it falls. It's always about the same. I have heavy whipping cream in my coffee (probably 6 T a day) real butter on my vegetables, fat in meats, eggs, avocados, coconut oil, etc. It adds up pretty fast. I don't eat much cocoa crack. I like it fine but I don't like getting into the habit of eating it and feeding the sweet taste monster.  I had some today and it was the first time I've had it in about 3 months.

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-03T22:39:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10922>

> **colleencoble;10961 wrote: You only do 50% fat? I never let my fat go that low. I keep it about 65-70%. It's helped keep me very stable after a 75 pounds loss on hcg. It works for me, there are days I do more but 50% is good plus it keeps my protein up to help with muscle repair.
>
>
>
> usmile777;10960 wrote:** Continuum Fitness - thank you for sharing what you do.
>
> I am figuring things out, still having hunger issues after being on 500 cal/day for 28 days, and it seems like fat is really helping me to keep my hunger under control.
>
> Would you adjust the ration of 50% fat, 40% protein, 10% carb if your client was a woman? Appreciate your help.

Do what you are comfortable with and what gives you satiety. My concern is after only eating 500 cal for 28 days, you lost both muscle and fat. What I really wanted you to see was that you were consuming more than 30% of your calories from fat. It seemed you had a misconception of the amount of fat you were consuming.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-03T23:00:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10933>

Have you ever done hcg? it's amazing how it works. It's the only diet that actually takes the fat from where you need to lose it. Awesome!

### Mery — 2012-04-02T22:07:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c-o.612/post-10459>

I was wondering if anybody has similar problems like me introducing to their diet CO.

Iâ€™ve been doing LR for 2 weeks and CT. On the 4th day I started eating CO. and then I had a sharp pain in my tummy and later on I got bloated, I was disappointed as Dr Jack said itâ€™s good for us specially if one has Leaky Gut like me.

My health is not as bad as it used to be 5 years ago, slowly Iâ€™ve been healing my body first Parkinson, Rheumatoid arthritis, depression, lack of energy, food allergies (I still got some like not been able to eat onions, leaks, chillies and CO) I stop taking CO on Sunday as I get bloated every time I eat.

I take Armour, adrenal, cortisone and vitamin C.

Any clues as why is this happening?

### quelsen — 2012-04-02T22:19:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c-o.612/post-10462>

Yes.

CO may cause cramping.

I dont know your situation but here is my recommendation / suggestion

1\. Find your bowel tolerance\* level for Vitamin C

2\. Start cleaning your gut with Magnesium Citrate at bowel tolerance levels do this for 8 weeks minimum. This will eliminate excess waste in the track.

3\. Take 1000 mg less than BT of Vitamin C daily.

4\. Take a strong Probiotic such as Standard process ProSynBiotic or Ultimate Flora Super Critical 200 Billion both available. Take from amazon i think. You want to take a very large dose with Fage or other greek ( clean ) yogurt ( unless dairy intolerant) for 4 weeks then drop down to half that for 4 weeks. ( large dose = 10 caps of ProSynbiotoc daily)

5\. Consume 1 can of high quality coconut milk daily for 2 weeks.

In two weeks you should be able to stomach 1 tablespoon of CO straight. slowly increase that to a max of 8

happy huunting

Bowel Tolerance means take enough that you need to have a bowel movement.

### Mery — 2012-04-03T15:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c-o.612/post-10735>

Thanks Quelsen for taking the time to reply.

I do suffer from constipation and I do take Broom with dinner I take Vitamin C 3 grams a day and Magnesium citrate 1900 mg.

What do you mean with BT of Vitamin C daily pardon my ingnorance.

I've order Ultimate Flore Super Critical 200 Billion

I think I can have yogurt although in the past it didn’t work even though I used to make my own. thanks

### quelsen — 2012-04-03T16:32:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c-o.612/post-10761>

bowel tolerance on the Vitamin C

If you take 1900mg Magnesium and still have constipation issues then you are where i started.

after figureing out BT on C take a weekend and drink 2 - 3 8oz bottles of mag citrate from any pharmacy ( along with at least a gallon of water ) but realize that will be your entire weekend.

drink on bottle every 4 hours until you have the reaction.... you will know when you finally get it.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-03T17:16:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c-o.612/post-10774>

It sounds like you have a leaky gut (not surprising with RA & Parkinson's). Q's suggestions are good, and also Dr. Kruse has a blog post on his leaky gut prescription. Start slow and build up, ,and CO will heal that gut!

CO can also cause serious die-off in a gut with dysbiosis (many of us).

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-03T17:40:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c-o.612/post-10784>

Now, I eat anywhere from 2-6 T/day with no troubles.

# Thread: Recipe for mashed eggs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipe-for-mashed-eggs.613/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rojo — 2012-04-02T23:44:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipe-for-mashed-eggs.613/post-10503>

Hi all,

I thought I would share an easy recipe I enjoy. It's from the Primal Kitchen: A Family Grokumentary blog:

Ingredients

\* Four eggs

\* 2 tablespoons butter, or to taste (I used salted Kerrygold)

\* Pepper (I used freshly ground)

Directions

Boil eggs in a rolling boil for 15 minutes. Remove with a slotted

spoon and plunge immediately into bowl of ice water (this turns your

yolks the pretty yellow instead of sulfuric grey). Shell the eggs

while they are still warm, place in a bowl, mash with butter, garnish

with pepper, and serve.

# Thread: Help me out here - I am a miserable mess today.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### differentstory — 2012-04-03T00:07:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/post-10511>

I have been battling depression for a long time and have been on anti-depressants for 5+ years (Wellbutrin). On Wellbutrin, I have been AMAZING - but slowly it started to not be as effective at handling my blues and I could NOT eat healthy when on medication - I found myself eating a ridiculous amount of sweets throughout the day.

Fast forward 5 years, my menstrual cycle became very sporadic to the point that it stopped for over 6 months and my depression kicked in to high gear and couldn't be controlled by Wellbutrin anymore. I wound up in the dr's office and she handed me a new anti-depressant (Vibranyce I think??) and when I asked about it she said she "heard" it was "quite similar." Well I get home, start doing the research and realize it is SO NOT SIMILAR! I was pretty upset that my doctor would just hand me something like that without even really knowing anything about it - especially after everything I read about the side effects (from others that had just started taking it)!

Needless to say, at that moment, while researching the new medication, I decided that I really needed to take my life back. So now I am here, 2 months later.

I started primal eating almost 30 days ago. I CT - I'm having good progress with it. Have had lots of labs - still working to understand the results (I paid for them myself). I am supplementing. Increasing the vegetables (my goal is to get to 9 cups a day - currently I am at 5 cups a day).

I stopped my anti-depressants with good success in the beginning. I weigh 196. I've lost 6.6 pounds! My menstrual cycle came back this weekend (YAY ME!) and I have a feeling that it will be a regular cycle from now on as long as I continue eating primal (based on my history). My blood pressure is down. My temp is getting definitely moving around acting like it wants to stabilize.

But things are still rough.  I am 34 (although I feel like I am 75). I am really lacking in the ENERGY department. My mood and anxiety are a million times better - where I was probably a 2 out of 10, I am now a 5.5 out of 10. But I don't have any motivation to work. All I want to do is sit and read or sit and play on the computer. I can barely do laundry or clean the kitchen. I am FAKING it every day at work and at home.  I am sleeping 10-11 hours a day too.

Today is a REALLY bad day for me as I just feel sad, down, wanting to cry. . .I am on my period - I know that is part of it. But, I've gone through this for FOUR WEEKS thinking it is going to get better but it is not getting better.

2 months ago when I started this journey, I had finally had it and started googling like crazy. I have symptoms of hyperthyroidism and marched into my dr. wanting to be tested - I just KNEW that was my problem. All of the tests came back "fine" though (she did do the T3 and T4). I don't have the numbers with me at the moment.

I think my problems are all hormone related (although isn't everything?). But I am not sure where to turn to next.

Also, just wanted to say that I did have my gallbladder removed when I was 17. I'm not sure if that has anything to do with my lack of energy.

Please please please help me figure out what to do next.

### sandy — 2012-04-03T00:14:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/post-10514>

HI

I just wanted to say, I know how you feel. I have been suffering from depression to varying degrees for many years. It got so bad a couple of years ago that I went to the doctor. He gave me lexapro which made me sleep for 12 hours! I got off of that and now take magnesium malate instead. It really helps me from feeling like I am going crazy.

I still have the same things as you as far as motivation- don't feel like doing anything, don't feel like cleaning, I would stay in bed most of the day if I could get away with it.

I don't have any answers for you. I am hoping the leptin reset and CT will help me. I also plan on going to a doctor who is willing to work on my hormone levels and andrenals and things like that.

I hope we both find the answer! hugs

### NotAnother30 — 2012-04-03T00:17:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/post-10515>

I understand how you are feeling. I too was on a similar journey but was able to correct things one at a time. The final one with me is weight. I was just wondering if you consumed soy by any chance?

here's my N=1 [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?95-N-1-My-journey-on-the-Leptin-Reset](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?95-N-1-My-journey-on-the-Leptin-Reset)

I've got PCOS, bipolar, fibromaylgia, chronic fatigue. at some point we've all been where you are... it took me years of struggling to discover MDA, and Dr K... I wouldn't be where I am today.

take one day at a time... and remember your thoughts can change your DNA. Think about what you want to be tomorrow, or the next hour and it will happen.

### differentstory — 2012-04-03T00:56:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/post-10539>

> **sandy;10583 wrote:** HI
>
> I just wanted to say, I know how you feel. I have been suffering from depression to varying degrees for many years. It got so bad a couple of years ago that I went to the doctor. He gave me lexapro which made me sleep for 12 hours! I got off of that and now take magnesium malate instead. It really helps me from feeling like I am going crazy.
