# Thread: Learning how to breathe
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-04-09T20:23:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-13058>

For me, all signs point to breathing as something we will be dealing with soon on our journey to optimal. I feel to dumb sounding like I don't know how to breathe....but I actually think I do it wrong. Any tips on how to optimally oxygenate? Are there exercises or ways to train the body to breathe correctly? I tend to be a shallow rapid breather.

### Pardus — 2012-04-09T22:00:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-13112>

Hi ColdBren,

I did a little research on Buteyko breathing a while back. It's a controversial method though...

Lately what I have been trying:

\- breathing from the diaphragm (not the chest cavity) similar to how a baby breathes (before they grow up and become self conscious of their tummy sticking out)

\- breathing slower

\- breathing through your nose, to moisten the air as it comes in.

### ColdBren — 2012-04-09T23:17:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-13136>

nothing more beautiful than a baby with their stomach out for all to see.....i wish that didn't stop being cute lol

### SimonM — 2012-04-10T00:36:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-13147>

Yeah, what is it with Buteyko? All sounds logical, but never seems to have caught on. @ColdBren, there is a stack of material in Buteyko and elsewhere saying that "overbreathing" - which sounds like you may be doing - is bad news. Carbon dioxide seems to be as important as oxygen for us, and rapid breathing decreases Carbon dioxide levels which then leads to us, paradoxically, breathing harder and getting lots of oxygen into our blood, but less of it gets out of our blood and into our tissues...

This is typically what happens at the start of (running) races and during extreme climbing.

Agree with Pardus: breathing through the nose seems to be a key idea, and taking fewer breaths is also good. [Stephen Elliott's website](http://http://www.coherence.com/) has a stack of info on breathing plus a free timer you can download to practise getting into their recommended rhythm.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-12T19:19:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/learning-how-to-breathe.752/post-23431>

Yes, I agree, breathing can really alter your inner world and help those with problems with thoughts driving adrenals (like me). I agree with the breathing with your belly mentioned up there. I also like what is called the yogic three part breathing. Basically you imagine that you are breathing into your belly first, then middle chest, then upper chest and then you exhale in reverse order. I also will do 5 breathing - breathe in to the count of 5, hold for count of 5 and exhale for count of 5. I just bought this little gizmo that trains you on breathing if you wanna check it out-

[http://www.bulletproofexec.com/emwave2-hrv-data-export/](http://http://www.bulletproofexec.com/emwave2-hrv-data-export/)

good luck


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Jack's busy Marketing Monkeys -- a format problem
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T21:05:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-13085>

You guys seem to be working non-stop today.

Anyway, I'm 65, and I need a moderately generous font size.

I don't see how to change that when reading other people's posts.

I can enlarge the posts with my browser, just by typing ctrl-+, however, once the font size gets big enough the page is too wide for my monitor. To see the whole line in the post, I lose the avatar and name of poster, and have to scroll back and forth.

A way to make the page narrower? A way to increase font size when reading the forum?

### admin — 2012-04-09T21:46:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-13109>

There is not another solution to this other than the ctrl+ or ctrl-

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-11T01:05:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-13512>

> **Jack's Marketing Monkeys;13178 wrote:** There is not another solution to this other than the ctrl+ or ctrl-

Excuse me, why not?

If I use ctrl+ till the print is large enough, the lines are too long to read without scrolling.

Other sites have a little icon to click for a larger or smaller font. Why not here?

### admin — 2012-04-11T01:20:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-13519>

I don't believe I've seen those icons on other sites. It has become universal to change font sizes using the ctrl+ and ctrl- in all browsers.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-12T06:53:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jacks-busy-marketing-monkeys-a-format-problem.755/post-14108>

> **Jack's Marketing Monkeys;13588 wrote:** I don't believe I've seen those icons on other sites. It has become universal to change font sizes using the ctrl+ and ctrl- in all browsers.

However, when one changes the size of font in articles using ctrl+, the line lengths adjust to the larger print. On this site, the lines just get longer and longer, till one has to scroll.

I'll see if I can fish up some sites which still have the font size icon. Maybe they have become fewer and I haven't noticed.

### David — 2012-04-09T22:03:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thomas-jefferson-knew-about-cold-thermogenesis.750/post-13113>

Cold water probably had a place in medical treatment well before we turned to a drug-based disease treatment scheme.

â€œAnd now did the colonists possess an efficacious agent to act against the inflammation which might occur?

Yes. They had one, for nature had generously lavished it. They had **cold water**, that is to say, the most powerful sedative that can be employed against inflammation of wounds, the most efficacious therapeutic agent in grave cases, and the one which is now adopted by all physicians.

Cold water has, moreover, the advantage of leaving the wound in absolute rest, and preserving it from all premature dressing, a considerable advantage, since it has been found by experience that contact with the air is dangerous during the first days.â€￾

_Jules Verne, The Mysterious Island_

### Owl — 2012-04-10T18:07:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-getting-nasty-heartburn-at-night.756/post-13369>

You need to take more HCL, with every meal that has protein. One capsule is not enough is you're having heartburn.

"If the first dose of Betaine HCL produced no noticeable stomach discomfort, try taking two capsules at the start of your next meal, again monitor for burning and or heaviness during and after eating. If taking two capsules produces some discomfort, but one capsule does not, restrict yourself to one capsule at the start of each meal. If the two capsule dose produced no discomfort, try three capsules at the start of your next meal.

If all goes well when taking three capsules at the start of each meal, stay with that dosage, most individuals will not require increasing the dosage to four capsules. Regular use of supplemental hydrochloric acid will in most cases "re-train" the stomach to produce higher concentrations of acid on its own. This process may take anywhere from several weeks to several months, depending on the individual. The most significant indication that acid production is improving is that Betaine HCL supplementation can no longer be comfortably tolerated.

An Example:

After having used the Betaine HCL for eleven weeks, at a dosage of two capsules per meal, without any discomfort, Joan now notices that she feels a burning sensation when taking the two capsules. She therefore reduces her dosage to one capsule at mealtime. After several more weeks, even one capsule per meal produces burning and Joan discontinues altogether. This is a sign that the stomach has been re-trained to produce adequate concentrations of hydrochloric acid without the further need for supplementation.

When consuming meals that contain little or no protein, much less hydrochloric acid is needed by the stomach to process its contents."

### janagram — 2012-04-12T09:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-getting-nasty-heartburn-at-night.756/post-14141>

thanks, owl. will implement.

[http://www.idpublications.com/journals/PDFs/JACI/JACI\_MostCited\_1.pdf](http://http://www.idpublications.com/journals/PDFs/JACI/JACI_MostCited_1.pdf)

I was trying to compare my raised skin from bad allergies to an earlier ct reaction. I was thinking the brown stuff was plumping up. The white stuff harbors IL-6. Talked about the need for a controlled burn.

"Other cytokines and chemokines

In addition to IL-6 and TNF-a, WAT expresses and

releases a variety of other cytokines and chemokines.

WAT-derived IL-1 receptor antagonist is markedly increased

in the serum of obese subjects, as is IL-18,

although its ultimate source has not been identified.57,58

WAT also produces chemokines, including IL-8, MCP-1,

and macrophage inflammatory protein 1.

CORRELATION BETWEEN ADIPOSITY

AND IMMUNITY-INFLAMMATION

Obesity, particularly visceral adiposity, is associated

with chronic low-grade inflammation, as indicated by

increased levels of the inflammatory markers CRP and

IL-6 in the circulation of obese subjects. Although not

completely demonstrated, the current working hypothesis

is that adipokines, cytokines, and other factors produced

and released by WAT are responsible for the chronic

inflammatory state of visceral obesity. The concomitance

of inflammation, hypertension, and dyslipidemia increases

the likelihood of development of type II diabetes and

CVD.59 However, the ultimate reason for increased production

of proinflammatory factors byWATin obesity has

not been identified. Furthermore, visceral adiposity, rather

than simply a high body mass index (BMI; in kilograms"

I wonder if cold is any good for inflammation?

# Thread: High CRP + Labs

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs

### AmyJ — 2012-04-10T03:00:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-13194>

Hi everyone,

I could really use some help with the inflammation!! 51yo female, total hysterectomy 2008, no hormones then. Hashimotos. Gluten intolerant/not Celiac. 5'3.5" 173lb. Started Leptin Rx mid December and lost 15lbs which is phenomenal for me, I've never been able to lose more than 3-4lbs and would always gain it back + more. Cholesterol was 300 a year ago, too, 50mcg of Synthroid knocked it down to 230 in 5 months but I started going hyper. Dropped to 25mcg and up the cholesterol went again. At the time of the tests below I started on 60mg Armour and .5MG/.25MG E2/E3 and 50MG Progesterone, both sublingual. The original prescription was double but it made me very depressed/moody. I eat mostly keto, GF beef, shellfish, pastured pork, sometimes chicken. Not fond of veggies, no fruit, only dairy is GF ghee and occasional hard cheese. Once in a while a nightshade will slip in, as will a grain. I was traveling when that happened, just before the tests. Haven't had them in a while again. I've been spot CTing because it feels so good. I've been waking up between 2-3am with what feels like a surge of adrenalin, otherwise sleep ok. A 24 hr cortisol test will be coming up.

What is the most effective and quickest way to reduce the high CRP? How long will it take to get it down and are there other tests I should have? I'm not sure what's causing it but it's probably stress. I live in an area that was devastated by a wildfire last Labor Day weekend. I'm working on the mental aspects of the situation but I'm not quite where I should be.

VAP

Total LDL-D Direct 212 (=40 md/dL)

Total VLDL-C Direct 21 (

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-10T03:31:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-13200>

Your ratio of rT3 is too low (should be >20). This means some of your T4 that should be converting to T3 is actually getting converted to rT3. The FT3 should be in the top part of the range and yours is in the bottom part. The FT4 should be middle to highish in the range and it's in the bottom. All this tells you that

1) you have some underlying issue that is causing your rT3 and leaving you leptin resistant in the brain (might want to check your cortisol ...wake up times for cortisol surges are usually between 2-4am and get a full iron panel as your ferritin is super low.

2) you are probably not on enough thyroid meds. 60 mg is a very low dose in the scheme of Hashis. You need CT for sure!

The HA1c of 5.8 puts you at the pre-diabetic stage. CT will help this tremendously according to Dr. K. This number is the average of 90 days of your insulin so one time is not going to affect this. Something that you are eating is causing an insulin response. It may be the lactose in the cheese???? Maybe re-examine all the foods you eat from this perspective.

I'm not sure but I think both the Hashis and pre-diabetes can trigger a high CRP. Someone correct me if this is not right. Sorry can't help with hormones or cholesterol.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-13202>

Until you get your omega 6/3 ratio tested, try supplementing with krill oil. But you are on the right track. Be careful of meat that is labeled grass fed, but is fattened up before going to market with grains. I know this mostly is what Whole Foods sells, so you might be getting a lot of

omega 6/ low omega 3s with out knowing it. Just something to verify.

Back to read that blog...damn, I wish I could remember things! It's a miracle I still have a job. The fact that it's intuitive in large part helps a lot.

From what I understand the hsCRP is a tool that can identify potential cardiac disease - due to the levels of CRP protein in your blood. It isn't a true measure of inflammation, its just 1 piece of the puzzle. my CIMT showed inflammation but no plaque. inflammation can be a sign of rapid aging of the body - ie shortening telemores. Inflammation can be the result of what your eating, it can be influenced by environmental, and what epigenetic switches have been turned on.

Amy I've had great success just doing the LR from June to January - by then I had seen a reversal of leptin resistance - toward Leptin Sensitivity. In Feb I began CTing...and In March I added BHRT - btwn the 13 of March and this past monday I 've seen a drop in at least 7lbs & I'm mostly migraine free (not counting my stupidity from last night- causing one) I'd give the LR and CT a chance. if your doc's push statins and such - push back. Ask them to show you the journal articles where Statins are BENEFICIAL to WOMEN. (They can't show you any b/c there aren't any) Continue to push on diet/exercise and follow the LR - and discuss closer monitoring, Tell them You know that diet/exercise/weightloss will turn things around but its not something that's going to happen over night... tell them you're shooting for small slow weightloss - that in the end will result in HUGE POSITIVE GAINS in health. Figure how every long you been at weight X - it will take at least that long to get to a normal weight. Remember your doctor works for you, the recommend a treatment - but they CAN'T make you follow said treatment. IF you don't do what they recommend - what's the worst that can happen - they "Fire"

you as a patient - if so good - find a better doc, willing to work w/you. The best outcome - they work w/you , and you change their perspective on how they interact w/patients, and how they treat patients... and you become the starfish....

Probably differs from store to store. At mine the beef is grass fed and then the last 2 months the cows are fattened on vegetarian grains. At least that is what the WF butcher told me.

### mambo — 2012-04-29T21:22:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-crp-labs.762/post-19900>

The source of your IR is post-prandial, as is typical among women. Your BG stays elevated too long. My experience among ketogenic or VLC dieters who experience declining BG control is that their diet is way too acidic. If you're just eating meats and very little vegetables, your body's become way too acidic and this affects the functionality of your pancreatic beta cells. Combine this with physiological insulin resistance and you get prediabetes.

Add some veggies to your diet. You don't have to add safe starches but add broccoli, cauliflower, kale, and collard greens. Steam them if you can. Drink the juice after steaming them.

Your issue with inflammation is that you haven't optimized your thyroid function yet and there could be other sources causing inflammation: i.e., autoimmunity. Omega 6-3 imbalance is a red herring: it turns around slowly but should not affect your CRP much if you're already low-carbing. You have to look elsewhere and it could be your Hashimoto's, which overlaps with myriad other autoimmune conditions. That would be the avenue to look, not focusing on grass-fed/grass-finished beef. Ask for Anti Nuclear Antibodies.

> **Patty Cakes;13288 wrote:** My HA1c number just came back as 5.8 even though I haven been VLC for over 6 months.

**Posts:** 2

### Endless — 2012-04-10T09:05:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadians-where-to-get-these-labs-done.768/post-13279>

I can get basic bloodwork done at my doctor's office and it's covered under medical insurance provided I have symptoms or eligible risk factors....but nothing as extensive as I'm seeing here!

Where do I go to get this done, and how the heck do people afford to keep getting it done so often??

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-10T15:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadians-where-to-get-these-labs-done.768/post-13315>

> **Endless;13348 wrote:** I can get basic bloodwork done at my doctor's office and it's covered under medical insurance provided I have symptoms or eligible risk factors....but nothing as extensive as I'm seeing here!
>
> Where do I go to get this done, and how the heck do people afford to keep getting it done so often??

you find a doctor willing to order labs with the right marker so you don\`t have to pay (ie for thyroid s/he has to write fatigue as a reason) or you go to a naturopath and pay out of pocket and hope your extended covers the cost

however you should know that some of the labs just aren\`t drawn up here, RT3 for example is no longer available as a serum test the only way I\`ve seen to get it done is to go through a naturopath and have it ordered as a larger blood draw that is about $300

# Thread: Quickstart guide to Cold Thermogenesis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quickstart-guide-to-cold-thermogenesis.772/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Chrisgraff — 2012-04-10T14:14:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quickstart-guide-to-cold-thermogenesis.772/post-13299>

To avoid confusion, please refer to [the official quick-start guide.](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?834-Easy-Start-Guide-Leptin-Reset-and-Cold-Thermogenesis)

### Chrisgraff — 2012-04-10T14:16:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quickstart-guide-to-cold-thermogenesis.772/post-13300>

**Cold Thermogenesis

â€¢ best used with the diet & sleep protocols.

â€¢ should be added gradually.

â€¢ only for those with Omega6/Omega3 ratio of less than 10:1. (ideal ratio = 4:1)

**1\. Face dunks

â€¢ Remove facial products and/or makeup.

â€¢ 50-55ÂºF ice water in big pot or sink.

â€¢ Eat a meal high in fat/protein shortly before.

â€¢ Drink 16-32oz ice-cold water immediately before.

â€¢ Dunk/hold face in water as long as you can hold your breath.

â€¢ Never allow skin to fall below 50ÂºF â€“ Use Thermometer!

â€¢ Once daily for two weeks.

**2\. Wear compression shirt (ice, no water)

â€¢ tight compression shirt squeezes capillaries.

â€¢ Same preparation/precautions as face-dunking.

â€¢ Hold 20-40 lb bag of ice on torso (w/shirt).

â€¢ Increase 5 min each session (1 hour max).

â€¢ If skin temp/color gets too cold, take warm shower immediately.

**3\. Ice directly on skin (no water)**

â€¢ Same preparations/precautions as #2.

â€¢ Hold 20-40 lb bag of ice on torso (no shirt).

â€¢ Monitor skin temperature â€“ never lower than 50ÂºF.

â€¢ If you develop cold urticaria, stop CT.

4\. Cold showers**

â€¢ Optional transition to cold baths.

5\. Cold bath**

â€¢ Same preparations/precautions.

â€¢ Wear knitted hat, gloves, socks (for first week).

â€¢ Start with 68ÂºF to 72ÂºF tap-water (use unbagged ice if necessary).

â€¢ Gradually increase duration per session, (max 45 min then decrease temp).

â€¢ Gradually decrease temperature per session.

â€¢ Water never colder than 50ÂºF (thermometer).

Supplements to ease transition**

â€¢ Fish oil + Krill oil

â€¢ Bitter melon, (two hours prior)

â€¢ Matcha green tea powder stirred in cold water prior to CT.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-10T18:26:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quickstart-guide-to-cold-thermogenesis.772/post-13375>

My suggestion is to not progress too quickly. It takes the body a couple weeks to start adapting well. Don't get burned out before you even begin to see benefits!

### AKMan — 2012-04-10T22:20:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quickstart-guide-to-cold-thermogenesis.772/post-13461>

Great post! I would like to add a few things if you don't mind.

Ice baths - if 72 is too cold, start with 85 degree water and work your way down. The thermo-neutral point of water is said to be around 92 degrees (meaning your body is perfectly comfortable). At 80 degrees, your body HAS to increase metabolism to adapt to the water temp. If you can only do 70-80 degree water, that's OK, take it slow.

Signs - Your body will tell you if this is working. The first sign is goosebumps; when you see them, it means your body is reacting to the cold stimulus. The second is shivering; once you start shivering, your body is telling you it's too cold and is trying to warm up by rapidly contracting muscles. This is thought to be counter-productive to increasing BAT which acts in the non-shivering zone. Thirdly - white, numb fingers&toes. These are cause by constriction of the blood vessels to your extremities; it's the body's way of shutting off flow to unimportant areas to conserve core temp. This is a fairly normal, fairly harmless sign that you are adapting to the cold. If it happens, don't freak, flail and 'windmill' your arms around to get the blood back, but it should return to normal in 30 min to 1 hour on it's own. If numbness persists or fingers turn purple--see a doctor.

Hypothermia - A real danger! It can happen fairly quick in water colder than 60 degrees. First to go is your mental judgement, you may not realize what is happening until it's too late. Make sure you have a spotter with cold water. Nausea, dizzyness, mental confusion are all symptoms.

This from Wikipedia: Heat is lost more quickly in water\[18\] than on land. Water temperatures that would be quite reasonable as outdoor air temperatures can lead to hypothermia. A water temperature of 10 Â°C (50 Â°F) often leads to death in one hour, and water temperatures hovering at freezing can lead to death in as little as 15 minutes.\[32\] Water at a temperature of 26 Â°C (79 Â°F) will, after prolonged exposure, lead to hypothermia.\[33\]

### nonchalant — 2012-04-10T23:53:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quickstart-guide-to-cold-thermogenesis.772/post-13490>

### AKMan — 2012-04-11T00:21:09+0300

### Guest — 2012-04-20T00:09:14+0300

### AKMan — 2012-04-21T00:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quickstart-guide-to-cold-thermogenesis.772/post-17220>

Here's a recommendation for newbies to CT. For your very first CT session, find a container big enough to stand in. Fill it with ice-cold water. Preferably knee-deep. Get that water as cold as you can, 32-33 degrees. Put in ice and cold water, the ice shouldn't melt, that's how cold you want it. You can even get it colder with salt! 31 degrees would be perfect.

Now, stand in the water. Note the feeling. It will feel like pin-pricks. The skin will turn brite red in about 30 seconds. Wiggle your toes. In about 2 minutes, they will be hard to wiggle as you lose motor control. Note how your ankles hurt, that's because they aren't protected by much muscle or fat. The pain will go away in a few minutes and numbness will take over.

Your first reaction will be to GET OUT. You may suck in your breath involuntarily, your heart may race. You might scream a little at first. Try to stay in at least 5 minutes. During this time, focus on your breathing. Pay attention to what this coldness feels like.

When you get out, let your feet drip-dry. Pay attention to how long it takes the redness to go away. You may notice goosebumps on your legs. A toe or two may turn white and go completely numb several minutes after you get out. This is OK, it will go away. If several toes go very numb, and you want feeling back quickly, soak your feet in some warm water for a few minutes. This is cause by your blood vessels constricting. You may also notice you start to shiver several minutes after getting out--usually not during. This is caused by the blood vessels opening back up and dumping that cold blood in your core, your body senses it and puts muscles to work to warm you up.

OK, so what did we just accomplish? You have just braved water that is as cold as any human (or any land mammal) has ever felt. Ever. Water has never been colder than this. And you survived. Do this a couple more times and you will find you can jump right in under a perfectly controlled situation. No yelping, gasping, wanting to jump out. Now that you know how the coldest water you will ever encounter feels, you are ready to proceed.

For your first big plunge, try 60 degree water--it will be like child's play compared to 32 degrees! I guarantee you will soon be down to 50 degrees and lower, and you will never get that nagging thought out of your head, 'what would a whole-body plunge at 32 degrees be like?'

I'm here to tell you, I did it this week--after CT'ing for about 2 months...and...It was awesome! The first night I stayed for 15 minutes, then warmed the water up to 55 degrees for another 30 minutes. The next night, I went 33 degrees for 45 minutes. Your legs will be stiff and you will shiver when you get out, but at least you'll know you can do it!

NOTE: Please ice responsibly, have a spotter and learn the signs of hypothermia--it's not worth die-ing over!

### David — 2012-04-21T01:53:55+0300

The link is to a really good article that explains pretty much everything. The grains cause alcohol and acetic acid. Alcohol gets you drunk and acetic acid cleans your ears.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-10T18:35:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-your-own-fermented-vegetables.749/post-13377>

Chocolate, adding turmeric and such is a great idea. If the pickles are sliced, or quartered, it only takes a few days at room temp for them to ferment, a week at most. Then you put them in the fridge and the process slows down almost to a stop. They taste great, kinda sparkly like carbonated water was added to them.

I haven't made any whole pickles, but this year I plan on fermenting some baby cukes.

### Mylittlelambs — 2012-04-13T08:51:10+0300

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-13T15:46:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-your-own-fermented-vegetables.749/post-14669>

I have done a lot of fermenting over the years. Here are my index of recipes. The are some recommendations for books too. My favorite fermented food is kimchi; it also lasts the best and always makes it to the spring. I find fermented pickles are wonderful but must be eaten within 1-2 months of fermenting, earlier is better, otherwise they get soft and nasty. I've tried grape leaves and horseradish leaves. My husband wants to can pickles next year:

[http://www.eatkamloops.org/specific-carbohydrate-diet-scd-recipes/](http://http://www.eatkamloops.org/specific-carbohydrate-diet-scd-recipes/)

# Thread: A Handy Guide For O3 Content  Mercury In Seafood
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-handy-guide-for-o3-content-mercury-in-seafood.775/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-04-10T17:52:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-handy-guide-for-o3-content-mercury-in-seafood.775/post-13361>

Saw this today and thought it was awesome. I love the format it's in.. it makes it really easy to pick the best seafood.. especially for those of us on a budget.

[http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/2012/04/03/gIQABd16sS\_graphic.html?tid=sm\_gplus\_wap](http://http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/2012/04/03/gIQABd16sS_graphic.html?tid=sm_gplus_wap)

### Dan in Utah — 2012-04-10T18:19:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-handy-guide-for-o3-content-mercury-in-seafood.775/post-13373>

> **Lovelife;13430 wrote:** Saw this today and thought it was awesome. I love the format it's in.. it makes it really easy to pick the best seafood.. especially for those of us on a budget.
>
>
>
> [http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/2012/04/03/gIQABd16sS\_graphic.html?tid=sm\_gplus\_wap](http://http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/2012/04/03/gIQABd16sS_graphic.html?tid=sm_gplus_wap)

Holy Mackerel, lovelife! Thanks for posting this. Very informative.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-10T19:18:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-handy-guide-for-o3-content-mercury-in-seafood.775/post-13402>

Naturally occurring selenium in fish also binds to mercury and helps reduce accumulation. Per [Chris Kresser](http://http://chriskresser.com/selenium-the-missing-link-for-treating-hypothyroidism), other sources of selenium include: brazil nuts, crimini mushrooms, cod, shrimp, tuna, halibut, salmon, scallops, chicken, eggs, shiitake mushrooms, lamb, and turkey.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-11T14:35:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-handy-guide-for-o3-content-mercury-in-seafood.775/post-13694>

Np Dan. I love it.. it's just so easy and helpful in the infographic form.

Don: I'm not saying we should be super worried about mercury from fish, though some people seem to have more issues than others. There is also a point where you stop accumulating mercury. But, I'd still stay away from shark and the ones at the bottom as those are known to cause mercury issues in the Japanese who obviously already eat a lot of fish. I eat brazil nuts for the added selenium just in case there isn't enough in the fish. Also, don't forget the cilantro that Dr. Kruse suggests that will help chelate the mercury for us. As selenium just seems to make it inert, but I'm not positive it actually removes it. Also, this is a great guide just for picking the best Omega 3 fish even if you're not concerned w/ mercury.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-12T00:43:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-handy-guide-for-o3-content-mercury-in-seafood.775/post-13951>

I really liked that visual chart of omega 3 and contamination. Give some thought to the health of your gut flora. Our gut flora is the first line of defeat against infection and contamination. Here is Dr Natasha Campbell-McBride talking about healthy gut flora. She deals with autistic and spectrum disorders at her clinic in the UK and has a lot of experience with heavy metal contamination:

[http://www.eatkamloops.org/what-is-a-healthy-gut/](http://http://www.eatkamloops.org/what-is-a-healthy-gut/)

### nonchalant — 2012-06-27T23:22:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-handy-guide-for-o3-content-mercury-in-seafood.775/post-34038>

The chart doesn't have shrimp on it.

### Christy — 2012-04-10T21:35:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/index-by-date.782/post-13445>

I understand for research purposes the index alphabetically, is helpful---

however, having arrived late to the party myself, I quickly realized that a number of the blogs build on specific information contained in the blog or blogs posted prior to it. And therefore, although it is convenient to have them organized alphabetically, new people will soon find they need them organized by date, for the sake of continuity in order to follow with regard to all the new information.

just an thought

# Thread: Heidi needs Hashi's / hormone help
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heidi-needs-hashis-hormone-help.783/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2012-04-10T21:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heidi-needs-hashis-hormone-help.783/post-13447>

I have a friend who's 30 yr-old daughter is in trouble - Hashi's.

I'm not up on female hormones so much, but I can see some things in her email below - too much processed, chicken, little beef, nuts, cereal, and Lean Cuisine for gawd's sake.

Lots of TSH tests don't show us much, and the iron testing is incomplete, natch. T3 and T4 "normal"? We'll be the judge of that.

Can I get some feedback for her? I am planning to get pique her interest with some good solid answers and get her here to Optimal and join us!

Martin

Symptoms: I have been plagued for years with dry skin, eczema, constipation, hemorrhoids, frequent urination, lack of libido, and very irregular mestral cycles with heavy bleeding and cramping. I have also noticed that my hands and feet tend to stay cold all the time (as a child, I used to wear gloves at times while doing my schoolwork) and I get overheated easily if I am exposed to the sun. I am also tired ALL the time. I never feel rested. In addition, I am hungry frequently and get shaky sometimes if I miss a meal. I was on birth control pills to regulate my periods for 10 years and it worked, but I hated having to take a synthetic hormone, so I stopped them. What recently led me to the endocrinologist was my OB/GYN, as that I went to her with the complain of having my period or spotting most all month. Most months I may only go a week or so without it. She said I needed to take out stock in Kotex, ha! She did check my iron and it was normal. This past month has been a blessing. After having it for 35 days straight, I went 25 without it and then only had it for two. I hope this isn't too much detail (sorry)! The OB/GYN ruled out any gyno-related problems that might be causing my symptoms through ultrasound, like ovarian cysts etc.

Diet: My diet changes frequently and really should be healthier. I go through phases where I try to eat healthy and those where I eat junk. Since there are only two of us, we eat out a lot. Breakfast is usually coffee and one of the following banana, yogurt w/ fruit or granola, granola bar, or occasionally a chicken biscuit from Bojangles (once a month at most). Lunch is generally a salad from a fast food joint, veggie wrap from a local coffee shop, frozen meal (lean cuisine or healthy choice type), or burrito from a local shop (the kind where you build your own). Dinners vary greatly. Generally I eat sushi, pizza, burgers & fries, japanese hibachi, grilled chicken, chicken tenders, large dinner salads, pasta dishes, or grilled fish. I also love popcorn, which I make on the stove and just add salt. I also like sweets, but don't eat them daily or anything. For a snack I may have a handfull of nuts, bowl of cereal, fresh fruit, baby carrots, some pretzels, etc. Beverages include water, vitamin water, and tea maybe once a month, soda maybe three times a month, and I do drink alcohol maybe two to three drinks a week (glass of wine in the evening or a drink on the weekend kind of thing).

Tests: My TSH has been checked 4 different times. The first was years ago and all I know is that it was "normal". It was checked back in Oct, at which time it was 3.6 and the OB/GYN said that was normal (although the endo said it was high in his opinion). When he checked it (Feb) it was 1.6 and I just had it checked again by my general physician two and a half weeks ago and it was 1.7. My T3 and T4 have always been normal too, as far as I have been told. I went to the OB/GYN back in Oct and asked them to run a variety of hormone tests to try to figure out my lack of energy and lack of libido. All of them came back "normal". I asked my general physician to check my calcium and iodine levels and just called today and found out that they were "normal" as well.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-11T18:23:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heidi-needs-hashis-hormone-help.783/post-13777>

I am no expert but it sounds like she may have sex steroid hormone imbalance resulting in some adrenal fatigue. There have been hormonal issues for years if she has had to be on BC pills. So what is a normal range for a lab? Most doctors use incredibly wide ranges that almost any result would be normal. Some good books for reference points on female sex hormones are Dr. Uzzi Reiss and Dr. Dzugan. It appears the thyroid is struggling but may only be due to the adrenals and other hormones. Progesterone greatly effects thyroid and sometimes women just need a dab of progesterone to get their thyroid operating correctly. I suggest she start doing some reading to give herself knowledge and empowerment. While she is doing this she should definitely start the Leptin Rx which will help her with the energy (it gave me way more energy and I have Hashis and had adrenal issues). With Hashis many of us, if not all, have at the very least gluten intolerance but many have Celiacs. The Leptin Rx will give her an eating plan to allow her to get away from grains. CT would be great for her thyroid and adrenals too!

Plan of action:

1\. Start Leptin Rx - eliminate all grains NOW

2\. Get adrenal saliva coritsol test

3\. Read some books by a few hormone experts. Check her results to the range the experts provide.

4\. Start CT for energy and detox.

5\. Probably needs a full thyroid panel w/ rT3 (rT3 and FT3 need to be done at the same time for accurate ratio)

She has a lot going on and there is a lot to do but I hope this helps.

### Martin — 2012-04-14T14:58:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heidi-needs-hashis-hormone-help.783/post-15034>

Thanks Patty Cakes, great info to pass on to her. I think she is a researcher and reading up on Dr. Uzzi Reiss and Dr. Dzugan's work, and then tying it all together with Dr. Kruse's work should give her some relief.

Martin

# Thread: Optimal Deep Frying?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-deep-frying.786/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-04-10T22:58:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-deep-frying.786/post-13471>

I know this is somewhat weird as frying I think is something you want to stay away from when trying to be optimal...but my family isn't quite on the bandwagon...and even I from time to time eat things that aren't great. So whats the best option for using a deep fryer that won't break the bank. I found 5 gallons of Grass-fed Beef Tallow at USWellness meats for 105.00...I'm not sure how long that would last or even sure if its ok to use that in a fryer.

I know Coconut oil seems way too expensive to use in a fryer....but I'm not really sure how much you need to use in one so or how long it would last between replacements. Any and all advice is welcome. I'd like to get my family to use something better than standard vegetable oil. We don't fry things that often really but when we do it would be nice to have something better than junk vegetable oil.

Also sources to get stuff besides USWellness would be great they are out of the beef tallow I can't even get it there even if I wanted to at the moment.

### shilohman — 2012-04-10T23:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-deep-frying.786/post-13474>

At 105.00, that is expensive. I would say you are better of using non-grass fed beef tallow than any type of vegetable oil. Sounds good though.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-04-10T23:30:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-deep-frying.786/post-13482>

Non-grass fed beef tallow is probably still healthier than vegetable I guess....is beef tallow the best in regards to frying? I know you can also get other animal fats for frying too? If anyone knows a good site which compares cooking fats and lists whats good for what and why that would be great.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-10T23:46:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-deep-frying.786/post-13487>

I don't think deep fat frying became popular before the cheap, vegetable oils came on the scene. I fry french fries some times, but I use a small pot and lard or tallow. I render my own lard/tallow, it's easy, and I store it in the freezer.

### Adriana — 2012-04-10T23:51:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-deep-frying.786/post-13489>

I've been craving beef fondue...I think I'll look for beef tallow or avocado oil. Cannot deal with the thought of coconut oil flavored beef.

# Thread: Carb confusion
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-confusion.797/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mei — 2012-04-11T06:08:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-confusion.797/post-13641>

Hiya,

so. Some musings and earnest questions - stream of consciousness style! Any pointers or co-musers are graciously welcomed!

I'm down with the low g/per day carb cut-off, and thought I was fine with a bite/spoonful of something bad so long as I didn't go over the limit. I recently clued in (doiiii!) that lots of "good" stuff is pretty carb-y...nuts, coconut, veggies... So whereas I thought I was between 30 - 50 g, I was probably over 100.

I feel a little betrayed, having been devouring coconut butter/mana/concentrate while ignoring the nutritional label. I'm wondering if, indeed, carbohydrates of every kind are included in this number. If so, how does one go about knowing the carb content of produce, given variations according to size, type, soil, season, growing methods, etc...

To trigger ketosis, do we pretty much just eat animal products, then?

I'm also having a hard time eating in season, as what's "in season" at the local farmer's market isn't at all what would grow naturally here, or it's what's been in cold-storage all winter... AND I had a realization that preserving food, though oh so quaint, pragmatic, and delicious, technically interferes with circadian rhythms.

How precise do we need to be for optimum health (food type, timing, location as some factors) - I'm game to go as intense as required, but would take the leeway if it was there  Starting CT just as spring hits has me confused as to whether I should eat in season and continue CTing, eat in season and STOP CTing, or continue with a winter diet while CTing.

I've got, I don't know, 10 - 15 more lbs to lose, seem to be plateauing at this point with VLC paleo, random fasting, and an hour of CT at or below 50 degrees per day, hence the nitpicking questions. Also, I might just be impatient (I've been feeling amazing, hitting 4 weeks of CT pretty soon...lost weight, gained muscle, started sleeping like a pro, etc. initially but now I'm getting greedy waiting for more).

Maybe I'll be quiet now. Like I said, a penny for your thoughts....

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-11T06:15:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-confusion.797/post-13644>

I can't answer all your questions, but here is what I understand:

You definitely eat seasonally (which I would assume means what is growing/able to grow at that time of year in your geographical region). In terms of CT, you need to CT in summer IF you are trying to reverse illness/disease of any sort. If you do this, then I think you'd back your carbs right down to winter levels and eat winter-type carbs. If you don't have disease, then you can just do CT according to the seasons as well, which would mean lots in winter and not as much in summer. Though even in summer, you can keep the AC on in the car, etc.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-11T06:57:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carb-confusion.797/post-13654>

> **KiwiLauren;13713 wrote:** Though even in summer, you can keep the AC on in the car, etc.

It always strikes me as funny that we can keep the AC which is a much more modern invention of man than heat........

# Thread: CT guide clarification
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-guide-clarification.798/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kiera2 — 2012-04-11T06:25:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-guide-clarification.798/post-13647>

Face Dunking:

What you need: skin thermometer, a timer, ice, and a bathroom sink or larger bowl.

1.Remove make-up or facial products

2.Get the water between 50-55 degrees

3.Dunk your face into the ice water and hold as long as you can

4.Record time and dunk again

5.Continue for the next two weeks and work your way to using less breaths

6.DO NOT let your temperature fall below 50-55 degrees

Does #6 mean the temperature of your skin, or the temperature of the water?

### admin — 2012-04-11T16:51:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-guide-clarification.798/post-13725>

Your skin temperature.

### Chrisgraff — 2012-04-11T18:18:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-guide-clarification.798/post-13769>

Clarification #2: Drink cold water before or after CT?

CT Protocol says, "Also **drink 16-32 oz. of ice cold water immediately prior** to the test no matter what stage you are at."

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-11T18:19:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-guide-clarification.798/post-13772>

>

# Thread: overdoing CT? and other questions!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overdoing-ct-and-other-questions.805/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-11T17:24:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overdoing-ct-and-other-questions.805/post-13744>

can one over-do CT?

although I am by no means in this category, I wonder if one can do it too much?

thus far I have been doing 30 min in 58F water immersed up to waist and sometimes up to chest.

Its been only 8 days. I plan on doing it 5-7 days a week as time permits for 30-60 min.

not sure if I am really cold adapted. I read that it takes 2 weeks but I also read that it takes 12-14 months. so that is a bit confusing as well.

also, Jack has mentioned that CT should be done seasonally, as in late fall, winter months. I guess that is when one is healthy, and lean.

is there harm in doing it year round once one is "optimal" or at least healthy??

I know this is all very early in the game...

### shilohman — 2012-04-11T17:45:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overdoing-ct-and-other-questions.805/post-13754>

If you are trying to lose weight or fix an autoimmune disease, then CT as much as possible. If you are just trying to stay optimal, then do it seasonally.

How to tell when you are cold adapted? From my experience:

1\. Can control shivering, so it does not happen

2\. Only take cold showers now, no hot water

3\. Shave with only cold water

4\. And this is probably the biggest, when I exercise I don't get winded like I use to i.e. ketogenic

When I started CT I never pushed it. I just did a little at a time. When I read of others who shiver for long periods after CT, I think they are overdoing it. In fact, I wouldn't be surprised if they are prolonging their goal of being cold adapted.

Like a friend use to tell me when I was a kid, "take it easy greasy, you have a long way to slide."

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-11T18:01:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overdoing-ct-and-other-questions.805/post-13762>

> **shilohman;13823 wrote:** If you are trying to lose weight or fix an autoimmune disease, then CT as much as possible. If you are just trying to stay optimal, then do it seasonally.
>
>
>
> How to tell when you are cold adapted? From my experience:
>
> 1\. Can control shivering, so it does not happen
>
> 2\. Only take cold showers now, no hot water
>
> 3\. Shave with only cold water
>
> 4\. And this is probably the biggest, when I exercise I don't get winded like I use to i.e. ketogenic
>
>
>
> When I started CT I never pushed it. I just did a little at a time. When I read of others who shiver for long periods after CT, I think they are overdoing it. In fact, I wouldn't be surprised if they are prolonging their goal of being cold adapted.
>
>
>
> Like a friend use to tell me when I was a kid, "take it easy greasy, you have a long way to slide."

I have been doing it for at least a month . . . now in a pool approximately 64 degrees 5x/week.

I mostly take cold showers and still shiver afterwards. I like the shivering. Sometimes I stand in front the mirror as I dry through evaporation and watch how it affects my body . . . pretty interesting. I am cold adapting, but adapted? No.

There have been changes. Usually after I come out of the pool, I lay in the sun and let the D in. Even then, I some times shiver (Southern California Sun). I will probably continue to shiver since the cold adapting is progressive and I will need to go to a colder source soon.

I plan to do this through the summer or until my body tells me otherwise. Swimming is now one of my favorite exercise. I must get winded since I swim fast, sprint, and do resistance exercise. Normal non-HIIT does not seem to wind me anymore.

The biggest thing I notice is the feeling that my immunity is overclocked, kind of like that teenager invulnerability feeling we once had. I do sleep better even though my wake cycle is not optimum due to work. The cold does seem to help with that too to some extent. That I plan on changing in the future. Cold and dark is my goal.

So much of what we use to do as a society make so much sense now: The military using red filtered flashlights to read and see. Early to bed, early to rise mindset. Gotta go back . . . .

### agatha — 2012-04-11T23:45:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overdoing-ct-and-other-questions.805/post-13924>

Some of us have found that our bodies can't keep up with clearing the detox symptoms if we do too much too soon. This is miserable.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-12T00:45:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overdoing-ct-and-other-questions.805/post-13953>

> **agatha;13993 wrote:** Some of us have found that our bodies can't keep up with clearing the detox symptoms if we do too much too soon. This is miserable.

I agree. I lost 130 pounds from June 1, 2010 through April 14, 2011 and cleaned up my body via GCBC, paleo, and other wellness principles before Jack came onto the scene. Now its about tweaking the road to optimization for me.

The road can be bumpy. Enjoy the ride and take notes. They will come in handy for others. Believe me. My bowel movements did not reset for 8 months. When it happened I was like, WHEW . . . glad that was over.

Now I realize there was some serious problems with my gut PRIOR all the changes. The changes only illuminated this.

Experimenting and experiencing unexpected changes like that has proven very helpful. I would not change it for the world.

# Thread: Green light reduces melatonin too?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pardus — 2012-04-11T17:39:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-13752>

Hi everyone,

I saw these articles : [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/1896724](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/1896724) , [https://sleep.med.harvard.edu/news/356/Green+Light+Affects+Circadian+Rhythm](http://https://sleep.med.harvard.edu/news/356/Green+Light+Affects+Circadian+Rhythm)

that say green light will also reduce melatonin secretion.

If you have amber/orange glasses that only block blue, but not green as well, it might be possible that you are still messing with your circadian cycle if you look at light sources at night.

It might be that the only safe bet for sure would be red glasses.

Any thoughts?

### Marsha — 2012-04-11T18:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-13780>

That's why I bought these instead of the orange/yellow ones :

Laser glasses/goggles that block blue and green light.

[http://www.amazon.com/Bosch-DLA001-Laser-View-Enhancing-Glasses/dp/B003BNY7N4/ref=cm\_cr\_pr\_product\_top](http://http://www.amazon.com/Bosch-DLA001-Laser-View-Enhancing-Glasses/dp/B003BNY7N4/ref=cm_cr_pr_product_top)

They are darker and maybe harder to watch TV/Ipad etc. - everything is red/gray/black - darker value red of the F. Lux.

I can still read a book before sleeping, but find I get sleepier sooner when using these, so don't get as much reading done as I used to!

They just fit over my glasses.

Also great for CTing in the tub after the sun has gone down (but with light on, so I can see when I get out)!

### Cron23 — 2012-04-11T20:43:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-13839>

And I just got my blue blockers yesterday.... Thanks for the link!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-11T21:26:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-13859>

> **Cron23;13908 wrote:** And I just got my blue blockers yesterday.... Thanks for the link!

Auuu poop. It's neverending! This rabbit hole might go all the way to china.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-09T02:23:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63046>

I just ordered these as the ones above are out of stock.

DEWALT DW0714 Laser Enhancement Glasses

[http://amzn.com/B00093DJ4M](http://http://amzn.com/B00093DJ4M)

And to think I dropped $90 on the pair from lowbluelights.com. (But unlike the orange and these red ones, the LBL ones do fit over glasses well).

Will update with a review in a few days.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-09T04:19:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63065>

Good, let us know how they work. I'm particularly interested in whether they block enough light that would come in over the top and around the sides.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-09T20:14:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63176>

Got them in this morning. They look a little different than in the picture on Amazon. The arms are made of the same red plastic as the lenses. The nose piece is actually simpler but more sturdily constructed than the Amazon picture suggests. The whole thing is a dark red.

Coverage is excellent....without glasses on. Some leakage around the sides if I deliberately look hard in those directions, but otherwise nothing appreciable. Better coverage than the fit-over glasses from LowBlueLights.com.

The tight facial contouring makes it look like contemporary sportsy/stylized sunglasses (eg, Oakleys). This achieved my purpose to have blue and green blocking while wearing contacts at work (5pm-11pm shift) and not look too clunky/unprofessional.

These do not provide adequate coverage while fitted over eyeglasses, however. So I may order a similar pair in a fit-over frame to replace my LBL pair.

The DeWALT 0714 Laser Enhancement Glasses do completely block out blue and green, even yellow and orange, too. Your world becomes shades of red, actually. The LBL pair blocked blue absolutely, but still allowed all colors up through the greens.

Will update again after wearing these regularly for a couple of weeks to see if there's any appreciable effects on my circadian cycles. My job is mostly outside (security guard), but does periodically expose me to fluorescent lighting, from which I notice a very strong green component through my LBL glasses.

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/65028=545-ImageUploadedByTapatalk1352483303.335776.jpg)

### janagram — 2012-11-09T20:43:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63181>

I wonder. Ok, green is made from blue and yellow. Blue is not good. So therefore green is also not good. The same would be true for purple, inasmuch as it is blue and red. I suspect that blublockers will block that blue in these secondary colors as well.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-10T01:12:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63243>

IIRC, the research has shown that that 472nm is where the photoreceptive ganglion cells are most sensitive.

Light color is a spectrum of wavelengths. Blue is right around 470.

[http://www.gamonline.com/catalog/colortheory/spectrum.gif](http://http://www.gamonline.com/catalog/colortheory/spectrum.gif)

As you can see, Green spans from just under 500 to 575nm or so.

I wear the orange Uvex glasses. (Skyper 1933X) What I liked about these is I get a chart telling me exactly what light and how much they block: (Page 14)

[http://www.uvex.us/uploadedFiles/Uvex\_Lens\_Tech\_Broch(1).pdf](http://http://www.uvex.us/uploadedFiles/Uvex_Lens_Tech_Broch\(1\).pdf)

As you can see, they basically block all colors below 540, and most light up to 560 or so. By 600 nm, it’s barely doing anything. But 600 is yellow light.

So one question I have about this study: What’s “green” light? What was the spectoral makup of the light? Was there some blue in it as well? (Like how a CFL emits different colors at the same time)

Or just green? How much green? Is it green light ONLY, but green light that’s at 500nm? Might make more sense (and not matter for Uvex wearers).

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-10T04:54:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63292>

The blue light receptors involved with modulating circadian cycles that we all talk about are specialized and not involved in vision. The second article linked to by OP said that cone receptors most sensitive to green (~550nm) that are involved in vision also

produce a similar(albeit smaller, more quickly attenuating) modulative effect. This is what's surprising.....up to now it was though it was a separate system.

### Endless — 2012-11-10T05:18:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63295>

I'll stick with my Uvex Orange....this is good enough for me;

> SCT-Orange offers a wide range
>
> of spectral protection. It absorbs
>
> \>99.9% of potentially harmful
>
> UVA and UVB radiation. It further
>
> provides protection by completely
>
> absorbing visible light up to 540nm,
>
> which includes violet, blue and
>
> certain green wavelengths of light
>
> which are emitted by curing lamps.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-10T05:25:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63296>

So, I've had the laser glasses on for about two hours now (the sun went down at around 5pm here). It's weird, like being in a horror movie.  Definitely blocks way more light than standard blue blockers.....it is darker and harder to see. And I am already getting the need-to-go-bed-soon feeling, whereas with the standard blue blockers this feeling typically comes much later. Whether this is due to the addition of blocking green light or overall less light hitting my eyes (or both), I don't know....but it is having an effect.

Four more hours of work to go, but I'm getting the urge to pull a Costanza-style work-nap.

Earlier, while doing a patrol, some kid on the street asked me if I was an x-men cyclops because of the red glasses.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-10T05:40:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63300>

> **Endless;65159 wrote:** I'll stick with my Uvex Orange....this is good enough for me;

That may end up being the best compromise. While the red laser glasses definitely eliminate the chance that anything in the neighborhood just above 540nm might screw around with the SCN, they also block a LOT of light, to the point that it can be difficult to see in low-light situations.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-11T05:59:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63539>

key point to all the numbers...

enough light at any frequency will reset the SCN .......

the melanopsin receptors are directly wired, so less is required, and the green cones are most sensiitive to light 'in general' (you see green best),

it's all wired in proximity,, think photoptarmosis

anything short of turning off the light is the equivalent of taking a supplement.

### Jude — 2012-11-11T07:23:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63546>

> **Entelechy;65413 wrote: key point to all the numbers...
>
> enough light at any frequency will reset the SCN .......
>
> the melanopsin receptors are directly wired, so less is required, and the green cones are most sensiitive to light 'in general' (you see green best),
>
> it's all wired in proximity,, think photoptarmosis
>
> anything short of turning off the light is the equivalent of taking a supplement**.

Did a week with newly bought Amazon blue blockers, which certainly blocked the blue light from my modem. Had a week of difficulty in getting to sleep and didn't know why. Read this yesterday and decided to go back to my old sunnies last pm and see if there was a difference. Noted that I can still see muted blue lights on my modem with the 'oldies' on, but they provided a much darker terrain. But... when I stared yawning and feeling sleepy(first time in a week), I knew! .....had a great sleep. What gives?

Perhaps the old darkies cut out more of the green light?

### caroline — 2012-11-11T07:27:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63547>

I would be interested in knowing this too. My blue blockers were working great - with in an hour or two I would get really drowsy and want to go to sleep.....now it isn't happening at all.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-11T08:35:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-63550>

Well, the red laser glasses certainly block blue, leave green lights extremely faint (doesn't totally cut them), and makes everything in general much darker. So they're a win for most purposes.

But as Entelechy pointed out, any bright light exposure at any wavelength will screw things up. Learned that the hard way one time when accidentally turning the bathroom light on at night.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-18T02:59:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-65064>

I bought the red laser glasses (from Ace Tool; Amazon was out) and am not very impressed. They definitely block blue & green, which is great, and make lights darker which I like. However, for me, the fit is bad. They let in a lot of light underneath and around the sides. I tried wearing them on top of the Uvex orange but that was a total fail (but quite comical looking according to DH). If I could get the red lenses in a more wrap-around style, that would be perfect.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T03:16:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-65066>

blue light, up to 480nm is most important to block.

rather than trying to raise the cut off frequency to include green, my recommendation would be to find darker tinted glasses, with the same 540nm cut off point.

the lower transitivity will reduce ALL light getting to the eye.

Uvex have a safety glass with a similar profile to Uvex SCT orange..

it's a brown lens called Espresso, much darker with a VLT of only 15% compared to 45% for the Orange.

it only blocks 97 percent of the blue light, (note, as a percentage) but to blocks far more light in general. wear the brown ones when I cannot avoid brighter ambient light after dark. the orange ones suffice most of the time, in conjunction with dimmed lighting.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-18T05:18:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-65103>

I got some red laser glasses in the fit-over style for use with glasses. They are certainly way more leaky around the edges, but not horrible. The LBL pair fits over glasses the best.

My job exposes me to pretty intense green light (fluorescent hallway lighting in which the green component is VERY dominant when wearing blue blockers, LED traffic lights, among other strong sources), so for my purposes deliberately blocking green is needed. It should be noted that even the red laser glasses fail to block all green; rather, they just dim it dramatically. Which is probably a good thing, as it allows me to still see traffic light indicators while taking out the bothersome green from fluorescent lights. And the red laser glasses do significantly dim all light down, sometimes to the point that it's difficult to see and causing me ponder switching back to the LBLs.

Others' needs may differ. The brown tint idea is interesting.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T05:55:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-65105>

a thought then.. how about film gels?

they can be ordered in numerous tints, and cut to fit your existing glasses.

[here](http://http://amasci.com/amateur/irgoggl.html) is a guy who used three layers of Congo Blue on one layer of Primary Red to make some goggles that can only see infra red!

yeah, humans can see slightly into the infra red, you just never notice because the rest of the light is too strong.

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/67109=577-leefltr4.gif)

### x_danny_x — 2012-11-18T08:09:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-65107>

wow, what is the point of those Entelechy? reading that link of yours, the guy mentions it is for sunny days in the daytime.

it is possible to see with them if there is a cloudy day, so you can forget to use them at night in your home.

might as well try the Espresso or the red ones. i keep seeing those with blue coated ones and also gray-blue coated ones.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T17:10:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-65134>

my point was to select film gels specifically for the intended application, and cut them to go over an existing pair of glasses.

Bill Beaty made his to see infra red, to give a unique visual perspective of the world around him, because that's just fricken cool to nerdy types like me.

I've done some infrared photography back in the days of film, and it was incredible how different the world looks in adjusted spectrums. We experience our visual world in such a tiny slice of the electromagnetic spectrum. other creatures have different visual chemistry, and see an entirely different world. (i.e. some plain flowers are variegated to other wavelengths)

as for Uvex, here is a [pdf](http://http://uvex.us/uploadedFiles/ProductConfiguration/ProductLiterature/Uvex_Lens_Tech_Update2011.pdf) that includes spectrum charts and data for all their safety glasses.

keep in mind the frequency notch you want, and the VLT percentage.

you can see how optimal the SCT orange are to the application, with a very sharp cut off and good light transmission so you don't need a bright environment to see. The espresso, and gold mirror, let some light through at 500nm, but are much darker so overall less photons will make it to the retina in brighter environments. The charts also show why the simple amber glasses are ineffective, even thought the cut off point appears sufficient.

if you go something like the cobalt blue, they block out a lot of everything, but with a VLT of only 0.2% you won't see anything at all.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T17:31:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-65141>

here is a page of theatrical [gels](http://http://www.prolightingsupply.com/apollogel.html), listing their spectral response.

They can be cut with scissors to fit any glasses or goggles, and layered as well.

the Apollo 1800 maybe better suited to ones particular application?

### Lyndra — 2012-11-18T18:26:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-65154>

Thanks, I just ordered some of the theatrical gels. I'll try them as overlay on my glasses as well as my iPad when I use it at night.

### x_danny_x — 2012-11-19T03:21:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-65250>

i just order the those tinted red glasses from uvex to block the green and blue light.

### nonchalant — 2012-12-11T23:42:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-light-reduces-melatonin-too.806/post-67177>

I just got in a pair of red Dewalt glasses for looking at the computer.Â  I like them.Â  I did have difficulty looking at some photographs, because their color is gone and they are darker, so I just switched to my orange ones for the photos.Â  The red lenses are great for printed words.Â 

I see a small amount of light leakage at the bottom with them, but it isn't bad at all.

# Thread: Anticuchos--peruvian beef heart kebabs ('meat on a stick' for kids)--has nightshades
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anticuchos-peruvian-beef-heart-kebabs-meat-on-a-stick-for-kids-has-nightshades.808/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### thyme — 2012-04-11T19:20:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anticuchos-peruvian-beef-heart-kebabs-meat-on-a-stick-for-kids-has-nightshades.808/post-13804>

Take one whole ox heart, which should be about the cheapest cut your butcher has available. Trim off the fat and connective tissue--this will take you some time your first time through, but you will get faster with your second round. Cut into kebab size--about one to two inch squares. (If your butcher will do this for you, by all means, take it.)

In your blender, pulverize 1 tbsp cumin seeds. Add 3 to 8 chilies of your taste and to your taste--more if you like spicy, fewer if you don't--and puree again. Add 5-6 cloves of garlic, 2 tsp salt, 1 tbsp pepper, the juice of one or two limes, a splash of olive oil if desired and if eating, and a half cup of water. Blend together to create your marinade.

Marinate beef heart squares in refrigerator overnight. Soak bamboo sticks in water for at least thirty minutes, and turn your broiler onto its highest setting (or heat your grill, should you have one.) Thread the beef heart squares on the soaked bamboo sticks and put them under the broiler until cooked--usually 3-5 minutes in mine. Turn the sticks over and cook other side in same fashion.

It's not peruvian, but I boil down the used marinade to kill off any bad guys and then thicken it with a big lump of butter for a sauce. Can also serve with homemade mayo, or anything else you'd like.

# Thread: Detox or hyperthyroid?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-or-hyperthyroid.814/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### agatha — 2012-04-11T23:42:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-or-hyperthyroid.814/post-13921>

Hello all

I've been taking levothyroxine for a couple of years and 8 weeks ago my doctor raised my dose to 150 mcg from 125. My TSH was .9 and he said we could safely take it down to .4.

Anyway, last week I started getting heart palpitations at night and increased heartbeat ( up to 75 from my usual rate of 65). Also, my reflexes have been feeling over sensitive in the past couple of weeks. I thought I must be going hyperthyroid (since my TSH was already quite low) and stopped taking my meds over the weekend and the palpitations have settled down. I haven't had a chance to see the doc yet. Just wanted to check whether this sounds like true hyperthyroidism or just a detox reaction - I notice others seem to have had the palpitations temporarily.

I'd be grateful for any insights as my doc won't know anything about CT. Anyone?

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T00:07:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-or-hyperthyroid.814/post-13937>

The half-life of levothyroxine in the human body is approx 6 weeks. Upping your dose probably did not cause the issues you mentioned and stopping it did not solve them.

Do you have Hashi's or AI? Just curious why you are on levothyroxine.

I think too much levothyroxine doesn't usually cause hyperthroid symptoms unless you also take T3 with it since your body must convert the synthetic T4 to T3. The dosage increase wasn't that much.

### agatha — 2012-04-12T00:11:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-or-hyperthyroid.814/post-13939>

I have had high TSH and symptoms of hypothyroid in the past but I don't have the Hashis antibodies. I do however have MS so I don't rule out an AI problem with the thyroid.

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T00:51:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-or-hyperthyroid.814/post-13957>

My docs had me on levothyroxine (Synthroid) since 2005. I never had hypothyroid symptoms, just high TSH. They said I was hypothyroid whether I had symptoms of not. I quit taking it last summer for about 6 months and felt fine. My TSH crept up (around 4.0) and I started taking Synthroid again in January. I started CT'ing in Feb, stopped the Synthroid in March and will restest my TSH in a couple weeks. This time, however, I'm going to go based on how I feel, not my TSH value. Dr. K. says you can have high TSH/low T3/T4 as long as you are CT'ing and feeling good.

When I first got put on Synthroid, my TSH was 54. I had no symptoms, however over the next 3 years I put on 50 lbs, and had to start taking meds for high bp, high chol, high trigs, and had gout frequently. Primal Blueprint/paleo cleared up all the problems except high TSH...maybe CT can take care of that.

### agatha — 2012-04-12T19:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-or-hyperthyroid.814/post-14300>

Thanks for sharing your story Akman - like you I am more interested in how I feel than lab values. I have seen in other posts that you have come off your Synthroid and I have been waiting impatiently to see how it works out for you - good luck!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-12T21:04:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-or-hyperthyroid.814/post-14335>

I just made it 9 days w/ no thyroid medication. It's been quite good for me with just one other day where I had some headache that I questioned. Today I have a low throbbing headache that I could handle to try and go longer, but my hands are extremely cold and I think my basal temp is down around half a degree (I don't chart but check occasionally at the same time of day). Keep in mind I went in with 0 - T4 in my system. I've CT nearly daily for only around 20-30 minutes. Last couple of days I've raised from about 55 degrees to 70 and 65 degrees due shivering in tub making me think I should back off and work back down.

Probably best to stay on the thyroid for now. Maybe DEEP CT would take care of it completely but don't think I'm totally convinced about this aspect as a long term thyroid bypass.

ps - I don't ice myself in the bath.

### agatha — 2012-04-12T21:18:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-or-hyperthyroid.814/post-14346>

Yes, I don't intend to stop the meds entirely at present - have just backed off until the hyper symptoms have settled a bit more, then i'll go back in at a lower dose and see how I go.

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T21:26:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-or-hyperthyroid.814/post-14352>

> **agatha;14415 wrote:** Yes, I don't intend to stop the meds entirely at present - have just backed off until the hyper symptoms have settled a bit more, then i'll go back in at a lower dose and see how I go.

I think that is smart. Too many variables to jeopardize one's health.
