# Thread: Comparing CT and Sauna therapies
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-ct-and-sauna-therapies.815/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-11T23:48:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-ct-and-sauna-therapies.815/post-13927>

A Crossfit friend of mine posted a link to this blog on his FB and since he knew I was doing CT asked for my comment

[http://huntgatherlove.com/content/edges-human-tolerance-hot-and-cold](http://http://huntgatherlove.com/content/edges-human-tolerance-hot-and-cold)

So this is what I told him...

First, I would say that Dr. K is pushing the edge of the envelope. And he has put it out there how he did it. And he has put CT out there as an addition to the LRx that is not necessarily needed by everyone. Further there is a progression with it, and he clearly outlines that you shouldn't do it if you aren't in the right O6::O3 ratio. It is cold ADAPTATION, and pretty much like learning a language you can go submersion and suffer the consequences of your ignorance or you can go slow. The people who see "kills fat cells" and stop reading are the ones who should probably just go get Zeltique done. Because they jump into a cold bath for 45 mins, not realizing they have just obliterated the body's natural Fort Knox for storing xenoestrogens and all the other crap they put in their bodies for the last decade or more. And then your lymph system has to process it all which WILL have side effects - sinus drainage is a big one... so if you start CT in the height of allergy season or in the middle of a cold, well that is just plain stupid. The liver is also a huge component of detoxing the body, so the fool who keeps up his Guinness every night habit and does CT is going to - as aviators say - over stress the airframe.

With respect to her article, I would love to have her detail out all the science behind hot treatments and the pathology of their toxin clearing on the level of Dr. K's description of the science of CT. Without that she really just seems to be a whiner with a preference to Ray Cronise.

I've done CT baths only a few times using the "croak soak" method of getting in an empty tub, turning on the water at slow cold and letting it creep up over about 10 minutes, until I am chest deep. As I settle in I can turn the water on faster and colder. I don't bother with a skin thermometer and just toss in a couple of gladware containers of frozen water to chill it down a bit more. I stay about 30-40 mins, reading something. I do NOT like doing it before bed, because it does NOT make me sleepy. Then again, Natural Calm Magnesium keeps me awake too. For me, if it takes me until the next natural winter to be well cold adapted, so be it. I've got 45 years of SAD stored up in my body. And at my age there is plenty shifting on an endocrinologial level already.

My surgery to repair my tendons and ligaments in my foot basically included CT of my foot for 8 days. 30min on 30 min off for over a week. There were issues with that process that I would use as recommendations to the equipment manufacturer as upgrades, but I was able to be off narcotics w/in 72 hrs and in a weight bearing cast in 8 days!

My success using Dr. K's protocols - down 15 lbs with the LRx and limited CT. And unlike on Zone, I experienced NO post prandial hypoglycemia. Unlike Paleo alone, I am sleeping better and my middle age woman issues are moving back to normal. I have all the signs of being LS and am particularly fond of my now gorgeous skin on my elbows and feet. And that is a mighty good feeling for a woman who in January pretty much had only the word cancer to describe her skin.

So yes, when the peak of allergy season is passed, I will pop back into the tub. But I'm not in a hurry and nor should anyone else be.

### shilohman — 2012-04-12T02:26:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-ct-and-sauna-therapies.815/post-14000>

Sounds like your friend is cold adapted, but doesn't like it. You are right she is just whining. Saunas do detox you, but I really didn't understand what her point was.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-12T03:16:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-ct-and-sauna-therapies.815/post-14024>

> **shilohman;14069 wrote:** Sounds like your friend is cold adapted, but doesn't like it. You are right she is just whining. Saunas do detox you, but I really didn't understand what her point was.

My friend is NOT the author of the blog. He just asked me what I thought of the article since he new I did CT. And he's from one of those parts of the country where you can't help but get cold adapted!!

# Thread: BAB w/in 30 minutes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-12T03:23:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14033>

Are you all really able to eat your entire BAB within 30 minutes of waking? This is the only part of the protocol I seem to be struggling with. I eat a super clean diet and am really trying to stick to the leptin rx as closely as possible, but the timing is a bit tricky for me.

It's usually around an hour after waking until I am finished eating. For one thing, my BAB is so stinkin' huge it takes me a good 20-25 minutes just to eat it let alone the cooking time. I do prepare everything the night before and have it in a bowl so all I have to do is throw it in the pan to warm it up, but I take ox bile before my meals to help with fat digestion and that has to be taken 15 minutes before eating. I also try to do oil pulling every morning for 20 minutes and read that should be done prior to consuming breakfast.

I'm trying to figure out how I can fit in all of the pieces to this optimal puzzle!

### ealachan — 2012-04-12T03:29:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14036>

Yep, I have no problem with this part, but I've always been a hungry-first-thing-in-the-morning type, so I'm really used to getting up, making a quick pit-stop in the bathroom, then getting the kettle on and breakfast going.

### Croak — 2012-04-12T03:32:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14041>

I've always went by the assumption that you needed to _start_ on the BAB within 30 minutes, no need to wolf it down. Worked for me.

### Glamorama — 2012-04-12T04:23:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14057>

> **Croak;14110 wrote:** I've always went by the assumption that you needed to _start_ on the BAB within 30 minutes, no need to wolf it down. Worked for me.

Yes this is correct. I normally eat within 20 min of rising, and eating takes me about 25 min.

### cantweight — 2012-04-12T07:58:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14121>

I'll be honest, most days it more like an hour/hour+. I am getting 3 kids off to school and I havent been able to make it work yet...I know simple solution...get up early and feed myself before them. Easier said than done for me. I have a had a few of those well rested early rising days that I ate first, perhaps that will become the new norm for me. I am still having really great results. Week 5 LRX, week 3 CT

I take ox bile also...did not realize it should be taken 15 min prior to eating...thanks!

### Croak — 2012-04-12T08:47:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14135>

Thing is, once you get going on the LR, you'll find yourself getting up earlier, naturally. Of course, that also means bed by 11.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-12T10:50:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14148>

+1 on the starting BAB within 30 minutes of rising, but taking your time eating it. If I'm going to eat, I want to enjoy it (not get indigestion). Once you work out a routine, it's pretty easy.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-12T18:05:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-14244>

Thanks for all the feedback. When I initially started the reset I thought too I only had to start eating within 30 minutes. Somewhere down the line I thought for sure I read a comment by Dr K on the blog saying we should be finished within that timeframe . . . I must have misunderstood.

It would be much easier to get it in faster too if I could get my rear end moving in the mornings. When I first started the Rx I was waking much more refreshed and ready to start my day. The last 6 weeks or so (since starting CT actually) I just feel so dang tired and have a tough time crawling out of bed to get going. Hopefully this passes soon!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-16T17:26:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-15577>

I find it very difficult indeed to eat within 30 minutes of rising - I can manage within 40, but 30 continues to elude me unless I shower and dress after breakfast, which is not always easy to achieve.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-16T17:51:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-15589>

One thing that I do is have all my meat/veggies/CO measured, cut, prepped the night before, so once I'm in the kitchen, all I have to do is throw the meat on the grill, in the pan or in the oven and then go about my day. This saves a lot of time, vs. not having a plan and having to defrost something quick when you have nothing ready in the am.

### shilohman — 2012-04-16T17:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bab-w-in-30-minutes.820/post-15593>

This saves a lot of time, vs. not having a plan and having to defrost something quick when you have nothing ready in the am.

Yep that is what I do too. Saves a lot of time.

Was just wondering if there was any place online I could go where I could plug in how many grams of meat I had for breakfast and the exact correct protein grams would come up.

Just really wanting to make sure I hit at least 50 grams with breakfast.

Thanks guys!!!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-12T08:43:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-grams-in-meat.821/post-14133>

### janagram — 2012-04-15T00:03:03+0300

### janagram — 2012-04-15T00:07:09+0300

steak and most meat is about 7 grams an oz. Eggs are about 7 grams per egg.

# Thread: Wiggle room, or All-or-Nothing?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ginastarke — 2012-04-12T15:18:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/post-14194>

Hi all,

I'm trying to figure out how strict I need to be for the long haul, since I'm past the first 6 weeks. I have 70-something pounds to lose, and haven't had a cholesterol test in the last few months, so no nightshades for now. I try to get to bed as soon as it's completely dark, but you know how it is sometimes- life doesn't allow that.

Since I'm going to be under 25 grams carbs for the rest of the year, I was wondering if it's possible to work in some fruit as long as I stay under 25 grams, or if it has to be "permanent winter" for me until I see my goal weight.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-12T23:40:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/post-14411>

I think there are two issues to consider. One is seasonality - you would never eat any fruit that wasn't in season. Two is your boy's ability to be a fat burner (vs a sugar burner). If you are a sugar burner and/or LR, fruit will simply fuel that process, mess with your blood sugar, weight loss, etc etc. Personally, if I'm craving fruit it typically means that I'm craving sugar and cravings are a symtom that something is still amiss. Just my experience.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-13T02:18:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/post-14493>

I would consider opting for lower glycemic fruits. I.e. avoid:

banana

apple

pear

You can look up the glycemic load online easily. It seems that most berries have a really low glycemic load. On the other hand we don't need fruit in the short term and I haven't had more than a handful of grapes in the last 3 months. I plan on loading up over summer though! As far as I understand it it's the fructose in the fruit that gets the insulin resistance going as it has to be processed by the liver. I know there are plenty of beneficial food stuffs in fruit, I'm just laying off of them while doing CT etc...

jesse

### ginastarke — 2012-04-13T02:28:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wiggle-room-or-all-or-nothing.827/post-14498>

Summer's what I'm thinking about, too. I could live without most fruit, but going all summer without any Oregon strawberries or wild blackberries is a little unbearable. In my area in August, you can smell ripe blackberries on the wind, and it's more tempting than coffee, bakery, heck even a barbecue smell, (I bike home hungry for dinner, LOL ) I've been low carb so long I'm definitely a fat burner. I get a kind of sugar high off prime rib - only it lasts a lot longer without a crash!

# Thread: Thermometers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Santiago — 2012-04-12T18:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-14254>

Hi everyone,

I was looking for a good thermometer to read the skin temperature, and the infrared ones looked perfected, however I've realized that the accuracy is +- 2ÂºC, which is not very precise if one wants to make sure the skin is at 10/12ÂºC.

Mercury thermometers don't seem ideal for this application, cause probably the skin get warmed during the measure.

Do you any recommendation for precise thermometers?.

Best.

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T21:20:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-14347>

Even the best IR thermometers are about 1.5 deg C accurate. I used my infrared thermometer to measure a couple of mercury thermometers and determine how far off it was at different temps.

For instance, I have a mercury thermometer that is accurate to +/- .1 deg C. I hung that in my garage for a couple hours. It read 62 degrees F. I took my IR thermometer and aimed it at the base of the mercury thermometer and it read 61.5 deg F.

I did this at a couple different temperatures, and was able to determine that my IR thermometer was about 1/2 a degree low over the range of 30 - 75 deg F. That sort of put my mind at rest that it was fairly accurate to measure skin temp in the 55-75 deg range.

Hope that helps.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-14T03:52:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-14982>

That helps me. I use an IR therm and I don't have another therm to compare it against, so as long as it's reading in the 50-52f range then I'm assuming my skin is

### jsrhoades — 2012-04-15T03:41:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-15194>

A couple of very accurate reference points: Well-stirred water with ice will be at 32 deg F. Boiling water will be at 212 deg F. at sea level. Reduce by one deg F. per each 560 feet above sea level.

I bought a digital meat thermometer (Oxo brand) from Target for a $5-6 bucks, and it seems quite accurate. It read 31.6 in ice water and 212.0 in boiling water (altitude 250 feet). I think it is accurate for skin temp by laying the metal tip sidewise against the skin, but I haven't calibrated that.

### Santiago — 2012-04-15T21:08:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-15361>

I think I'll invest on a expensive (about $100) precise thermometer. For peace of mind, I think it will be the cheapest decision on the long run.

I'll buy something like this ( +- 1ÂºC) when I do a trip to US (customs and currency exchanges make it crazy buy it from Europe)

[http://www.amazon.com/Fluke-62-Mini-Infrared-Thermometer/dp/B000MX5Y9C/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1334513222&sr=8-1](http://http://www.amazon.com/Fluke-62-Mini-Infrared-Thermometer/dp/B000MX5Y9C/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1334513222&sr=8-1)

### hellojtm — 2012-04-15T22:27:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thermometers.830/post-15376>

I dropped my IR thermometer in the tub. Thankfully I dehydrated it in the oven over nite (just pilot light) and it's functional again! But I don't trust it, I'll just use jsrhoades good advice to double check it when it becomes convenient.

Santiago, hola amigo, Usted vive en EspaÃ±a?

jesse (from Art's forum)

# Thread: Cool vs. Cold, Ray Cronise' Perspective
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-04-12T19:25:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14288>

This was on [www.freetheanimal.com](http://http://www.freetheanimal.com), and got lost in the convoluted threading of comments, so I will post it all. Very interesting.

"Cool vs Cold?

Here is where I think the big gap exists. I think chronic cool trumps acute cold in terms of metabolic function. It sounds like the cliche turtle and hare argument, but metabolically it is much different. Let me share some data from last week (while we were all waiting for you comments to load and crashing iPhones).

For 7 days last week we had unseasonably warm temps in north Alabama â€“ highs in mid 80sF and lows in upper 60s. I measured my waking RMR every morning, before eating, just out of bed. It averaged 1582 kcal. Now, over the weekend the temperature dropped down to the 70s day/40-50s night. I let my house ride with outside temperature up to max of 70F(21C). That mean when I woke, the house temp was between 55-60F.

The last three days, my waking RMR was 1900, 1930, and 1920 kcal. Nearly a 4 mile run every day for no additional effort. As well, I know that my sleep time is much better during these cold evenings.

Some more interesting data. I did some cold soak baths (50F/10C) (10 minutes) using CO2/O2 monitoring. What I found was pretty amazing. As you would expect, I saw a boost in CO2 production on entering the bath. Anyone that has done this knows there is a slight pain period if the water is cold followed by a relaxation about two minutes after. During that time, when the pain vanished, my CO2 production dropped as well. It did not go all the way back to baseline.

But it gets better. I allowed it to come to rest (now 5 minutes in) and since I had my hands out of the water (someone here mentioned their feet â€“ so this is for you), once again CO2 went back up. What it tells me is the body VERY quickly (less than a minute) assesses and adjusts the blood flow accordingly. But it goes further. After a minute more I am back to a new settled point and when I stand up to get out â€“ it goes up AGAIN â€“ this time responding to air/evaporative loss driven by my warm body, room humidity and thin water film (no discernible movement in the room). Finally 10 minutes after I started it was still up. If youâ€™ve ever dehydrated running â€“ youâ€™ll know about that cold flash that happens as that last film of perspiration flashes off.

Now I post this to let you know that this is all extremely complex â€“ the body is absolutely AMAZING engineering. keeping a 180 lb hunk of titanium +/-.5 degrees is hard enough (ok yes, engineers, -thermal conductivity â€“ its an example), but our body does it every day.

On ice immersions Wimâ€™s core temperature RISES by a degree. skin settles in just above freezing.

If I was going to give EVERYONE a recommendation it would actually be quite simple: swim. I know itâ€™s not â€œgrokâ€￾ or â€œdripping testosterone,â€￾ just do it. If the water is about 80F/27C or lower you are in GREAT territory. Swim. My former company manufactured pools and yet I didnâ€™t learn to swim until AFTER I lost my weight. I was an avid scuba diver and wasnâ€™t afraid in any way of the water, but didnâ€™t know how to swim â€œlaps.â€￾

Make sure you control caloric intake in that window (2-4 hours) after and if you can do this in the morning while fasting, all the better.

80 doesnâ€™t sound like much, but there is nearly 20F delta in temperature and that drives heat loss (energy not, not temperature). Because you are physically active, blood stays flowing to the skin to dump and radiate. What several studies have noted is that hunger rises 2-4 hours after you swim and that is homeostasis kicking in trying to return us to â€œnormal.â€￾ I debunk the swimming makes you fat here: [http://bit.ly/Ictcwo](http://http://bit.ly/Ictcwo)

Added to this take a look at this post: [http://bit.ly/HC9lFR](http://http://bit.ly/HC9lFR) from Scott Parazynski. We have a lot of data concerning his weight loss on Everest climbs. Itâ€™s grueling, but it provides a lot of insight to how the body processes cold. (incidentally Wim made it to about 24K feet in shorts, no oxygen and no shirt. He had to turn back because of the foot injury filming the -40F run you may have seen on discovery. 3 months and it hadnâ€™t healed. Wim is just a bad-ass I donâ€™t care what you say).

There are literally hundreds of papers Iâ€™ve poured over since 2008. There is so much data itâ€™s overwhelming. I am very happy to contribute to pushing it all in this direction, but if you want to REALLY help, then I will say, Ice is a great â€œmental game.â€￾ It letâ€™s you know you can â€œdo it.â€￾ It can be great for adrenaline junkies. It helps sports injuries. Itâ€™s extreme.

â€¦but you probably can do much more metabolic good with periodic chronic cool. Swimming, cool walks, a few less layers, etcâ€¦ This means you should be able to thermal load all the way through the summer.

Scott, Wim, and I are continuing to work through all of this. There are dozens of other scientists we have consulted with, from Harvard to Holland, and everyone is very positive and supportive. We have a few things (including the breathing mentioned above) that will get everyone going and are trying to distill it down to some basic steps.

Please note, this is NOT an endurance sport. Thereâ€™s nothing to prove. Itâ€™s more like chasing parked cars â€“ eventually youâ€™re going to catch one and it isnâ€™t pretty. I hope everyone gets that lesson loud and clear from me. If you want to go extreme, then please have medical supervision â€“ or at least a 911 speed dial (youâ€™re doing what?). Remember the BIGGEST risk is not cooling down or surviving cold â€“ itâ€™s warming up. That is where you will get in trouble and people can and will die. I canâ€™t over emphasize this. Tim and I were careful to stay within reasonable exposure worlds. Donâ€™t be foolish.

I am headed for TEDMED2012, which moved to the Kennedy Center this year. I apologize that this is my only comment for now (perhaps you are relieved

Thanks everyone and letâ€™s keep this out of ad hominem attacks. It doesnâ€™t move us forward. Thereâ€™s plenty to learn and plenty credit to go around. Like Newton said, â€￾ If I have seen further it is by standing on ye sholders of Giants.â€￾

Ray

Letâ€™s bring this together."

### hellojtm — 2012-04-12T19:36:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14294>

Hi AKMan,

I like Ray but after all his recent dietary posts I've kind of lost interest. Sorry for this off the cuff editorial but I think he blackboxes the metabolism too much. So much of what he writes about cold is just how much energy you burn. An example of why I don't buy into this is intermittent fasting. IF has metabolic benefits that aren't because of limited total weekly caloric intake. There is a big difference in metabolic adaptation that can occur from deep stimulus. Here is a better example, he's basically arguing that jogging is superior to sprinting with his 80F is superior to 50-55F argument. But the stimulus is totally different. If you want to gain muscle then you'd sprint. If you want to gain muscle you lift to failure (or close to it) and then have a long recovery time. I wantonly believe that it's the same for cold exposure, you want the "deep inroad" (to steal a term from Body By Science) and then a long recovery.

best,

jesse

### chocolate — 2012-04-12T19:48:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14298>

I'm kinda with Jesse. I'm just swimming in a health club pool right now, but the unheated pool and cold showers were doing me much better. Plus when you read the neonate gut flora, it is for the 98 degree range and the 50 degree connoisseur range. Plus I get lasting results without getting sore in the cold. I just wake up harder and it makes me want it more cold. Plus the chlorine isn't so crappy when its cold.

### janagram — 2012-04-12T19:59:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14303>

this is all science (awesome)...but a bit over my head, however, I too have wondered, why don't these folks just get a swimming pool and not heat it? and do something natural, like swimming?

Also, from my reading, the pre-communist Tibetans were very cold adapted. A guy who was in prison there said the cold was so painful at night, but added, everyone in Tibet was cold at night....there was no heating!!! They let toddlers go naked, covered in yak butter, etc...

I live in California, but was a kid in No. Dakota...and have wondered just why people settled there when they could've migrated south...but maybe they knew something we didn't! (Of course I loved the snow when I was a kid!!)

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T20:02:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14305>

Ray's motives are clearly fat-loss and not a cure-all for disease and longevity, but many around here are also just after fat-loss. As far as I know, Dr. K and Ray C are working together on some projects, so I definitely don't view him as the enemy!

One comment that keeps popping up is, 'what if I can't take 50 degrees?' I just wanted to post this to show that cool, not cold is accepted for weightloss/metabolism boost.

### ealachan — 2012-04-12T21:06:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14338>

> **Croak;14405 wrote:** Me, I'm a fat fucker who realizes that fat loss is a _symptom_ of getting healthy. Not the other way around. I'm in it for the long game, not swimsuit season.

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T21:09:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14340>

> **PaleoMom;14398 wrote:** I'm wondering how one measures RMR in the way Cronise mentions also....

You'd need O2 and CO2 analyzers, ventilation measurement by a pnuemotach or turbine ventilometer (as used for VO2 measurement). There are also specific devices to measure metabolic rate such as the BodyGem.

Probably not for the average person to do...

### agatha — 2012-04-12T21:11:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14341>

It would be interesting to look at the indigenous traditions about this. Looking at the Russian stuff on YouTube they seem to do brief exposures in severely cold water. I like all of it- cool, cold, whatever..

### hellojtm — 2012-04-12T21:14:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14343>

Thanks for the clarification AKMan. It's a good post and I missed it in the free the animal thread because... it's out of control  I _think_ the advice to get chronic low levels of exposure to cold make sense, but the deeper inroads are going to have a bigger impact on gene expression and ultimately the results. I normally fall back on the idea that if we don't want the calories, i.e. we are doing cold exposure to waste calories, then we shouldn't eat them. But I understand that some folks have already eaten them and now want to waste them off as fast and as comfortably as possible. The mild exposure should be a good tool in that regard.

best,

jesse

I've been wanting to take Ray to task on the dietary blog posts for a while but haven't mustered the energy... I definitely do not consider him the enemy. I like his writing and what he has done and his intellect, but I just want him to get the evolutionary biology angle. I think that relying on modern science is somewhat fragile, i.e. pointing out that vegetables have proteins doesn't mean they are a good source of protein for a human. If all protein in all substances were good sources then we wouldn't have celiacs and undigested proteins making it into our intestines. He also flirts with what I consider dangerously low cholesterol levels because he's following conventional wisdom on that topic. I respect him though, he is specialized and hasn't had time to dig into these other topics as extensively as the cold exposure stuff. If he did then I would hope he could school me on it

### AKMan — 2012-04-12T21:29:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-vs-cold-ray-cronise-perspective.832/post-14355>

>
>
>
> I've been wanting to take Ray to task on the dietary blog posts for a while but haven't mustered the energy... I definitely do not consider him the enemy. I like his writing and what he has done and his intellect, but I just want him to get the evolutionary biology angle. I think that relying on modern science is somewhat fragile, i.e. pointing out that vegetables have proteins doesn't mean they are a good source of protein for a human. If all protein in all substances were good sources then we wouldn't have celiacs and undigested proteins making it into our intestines. He also flirts with what I consider dangerously low cholesterol levels because he's following conventional wisdom on that topic. I respect him though, he is specialized and hasn't had time to dig into these other topics as extensively as the cold exposure stuff. If he did then I would hope he could school me on it

I hear you. Macronutrients aside, he does seem to favor a calorie restricted diet, same as Dr. K.

# Thread: Water Kefir
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pardus — 2012-04-12T21:40:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-14360>

Hi,

Has anyone tried water kefir as a probiotic for leaky gut?

Milk kefir is definitely out of the question for us auto-immune folks, but might water kefir be okay?

I know there is some residual fructose left in the water kefir, so I'm wondering if this would cause a reaction, especially since many of us have very sensitive guts.

What are your thoughts? Any comments on your personal experience?

### chocolate — 2012-04-13T17:08:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-14717>

I know the gaps diet says to use kraut juice in the broth. That was a huge turning point for my feeling better. I just had trouble reintroducing foods. The broth and kraut are what a brought with me to VLC Paleo. I just didn't feel very good. I can't say I had a leaky gut. My joints quit hurting from the grain and the cold water pushed the weight off.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-13T17:41:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-14732>

Dr K recommends Kevita. Have you tried it? If you get the plain one it's 2g of sugar a serving

[http://kevita.com/](http://http://kevita.com/)

It's pretty good but I am super cautious when I drink it. I usually only have a 1/4 cup a day.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-14T00:41:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-14897>

I've just ordered kefir water grains and will be making some next week. I'll let you know. I know Dr K drinks the Kevita brand and Mark Sisson did an article on it that seemed to imply that the sugars are used by in the process. I'm pretty sensitive to sugar/cravings, so I'm hopeful.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-17T20:39:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-15942>

FWIW, I have been using both home brewed kombucha and water kefir. The key to not having a lot of residual sugar is the length of fermentation. Most directions are to only ferment the water kefir for 2 days. Taste it - if it is still sweet let it ferment longer. I let my water kefir grains ferment for 7-10 days. It should be tangy like a light vinegar (low sugar, lots of acid from fermentation). If you drink 8 oz and you get sleepy then it probably has too much sugar for you.

For the kombucha I let that go for 14-21 days. I have found the best results when it tastes like strong apple cider vinegar. Not my favorite flavor profile, but that is what works for me.

These are the fermentation times it has taken in the winter (ambient house temps ~ 58 degrees). Not sure how long it will take in the summer. Also, since my house is cold in the winter I use a electric seed starter mat wrapped around the brew jar to keep the kombucha at it's preferred temp - water kefir grains don't seem to need the extra special treatment.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-22T05:09:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-17652>

I've just finished making my 3rd batch of water kefir. It's super easy, tasty and quick to make. The advice of the woman who has been making it for years told me that the longer you ferment it the more alcohol it has and the less sugar, but also the less priobiotic it is (still great though). I've been doing mine for 48 hours with the grains in the water (it's autumn here, just out on the kitchen counter) and then another 24 with just the liquid poured off (at which point I add ginger). Then into the fridge. It's slightly fizzy and very easy to drink. Even my kids like it.

### Pardus — 2012-04-22T05:27:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-17654>

KiwiLauren,

Thanks for sharing! Please keep us informed on your progress.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-27T02:31:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-19195>

Just an update... I totally recommend making your own water kefir. I now have 10x the amount of grains I did 10 days ago (talk about a bargain) and they just keep reproducing. It is autumn here (no heat in house) and they produce a batch in 24 hours which I can do a second ferment on (without the grains in it) for another 24. One of my children loves it plain, the other adds a tablespoon of fresh squeezed lemon juice to it. My husband and I drink it plain, but we also sometimes flavour it by putting sliced ginger in it (it's reproducing so fast that I don't always have ginger on hand). It seems to produce slightly fizzier drink by putting it in a bottle with a narrow neck (vs a jar that is the same width top to bottom). All in all, super easy. And I've noticed that I don't need to take digestive enzymes anymore either.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-01T04:32:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-20277>

Just got some water kefir grains today. I put then in a jar with water, organic raw sugar, raisIns and some kevita. The instructions she sent with the grains caller for kefir starter or lemon and I have neither, so I used the kevita. Hope it works. It's still pretty cold here with my housE thermostat reading 53. So I hope this works! I also got a kombucha scoby and started that. I'm going to dry some apples to use in future batches. But that's a project for another day! Gotta try my hand a coconut yogurt soon and make another batch of sauerkraut. My gut is gonna be tight! Lol

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-16T21:28:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-38449>

I was thinking of buying water kefir grains it sounds very good...I have a few questions though. Anyone have a link to a good container type to buy for this? I could either buy it at a store or Amazon...depends on what kind of container I guess and the cost. Also I wanted to use honey and/or maple syrup. Any recommendations on amounts of each? I know you need a certain amount of whatever sugar you are using to get the right balance...anyone have experience with using honey or maple syrup?

I'm talking about Raw unfiltered honey and grade b maple syrup by the way. Also I don't suppose anyone has a detailed site on making water kefir and how much sugar/probiotics a given amount of time will produce?

One more thing....actually probably not the last but for now my last question is where do I buy them? [http://waterkefirgrains.com/](http://http://waterkefirgrains.com/)

Is this site any good has anyone used it before? I live in Lewisville, TX Is there anyone nearby that I could get some from maybe? I'd be willing to pay for them if you want.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-16T21:43:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-38456>

Honey is anti bacterial and not recommended for any type of probiotic fermentation

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-16T23:16:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-38501>

I forgot to mention I already read that somewhere...they didn't make it sound like it wouldn't work though. They said that it would weaken or could kill the kefir grains....but I figured I could cycle between production runs to grow more grains and runs with honey too.

Will using honey not produce probiotics at all or anything beneficial? I understand it may not be sustainable by itself as it will consume the grains since honey is anti-bacterial, but figured it might still work to produce something good.

Would love to hear more on this before buying Kefir grains and wasting money if its just going to destroy them without actually working. I have quite a bit of honey and like honey so I figured it would be a nice way to use some of it. If anyone has successfully used honey to make water kefir and thought it was a good thing I would love to hear about it.

Is the Grade B Maple syrup something I can use? What do you recommend?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-16T23:39:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-38507>

Coconut water is good. If you use things that inhibit fermentation u have a lab experiment. U might grow things u weren't planning on growing.....

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-17T00:31:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-38526>

If it won't work I guess that's fine...just wanted to use it because its something I already have. I might go ahead and see what comes out of it if I try. I'll let you know what happens if I do thanks for the input.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-08-10T03:23:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-44033>

Tomorrow I will look for kefir crystals. I want to make home made kefir too! Will get kombucha too. I always save and drink the left over liquid from sourkruat. Happy guts R Us!

### Raiken3712 — 2012-08-10T04:50:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-44050>

Mine don't seem to be multiplying...but I think they are still working as bubbles are forming at the top...so something is going on. It did say it can take several batches my next will be either my 4th or 5th..

### somewhatchubby — 2012-10-12T00:08:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-57666>

Hi, can anyone tell me how long you can keep the water kefir after a second ferment. I put some in a kilner jar and it has foam .bubbles all over the surface.is this the norm ?Its been about three days.

Maybe I should sling it ?

### Holly — 2012-11-08T03:59:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-62799>

> **somewhatchubby;58987 wrote:** Hi, can anyone tell me how long you can keep the water kefir after a second ferment. I put some in a kilner jar and it has foam .bubbles all over the surface.is this the norm ?Its been about three days.
>
> Maybe I should sling it ?

I do my first ferment for 1-2 days. Pour into airtight bottles for usually less than 12 hours as the pressure of the carbonation starts to seep from the bottle. I think I get extra carbonation because I add just a bit of pineapple juice in addition to any other flavoring. I hear that the enzymes in the pineapple increase the carbonation. Sure does the trick for me for both my water kefir and kombucha. But we really like the fizz at our house. yumm

### Thor — 2012-11-13T04:35:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-kefir.838/post-63950>

i'll have to look into it - kefir

# Thread: What is 500 cal like on HCG?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-04-12T22:50:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/post-14383>

I know almost nothing about HCG except you eat 500 cal a day for a while without feeling hungry or losing muscle.

For you that have done it, have you ever tried 500 cal/day without the HCG? What was the difference?

The reason I'm asking, when I was almost 100lbs heavier, I was doing Primal Blueprint and lost most of the weight over a year. During that time, I toyed with fasting and calorie restriction. If I ate less than 1000 calories a day, I would actually gain weight--probably water weight from stress?--and I'd feel like total crap and couldn't exercsie at all after a day or two. I could IF daily, skipping breakfast, but couldn't go 36-48 hours without feeling really bad.

Anyway, now I'm down to 168lbs from 250lbs (I'm a 5'10/46yo/m) I have been CT'ing since Feb. I find now I can restrict calories to well under 1000 for several days in a row and throw in a 48 hour fast and feel great. It doesn't effect workout or mood. I only have like 10lbs to lose, but calorie restricting on CT is getting me there fast.

I'm wondering if I'm experiencing HCG-like results from CT+calorie restriction.

Thanks

### indigogirl — 2012-04-12T23:14:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/post-14393>

Hi AKMan,

I am not starving on 500 calories a day while using pharmaceutical grade HCG. I did subcutaneous shots the first time I did it and am now doing sublingual drops. The only time I was hungry doing HCG was when I tried homeopathic drops. They just didn't work for me and I was starving and stopped after 2 weeks. I did lose 10 lbs in those 2 weeks, but couldn't face staying on it that hungry.

Hopefully Colleen will chime in here. She is a moderator on an HCG Forum.

Typically I lose about 7-10 lbs the first week and then average about .5 lbs a day. When one is closer to their goal weight I think the losses are less.

Don't know if I answered you question???

### Croak — 2012-04-12T23:48:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/post-14415>

Lots of people do it with homeopathic "HCG", which IMNSHO really is doing it without HCG.

Personally, my CT results are around the low end of my prescription HCG results (I did one 40 day round, dropped 30 pounds, then started the Leptin Reset, where I've lost another 30 and counting). Early days of HCG were about a pound a day, stalls in the middle, then about .5 to .2 a day until the end, and I was dead strict on the Simeons diet/portions.

Long story short, if I had started the LR/CT in November instead of a round of HCG, I suspect I'd be within a few pounds of the same weight I am at now, with a _lot less hassle, and be that much healthier to boot.

What hassle, you ask? Injections, the exactitude of the portions and the narrowness of what was allowed, the HCG expense, the gallons of water and associated trips to the head, and the ever-present worry that "this just ain't natural for a male". That, and I was indeed hungry, a _lot_. Not crippling hunger, but a near-constant gnawing, no matter how I adjusted the dosage (sorry Colleen, but IMHO it just doesn't work as well at reducing hunger in a male, even though we tend to lose more weight, faster).

Willpower got me through it more than hormones. I won't do it again, and I won't recommend it to anyone now that I know about the LRx and CT. That said, it did lead me to Kruse via the HCG community, so I'm thankful for that, as well as the 30 pounds that showed me it could be done (I'm 47, never deliberately_ dieted a day in my life prior to HCG).

### ealachan — 2012-04-12T23:59:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/post-14421>

I'm curious, because I only vaugely know how the HCG thing works...how do people not just automatically pile all the weight back on after a round of HCG? If you're spending 40 days eating 500 calories a day then go back to normal eating, how does your body not think "OMFG, MUST STORE ALL THE CALORIES FOR THE NEXT FAMINE!!!" Clearly it works for some people, but do you have to keep doing never-ending rounds of HCG to keep the weight off?

### Croak — 2012-04-13T00:06:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/post-14426>

It's supposed to "reset" your h'thalmus to your new set weight. The post-injection phase is fairly regimented as well (Phase 3 is very low carb), and indeed, a lot of people see the wheels start falling off in this period, for various reasons. And after Phase 3, many just go right back to a SAD.

That said, a lot of people "stabilize" within a few pounds of last injection weight and are able to stay there. It works, and like I mentioned above, that first round has a motivating power that is far beyond its actual clinical value...that is not to be scoffed at.

But I honestly believe that the LR/Paleo/CT way is superior in every respect except those early ultra-rapid losses which may or may not be sustainable.

### rocketmom — 2012-04-13T01:22:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/post-14471>

It works. It is amazing how quickly you will lose weight. The losses keep you going. It is the hardest thing I have ever done. This diet is not for the "faint of heart" and it seriously impacts you and those around you with the strictness of the plan. Going to social events and out to dinner is near impossible unless you eat prior to going and just sip on water(which is what I do, my husband loves to dine out). It gets boring and the limitations of allowed foods is a killer if you don't like some of the foods. When I finish this round, I am **done**. I will do the paleo/LRx which helped me stabilize and even lose in P3/4 the maintenance rounds. I am also considering the JUDDD plan that seems to be working for many members of another group I am on.

dennise

### colleencoble — 2012-04-13T01:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/post-14485>

I've seen too many men do this with no hunger to believe it doesn't work as well for a male, Croak. That said, there are a few people--male AND female--who continue to have hunger or weakness on the protocol. When I hear about that, I tell them to stop immediately because without hcg they are on a starvation diet.

Here's where hcg differs from anything I've ever done, AK (and I've done EVERYTHING) and that's the fact that it takes the fat from where you're storing it. For me that meant the hips and thighs. For those who carry it in their belly, it comes from there. HOWEVER, if there is hunger, the fat comes from normal fat stores and does not reshape right. I've had a couple of rounds where I had to tweak the dose because of hunger and the difference in myself was very pronounced. I would start to lose in my face and neck and begin to look gaunt. It's amazing how different it is.

Also on those 500 calories there is no hunger when the dose is right. You feel very "up" and energetic. Very satisfied with your food. I absolutely love the protocol. I still recommend it to everyone. Women don't seem to drop weight fast with CT like you men do. I don't know what that means other than maybe our hormonal issues are preventing the losses. But hcg always works quickly. A woman can pretty much count on at least 20 pounds gone in 40 days, often more like 25-28. It's also the EASIEST diet I've ever done because it really works and the weight just falls off. I actually like the boredom because it helps break food addictions and it allows people to see underlying food intolerances that led to their chronic obesity in the first place.

Hope that answers your questions.

### MaggieC — 2012-04-26T09:29:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/post-18983>

Well done as usual Colleen!

### MartiD — 2012-04-26T15:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-500-cal-like-on-hcg.843/post-18994>

I did two rounds of HHCG starting last June with 6 weeks in between doing LRX. I dropped a few pounds first time after HHCG and I was down 12 pounds doing reset in Fall. I've continued the reset format this Spring and added CT in February. I'm down a few more pounds but obviously I look different over the last few months because I have friends who see me every 3 weeks tell me I look so much smaller! I try to not get to hung up with scale number. I've considered another short round to get another 15 lbs off but can't decide. I'm really comfortable with doing the reset. The only problem is I've been on reset since late October and really can't tell if I'm LS or not!

# Thread: a different lunch....and good!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-lunch-and-good.845/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-04-12T23:52:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-lunch-and-good.845/post-14418>

I was out of homemade mayo and I wanted tuna, so I thought about how good tuna casserole used to be....and I thought what it would take to make it optimal. Well, I took org. cream (a cup) and reduced it with a tsp. mustard, a shake of turmeric, salt, and grated romano cheese (sheep) and then added 1 can tuna. Served over sauteed greens. Oh my. Yum.

### ealachan — 2012-04-13T00:02:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-lunch-and-good.845/post-14425>

> **janagram;14487 wrote:** I was out of homemade mayo and I wanted tuna, so I thought about how good tuna casserole used to be....and I thought what it would take to make it optimal. Well, I took org. cream (a cup) and reduced it with a tsp. mustard, a shake of turmeric, salt, and grated romano cheese (sheep) and then added 1 can tuna. Served over sauteed greens. Oh my. Yum.

Sounds good!

I like to do tuna mixed with a little lemon juice and olive oil, some roasted red peppers, good oil-cured olives, and artichoke hearts, all piled on a bed of greens. Nom!
